Nature: cold [han 寒; the Shennong Bencaojing records 'ping / neutral,' but canonical post-Han commentators, including the Mingyi Bielu and all major Kampo sources, emend this to cold, which aligns with consistent clinical use]. Classical category: middle herb [zhong pin 中品].

Channel tropism [gui jing 歸經]: Lung [fei 肺], Gallbladder [dan 膽], Stomach [wei 胃], Large Intestine [da chang 大腸], and — in extended Kampo usage — the Heart [xin 心] and Small Intestine [xiao chang 小腸].

Japanese Kampo name: Ōgon [黄芩], read in on'yomi; the dried root of Scutellaria baicalensis is the standard Kampo drug, regulated under the Japanese Pharmacopoeia.

Primary classical indications (Shennong Bencaojing tradition, c. 200 CE): 'It governs [zhu 主] the various heat [conditions] [zhu re 諸熱]; jaundice [huang dan 黃疸]; intestinal dysentery with bloody flux [chang pi 腸澼]; diarrhea and discharge [xie li 洩利]; expelling water accumulation [zhu shui 逐水]; breaking up blood closure / amenorrhea [xia xue bi 下血閉]; malignant sores [e chuang 惡瘡]; deep-rooted abscesses and erosive ulcers [ju shi 疽蝕]; and fire-scalds / burns [huo yang 火瘍].' Alternate classical name: 'Rotten-gut herb' [fu chang 腐腸], referring to the hollow, decay-prone interior of aged roots. Habitat recorded as: mountain valleys and river gorges [sheng chuan gu 生川谷].

Five Flavors [wu wei 五味]: Bitter [ku 苦] — the sole flavor assigned in the classical text; bitterness is held to drain, dry, and descend, making Huang Qin suited to clearing damp-heat accumulations.

Four Natures [si qi 四氣]: Cold [han 寒]. Clears heat and drains fire [qing re xie huo 清熱瀉火]; resolves toxicity [jie du 解毒]; dries dampness [zao shi 燥濕]; cools the blood and stops bleeding [liang xue zhi xue 涼血止血]; and, in fetal medicine, calms restless fetus [an tai 安胎].

Therapeutic actions elaborated in Kampo tradition: (1) Clears heat from the Upper Burner [shang jiao 上焦], particularly the Lung — used for cough with yellow phlegm, chest oppression, and febrile respiratory conditions. (2) Dries dampness and clears damp-heat in the Stomach and Intestines — principal drug for dysentery, diarrhea, jaundice with a damp-heat etiology. (3) Cools blood heat to stop hemorrhage — epistaxis, hemoptysis, hematuria, metrorrhagia. (4) Clears heat toxin in sores and swellings — applied internally and occasionally topically. (5) Quiets the fetus in threatened miscarriage attributed to heat in the Chong and Ren vessels.

Key classical and Kampo formulas [fang ji 方劑]: 
• Xiao Chaihu Tang [小柴胡湯] (Minor Bupleurum Decoction) — harmonizes Shaoyang; Huang Qin clears Gallbladder heat alongside Chaihu.
• Huangqin Tang [黃芩湯] — Shanghan Lun formula for Taiyang-Shaoyang diarrhea with abdominal pain.
• Gegen Huangqin Huanglian Tang [葛根黃芩黃連湯] — clears intestinal heat; treats febrile diarrhea.
• Da Chaihu Tang [大柴胡湯] — stronger Shaoyang-Yangming formula; Kampo mainstay for biliary and gastrointestinal inflammation.
• Wen Jing Tang [溫經湯] variants and Baitouweng Tang [白頭翁湯] also incorporate Huang Qin in some traditions.
• In Kampo, Ōgon features prominently in Saikokeishito [柴胡桂枝湯] and Ōgontō [黃芩湯] for inflammatory and febrile pediatric conditions.

Preparation notes: The dried root [gen 根] is used. Raw [sheng 生] Huang Qin is preferred for clearing heat and draining fire; wine-fried [jiu chao 酒炒] to ascend to the Upper Burner and reduce cold injury to the Stomach; charred [tan 炭] to stop bleeding. Typical decoction dose in Kampo: 3–9 g dried root.

entities_mentioned.name: 黃芩 (Huang Qin / Ōgon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scutellaria baicalensis Georgi (Lamiaceae) — Baikal skullcap; dried root [radix]
entities_mentioned.notes: S. baicalensis is the standard pharmacopoeial source in both Chinese and Japanese traditions. S. amoena and S. viscidula are occasionally listed as regional substitutes in the Chinese Pharmacopoeia but are not accepted in Japanese Kampo regulation. The alternate classical name 'fu chang' (腐腸, rotten gut) describes the pithy, hollow interior of older roots and was used as a quality indicator — hollow roots (older plants) were considered superior in some traditions, inferior in others.

entities_mentioned.name: 小柴胡湯 (Xiao Chaihu Tang / Shōsaikotō)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula; Kampo OTC preparation regulated in Japan
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most important Kampo formulas; Huang Qin/Ōgon is one of seven constituent herbs. Widely studied for hepatoprotective and anti-inflammatory effects in modern research.

entities_mentioned.name: 黃芩湯 (Huangqin Tang / Ōgontō)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical formula from the Shanghan Lun (Treatise on Cold Damage, c. 200 CE)
entities_mentioned.notes: Huang Qin is the chief [jun 君] herb in this formula. Treats diarrhea with abdominal pain and fever in Shaoyang-Taiyang combined disease.

results.translator_notes: 1. NATURE DISCREPANCY: The raw Shennong Bencaojing source text records 'ping' (平, neutral/balanced) as Huang Qin's nature, which is historically anomalous given the herb's unambiguous heat-clearing clinical profile. Post-Han consensus — including the Mingyi Bielu (名醫別錄, c. 500 CE), Li Shizhen's Bencao Gangmu (1596), and all major Kampo authorities — corrects this to 'han' (寒, cold). The translation follows the corrected canonical reading and flags the original. This is a known and frequently discussed textual issue in Bencao scholarship. 2. SOURCE TEXT BREVITY: The supplied source segment is a prompt/label rather than a full classical quotation. The original_text field above reconstructs the standard Shennong Bencaojing entry for Huang Qin as attested in the Mori Tateyuki recension and major critical editions; this is standard practice for Plantacopia archival entries when the supplied text is a header rather than a full transcription. 3. KAMPO ELABORATION: Japanese Kampo extended the channel tropism and refined processing methods (wine-frying, charring) beyond what the Shennong Bencaojing specifies; these additions are clearly attributed to post-classical tradition in the translation.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Huang Qin 黃芩

results.title_inferred: Zhi Mu (Anemarrhena rhizome): Classical and Kampo Entry
results.original_text: 知母 — 味苦，寒。主消渴熱中，除邪氣，肢體浮腫，下水，補不足，益氣。（神農本草經）
results.translation:
Zhi Mu [知母, Anemarrhena asphodeloides Bunge, rhizoma]. Flavor: bitter [苦, kǔ]. Nature: cold [寒, hán]. Japanese Kampo name: Chimo (チモ / 知母). Classical category: Middle herb [中品, zhōng pǐn] in the Shennong Bencaojing hierarchy.

Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]: Bitter (苦). Some later commentators (e.g., Li Shizhen, Bencao Gangmu, 1596) add a subsidiary sweet quality.

Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]: Cold (寒). Classified as markedly cold in yin-nourishing applications.

Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]: Enters the Lung (肺), Stomach (胃), and Kidney (腎) channels. Post-Tang elaborations also associate it with the Triple Burner (三焦) in the context of draining ministerial fire.

Primary Therapeutic Actions (Shennong Bencaojing tradition, c. 200 CE): 'Governs wasting-thirst [消渴, xiāo kě] and internal heat [熱中, rè zhōng]; eliminates pathogenic qi [邪氣]; disperses swelling and edema of the limbs [肢體浮腫]; promotes downward movement of water; supplements insufficiency [補不足]; and augments qi [益氣].'

Kampo Elaborations: In the Japanese Kampo tradition, Chimo (知母) is principally employed to: (1) clear heat and drain fire [清熱瀉火] — particularly lung fire manifesting as high fever, irritability, and thirst; (2) nourish yin and moisten dryness [滋陰潤燥] — addressing lung and kidney yin deficiency with symptoms of dry cough, afternoon fever, night sweats, and steaming bone disorder [骨蒸, gǔ zhēng]; (3) generate fluids and relieve thirst — used in patterns of stomach heat consuming fluids, as in diabetes-adjacent wasting-thirst syndromes. The Kampo tradition particularly values its action on 'yin fire' (陰火) — deficiency heat arising from kidney yin depletion — distinguishing it from herbs that clear repletion heat.

Key Classical Formulas: (1) Zhi Bai Di Huang Wan [知柏地黃丸] — with Phellodendron (黃柏) and Rehmannia (地黃); treats kidney yin deficiency with ascending fire, night sweats, steaming bones. (2) Bai Hu Tang [白虎湯, White Tiger Decoction] — with Gypsum (石膏), Japonica rice (粳米), and Licorice (甘草); primary formula for Yang Ming stage heat with high fever, profuse sweating, intense thirst, and flooding pulse. (3) Bai Hu Jia Ren Shen Tang [白虎加人參湯] — same base with added Ginseng (人參); for qi and fluid depletion accompanying yang ming heat. (4) Er Mu San [二母散] — with Fritillaria (貝母); clears lung heat and moistens dryness for cough with scanty, difficult sputum. (5) Zhi Mu Fu Ling Wan [知母茯苓丸] — used in Kampo for lung-dryness cough patterns.

Preparation: The rhizome is harvested in spring or autumn, dried, and used raw [生知母, shēng zhī mǔ] for clearing heat and draining fire, or salt-processed [鹽知母, yán zhī mǔ — stir-fried with brine] to direct the herb's action downward to the kidney channel and enhance its yin-nourishing properties. Standard decoction dose: 6–12 g. Contraindicated in spleen-stomach deficiency cold with loose stools.

entities_mentioned.name: 知母 (Zhi Mu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anemarrhena asphodeloides Bunge (family Asparagaceae / formerly Liliaceae); dried rhizome (Rhizoma Anemarrhenae)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most securely identified classical Chinese materia medica. The steroidal saponins (timosaponins) and steroidal glycosides have been studied for antipyretic, anti-inflammatory, anti-diabetic, and neuroprotective properties consistent with classical indications.

entities_mentioned.name: 白虎湯 (Bai Hu Tang, White Tiger Decoction)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical formula from the Shanghan Lun (傷寒論, c. 220 CE) by Zhang Zhongjing
entities_mentioned.notes: Cornerstone formula for yang ming (陽明) stage repletion heat; Zhi Mu is a principal herb alongside Gypsum (石膏).

entities_mentioned.name: 知柏地黃丸 (Zhi Bai Di Huang Wan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical Kampo/TCM formula; modification of Liu Wei Di Huang Wan (六味地黃丸)
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely used in Kampo for kidney yin deficiency with deficiency fire; Zhi Mu paired with Phellodendron (黄柏, Huang Bai) forms the 'Zhi Bai' (知柏) combination.

entities_mentioned.name: 消渴 (Xiao Ke, Wasting-Thirst)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Broadly corresponds to diabetes mellitus and related metabolic disorders in modern medicine
entities_mentioned.notes: The classical category is broader than modern diabetes; includes any syndrome of excessive thirst, urination, and wasting. The Bencaojing listing of Zhi Mu for Xiao Ke is among the earliest textual associations.

entities_mentioned.name: 骨蒸 (Gu Zheng, Steaming Bone Disorder)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely includes tuberculosis and other chronic febrile wasting diseases
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical yin-deficiency heat pattern characterized by afternoon/evening fever, night sweats, and sensation of heat emanating from the bones; a key Kampo indication for salt-processed Zhi Mu.

results.translator_notes: The source text as supplied is a modern scholarly summary prompt rather than a verbatim classical passage; accordingly, the 'original_text' field reconstructs the core Shennong Bencaojing entry in classical Chinese (consistent with received editions such as the Mori Risshi 森立之 reconstruction, 1854), while the translation and entry expand this with documented Kampo and subsequent classical commentary. No OCR errors were present. The Bencaojing itself does not use the term 'yin fire' (陰火), which is a later conceptual development associated with Li Dongyuan (李東垣, Jin dynasty) and elaborated in Kampo; this distinction has been preserved in the entry structure. The channel tropism (gui jing) system postdates the Bencaojing proper and derives from Song–Jin–Yuan dynasty elaboration; this is standard practice in Plantacopia entries covering this text tradition.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Zhi Mu 知母

results.title_inferred: Sheng Di Huang (Raw Rehmannia Root): Classical Monograph and Kampo Elaboration
results.original_text: 生地黃 — 味甘，寒。主折跌，絕筋，傷中，逐血痺，填骨髓，長肌肉，作湯除寒熱積聚，除痺，生者尤良。久服輕身不老。
results.translation:
Sheng Di Huang [Raw Rehmannia Root; 生地黃]

Chinese Name (Pinyin): Shēng Dì Huáng | Characters: 生地黃
Japanese Kampo Name: Jio (生地黄, しょうじおう / じおう)
Classical Category: Superior Herb [上品, shàng pǐn] — among the highest rank, suitable for long-term use without toxicity

---

FIVE FLAVORS [五味, wǔ wèi]:
Sweet [甘, gān]; subsidiary bitterness noted in later commentaries.

FOUR NATURES [四氣, sì qì]:
Cold [寒, hán].

CHANNEL TROPISM [歸經, guī jīng]:
Heart [心], Liver [肝], and Kidney [腎] channels. Later Tang and Song elaborations (e.g., the Bencao Yanyi and Japanese Kampo lineages) further emphasize the Kidney and Liver channels as primary seats of Yin nourishment and blood cooling.

---

THERAPEUTIC ACTIONS (Shennong Bencaojing tradition, c. 200 CE):

1. Treats fractures, ruptures of sinews, and internal injury [主折跌、絕筋、傷中]: The raw form was held to promote the knitting of sinew and bone following trauma.
2. Expels blood impediment [逐血痺]: Drives out stagnant blood obstructing the channels and network vessels [絡脈], relieving numbness and pain.
3. Fills the bone marrow [填骨髓]: Replenishes the Kidney Essence [腎精] that engenders marrow, supporting structural vitality.
4. Builds flesh and muscle [長肌肉]: Nourishes the post-heavenly nutritive qi [營氣] and Blood, thereby generating muscle tissue.
5. Prepared as a decoction [作湯], eliminates cold-heat accumulations [除寒熱積聚] and relieves impediment conditions [除痺]: The liquid preparation was considered especially effective for clearing accumulations of pathogenic heat and cold lodged in the interior.
6. The fresh/raw form is superior [生者尤良]: The Shennong Bencaojing explicitly privileges the freshly harvested, unprocessed root over the dried form for its stronger blood-cooling and fluid-generating actions.
7. Extended use lightens the body and prevents aging [久服輕身不老]: A classical tonic claim characteristic of Superior Herbs.

---

KAMPO ELABORATIONS (Japanese tradition, Edo period and onward):

In the Kampo tradition, Jio (生地黄) is distinguished sharply from Kan Jio (乾地黄, dried rehmannia) and Juku Jio (熟地黄, processed/prepared rehmannia). Sheng Di Huang in its raw or lightly dried state is valued above all for:
- Cooling Blood heat [涼血, liáng xuè]: Canonical in febrile disease patterns where heat has penetrated the Blood level [血分], causing reckless movement of blood (hemorrhage, purpura).
- Nourishing Yin and generating fluids [滋陰生津]: Addresses Kidney and Liver Yin deficiency with symptoms of dry mouth, afternoon fever, night sweats, and emaciation.
- Stopping bleeding [止血]: Both direct hemostasis and by cooling the Blood to prevent its reckless movement.

Kampo texts such as the Honzō Kōmoku Keimō (本草綱目啓蒙, 1803, Ono Ranzan) and the Yakuchū Bencao Ganmu tradition further classify Sheng Di Huang as an herb that 'enters the Blood division and cools without drying' — distinguishing it from bitter-cold herbs that damage fluids.

---

KEY CLASSICAL FORMULAS:

1. Sì Wù Tāng [四物湯, 'Four-Substance Decoction'] — foundational Blood-nourishing formula; the raw or dried form anchors the Blood-cooling action alongside Dang Gui, Bai Shao, and Chuan Xiong.
2. Liù Wèi Dì Huáng Wán [六味地黃丸, 'Six-Ingredient Rehmannia Pill'] — though this formula traditionally uses Shú Dì Huáng (processed), some variant traditions substitute Shēng Dì Huáng for stronger Yin-cooling.
3. Xī Jiǎo Dì Huáng Tāng [犀角地黃湯, 'Rhinoceros Horn and Rehmannia Decoction'] — the paradigmatic formula for Blood-level heat with hemorrhage; Shēng Dì Huáng is indispensable here for cooling Blood and stopping bleeding.
4. Qīng Yíng Tāng [清營湯, 'Clear the Nutritive Level Decoction'] — Warm Disease school formula using Shēng Dì Huáng to cool the nutritive level and protect Yin.
5. Zhú Yè Shí Gāo Tāng [竹葉石膏湯] variant uses — for febrile exhaustion of fluids.

---

PREPARATION NOTES:

- Fresh juice [鮮汁]: Pressed from the raw root; strongest cooling and hemostatic action.
- Decoction [湯劑]: Standard preparation; the Shennong Bencaojing specifically recommends decoction for accumulation and impediment conditions.
- Raw dried [生乾地黃]: Light drying preserves most of the cooling nature.
- Contraindicated with: Lai Fu Zi [萊菔子, radish seed] per classical incompatibility lists; use with caution in Spleen-deficient patients with loose stools, as the cold, moist nature may aggravate digestive weakness.

MODERN IDENTIFICATION: Rehmannia glutinosa (Gaertn.) Libosch. ex Steud. (Family Orobanchaceae / formerly Scrophulariaceae). The fresh or lightly processed tuberous root. Confidence: HIGH.

entities_mentioned.name: Sheng Di Huang / 生地黃
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rehmannia glutinosa (Gaertn.) Libosch. ex Steud. — fresh or raw-dried tuberous root; family Orobanchaceae (formerly Scrophulariaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most securely identified and continuously used herbs in the Chinese materia medica. The Shennong Bencaojing entry is foundational; the raw form is distinguished from processed (Shu Di Huang 熟地黄) in all subsequent literature. Japanese Kampo texts consistently use the term Jio (じおう) for both forms, disambiguating by prefix: Sho-jio (生地黄) vs. Juku-jio (熟地黄).

entities_mentioned.name: Xī Jiǎo / 犀角 (Rhinoceros Horn)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Horn of Rhinoceros spp. — now CITES-prohibited; substituted clinically by Shuǐ Niú Jiǎo (水牛角, water buffalo horn) in modern practice
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as co-herb in Xī Jiǎo Dì Huáng Tāng. Included for classical formula completeness; rhinoceros horn is not available or legal in contemporary medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Shu Di Huang / 熟地黃
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rehmannia glutinosa — processed (wine-steamed) root
entities_mentioned.notes: The processed form diverges significantly in nature (warm, sweet) and actions (tonifies Blood and Essence without cooling). Distinguished from Sheng Di Huang throughout this entry to prevent conflation, a common source of error in translation.

entities_mentioned.name: Ono Ranzan / 小野蘭山
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Japanese Kampo botanist and pharmacologist (1729–1810 CE); author of Honzō Kōmoku Keimō (本草綱目啓蒙, 1803)
entities_mentioned.notes: Representative of the mature Edo-period Kampo elaboration tradition that systematized Chinese materia medica for Japanese clinical use.

results.translator_notes: 1. SOURCE TEXT CONDITION: The provided segment is a modern summary prompt rather than a scanned classical Chinese primary source passage; accordingly, the 'original_text' field reconstructs the canonical Shennong Bencaojing entry for Sheng Di Huang from the received textual tradition (森立之 Mori Risshi critical edition and related recensions) to provide an authentic classical anchor. The entry 生者尤良 ('the fresh form is especially effective') is a distinctive and historically significant clause preserved across major recensions. 2. FORMULA NOTE: Liù Wèi Dì Huáng Wán (六味地黃丸) canonically uses Shú Dì Huáng; this is noted explicitly to prevent a common conflation. 3. KAMPO vs. TCM DIVERGENCE: Kampo tradition tends to use Sheng Di Huang more conservatively than mainland TCM, often preferring the processed form (Juku Jio) for chronic tonification; the raw form is reserved for acute febrile or hemorrhagic presentations. 4. INCOMPATIBILITY: The classical prohibition against combining Di Huang with Lai Fu Zi (radish seed) is noted in the Bencao Yanyi and repeated in Kampo texts; its pharmacological basis remains unverified but should be flagged for clinical completeness. 5. OCR NOTE: No OCR errors were present in the source segment as submitted; the original_text is a scholarly reconstruction, not a transcription.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Sheng Di Huang 生地黃

results.title_inferred: Xuan Shen (Figwort Root): Classical Materia Medica Entry
results.original_text: 玄參 — 味苦，微寒。主腹中寒熱積聚，女子產乳餘疾，補腎氣，令人目明。一名重臺。生川谷。
results.translation:
Xuan Shen [Dark Parameter / Figwort Root]

**Chinese Name:** Xuān Shēn (玄參)
**Japanese Kampo Name:** Genjin (玄参)
**Classical Category:** Middle Herb [中品, zhōng pǐn] — capable of nourishing the human constitution; may be used over extended periods with care.

**Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]:** Bitter [苦, kǔ]; some classical sources additionally note a faint saltiness [微鹹].
**Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]:** Slightly Cold [微寒, wēi hán].
**Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]:** Kidney [腎], Lung [肺], Stomach [胃] — as elaborated in post-Shennong commentary and codified in the Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目, 1596) and subsequent Kampo systematization.

**Shennong Bencaojing Therapeutic Actions (c.200 CE):**
Governing [主]: accumulations of cold and heat within the abdomen [腹中寒熱積聚]; residual disorders following childbirth and lactation in women [女子產乳餘疾]; supplements Kidney Qi [補腎氣]; brightens the eyes [令人目明].
Alternate classical name: Chóngtái [重臺, 'layered platform']. Habitat: grows in river valleys and mountain gorges [生川谷].

**Elaborated Therapeutic Actions (Kampo and post-classical tradition):**
1. Clears Heat and cools the Blood [清熱涼血]: addresses febrile disease [溫病] at the Ying [營] and Blood [血] levels; high fever, eruptions, purpura.
2. Nourishes Yin and reduces Deficiency Fire [滋陰降火]: dry throat, oral ulcers, sore throat due to Kidney-Yin deficiency allowing Ministerial Fire to rise.
3. Resolves toxins and disperses nodules [解毒散結]: scrofula [瘰癧], goiter [癭瘤], hard swellings, sore throat with toxin accumulation [咽喉腫痛].
4. Moistens dryness: constipation due to Intestinal dryness from Yin deficiency.
5. Softens hardness [軟堅]: classical application in masses and accumulations, consistent with the Shennong indication of 寒熱積聚.

**Key Classical Formulas [方劑]:**
- *Zengye Tang* [增液湯] (Wenbing Tiaobian, 1798): Xuan Shen + Sheng Di Huang + Mai Dong — cardinal formula for Yin-deficiency constipation and fluid insufficiency.
- *Yangyin Qingfei Tang* [養陰清肺湯]: Xuan Shen featured prominently; used for diphtheria and dry, painful throat.
- *Xiao Luo Wan* [消瘰丸]: Xuan Shen + Mǔ Lì + Bèi Mǔ — for scrofula and phlegm-fire nodules.
- *Qingying Tang* [清營湯] (Ye Tianshi tradition): Xuan Shen in formulas for heat entering the Ying level.
- In Kampo (Japan), Genjin appears in *Keishi-bukuryo-gan* variants and in formulas targeting 'yin fire' [陰火] syndromes as theorized by Li Dongyuan and transmitted through the Go-san-ke school.

**Preparation Methods:**
Classically decocted raw [生用, shēng yòng] for heat-clearing and toxin-resolving actions. May be prepared with salt-frying [鹽炒] to direct action to the Kidney channel. Standard decoction dose in classical sources: 9–15g (up to 30g in severe cases). Contraindicated with [反] Black Veratrum Root [藜蘆, lí lú] per classical incompatibility rules [十八反, shíbā fǎn].

entities_mentioned.name: 玄參 (Xuān Shēn)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scrophularia ningpoensis Hemsley (Scrophulariaceae); root used medicinally. Occasionally S. buergeriana or related species in regional practice.
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 玄參 ('dark/mysterious parameter') likely references the dark color of the dried root. Alternate classical name 重臺 ('layered platform') may refer to the tiered appearance of the inflorescence. The Shennong Bencaojing entry is terse; most clinical elaboration derives from the Tang (Su Wen commentaries), Song (Bencao Yanyi), and Ming (Bencao Gangmu) eras, with Japanese Kampo transmission through the Edo period (江戸時代) schools.

entities_mentioned.name: 藜蘆 (Lí Lú)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Veratrum nigrum L. or V. album L. (Melanthiaceae/Liliaceae); Black False Hellebore / White Hellebore root
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical incompatibility antagonist [相反] to Xuan Shen per the Shíbā Fǎn (十八反) system. Highly toxic; the prohibition is pharmacologically plausible and consistently maintained across traditions.

entities_mentioned.name: 增液湯 (Zēngyè Tāng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical formula: 'Increase-the-Fluids Decoction' — Xuan Shen, Sheng Di Huang, Mai Men Dong
entities_mentioned.notes: Formulated by Wu Jutong (吳鞠通) in the Wenbing Tiaobian (溫病條辨, 1798). Canonical in both TCM and Kampo for Yin-fluid depletion.

entities_mentioned.name: 消瘰丸 (Xiāo Luǒ Wán)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical formula for scrofula: Xuan Shen + Mu Li (oyster shell) + Zhe Bei Mu (fritillary bulb)
entities_mentioned.notes: Illustrates the 'soften hardness and disperse nodules' [軟堅散結] application of Xuan Shen, consistent with the Shennong indication of 寒熱積聚.

results.translator_notes: The source text provided is a modern prompt-derived synthesis rather than a verbatim manuscript passage; accordingly, the 'original_text' field has been reconstructed from the canonical Shennong Bencaojing entry for 玄參 as it appears in standard critical editions (e.g., the Mori Tateyuki 森立之 recension, 1854, and the Sun Xingyan 孫星衍 edition, 1799). The translation and elaboration draw on: (1) Shennong Bencaojing [神農本草經, c.200 CE], (2) Bencao Jing Jizhu [本草經集注, Tao Hongjing, c.500 CE], (3) Bencao Gangmu [本草綱目, Li Shizhen, 1596], (4) Wenbing Tiaobian [溫病條辨, Wu Jutong, 1798], and (5) Japanese Kampo sources including Yakuchō [薬徴, Yoshimasu Todo, 1771] and Koho-school commentaries. The Five Flavors 'faint saltiness' attribute is post-Shennong (appearing in Bencao Yanyi and elaborated in Ming sources) and is flagged accordingly. Plantacopia users should note that the classical channel-tropism system [歸經] was not codified until the Jin-Yuan period (金元, 12th–14th c.) and is therefore anachronistic when attributed directly to the Shennong text.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Xuan Shen 玄參

results.title_inferred: Mai Dong (Ophiopogon japonicus) — Classical and Kampo Monograph
results.original_text: 麥冬 — 味甘，平。主心腹結氣，傷中傷飽，胃絡脈絕，羸瘦短氣。久服輕身，不老，不飢。生川谷及堤阪。
results.translation:
Mai Dong [麥冬, 'wheat winter']. Flavor: sweet [gan 甘]. Nature: balanced/neutral [ping 平], tending toward mild cold [wei han 微寒] in later elaborations. Channel tropism [gui jing 歸經]: Lung [Fei 肺], Stomach [Wei 胃], Heart [Xin 心].

Classical category: Superior herb [shang pin 上品] — suitable for prolonged use without toxicity.

Primary actions according to the Shennong Bencaojing tradition: Governs [zhu 主] binding and stagnation of qi [jie qi 結氣] in the chest and abdomen; injuries [shang 傷] to the interior caused by overeating [shang bao 傷飽]; severance or collapse of the stomach's network vessels [wei luo mai jue 胃絡脈絕]; emaciation [lei shou 羸瘦]; and shortness of breath [duan qi 短氣]. Prolonged consumption [jiu fu 久服] lightens the body [qing shen 輕身], prevents aging [bu lao 不老], and suppresses hunger [bu ji 不飢]. It grows in river valleys and along embankments and slopes [sheng chuan gu ji di ban 生川谷及堤阪].

Kampo (Japanese) elaboration: In Kampo medicine Mai Dong (Jp. Bakumondō 麦門冬) is classified as a yin-nourishing, fluid-engendering [yang yin sheng jin 養陰生津] herb of the first rank. Key actions include: (1) nourishing Lung yin and moistening dryness [run fei zhi ke 潤肺止咳] — used in patterns of dry, non-productive cough with scanty sticky sputum, often from Lung heat or Lung dryness; (2) nourishing Stomach yin and generating fluids [yang wei sheng jin 養胃生津] — indicated in febrile disease injuring stomach fluids, causing thirst, dry mouth, and constipation; (3) clearing Heart heat and calming the spirit [qing xin chu fan 清心除煩] — used in yin deficiency patterns with vexation, insomnia, and palpitations.

Key classical formulas featuring Mai Dong:
— Maimendō-tō (麦門冬湯 / Mài Mén Dōng Tāng, Jingui Yaolüe): Principal herb; treats ascending counterflow of Lung qi with cough, spitting of saliva, dyspnea. Combined with Pinellia (Ban Xia), Ginseng (Ren Shen), Licorice (Gan Cao), Jujube (Da Zao), Japonica rice (Geng Mi).
— Zengye Tang (增液湯): Nourishes yin and increases fluids in febrile disease; combined with Xuanshen (Scrophularia) and Shengdihuang (Rehmannia).
— Qingying Tang (清營湯): Clears nutritive-level heat; Mai Dong supports fluid preservation alongside Shengdihuang and Xuanshen.
— Tianwang Buxin Dan (天王補心丹): Classical formula nourishing Heart yin and calming the spirit; Mai Dong is a supporting herb.
— Shengmai San (生脈散): Tonifies qi, nourishes yin, stops sweating; Mai Dong combined with Ren Shen (Ginseng) and Wu Wei Zi (Schisandra). Widely used in Kampo for cardiac and pulmonary debility.

entities_mentioned.name: Mai Dong 麥冬 (Bakumondō 麦門冬)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ophiopogon japonicus (Thunb.) Ker Gawl. — tuber of mondo grass (Asparagaceae / Liliaceae sensu lato)
entities_mentioned.notes: The drug consists of the dried fleshy root tubers. O. japonicus is the standard pharmacopoeial species in both Chinese and Japanese practice. Ophiopogon japonicus contains steroidal saponins (ophiopogonins), homoisoflavonoids, and polysaccharides; modern studies support immunomodulatory, cardioprotective, and anti-inflammatory activities consistent with classical indications.

entities_mentioned.name: Maimendō-tō / Mài Mén Dōng Tāng 麥門冬湯
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical compound formula from Zhang Zhongjing's Jingui Yaolüe (金匱要略, c. 220 CE). Still officially listed in the Japanese National Health Insurance formulary as Kampo preparation No. 29.

entities_mentioned.name: Shengmai San 生脈散
entities_mentioned.notes: Formula attributed to Li Dongyuan (Jin dynasty). Extensively used in Kampo cardiology; a modern injectable form (Shengmai injection) is used in Chinese hospitals for cardiac failure.

entities_mentioned.name: Zengye Tang 增液湯
entities_mentioned.notes: From Wenbing Tiaobian (溫病條辨, Wu Jutong, 1798 CE). Canonical formula of the Warm Disease school for yin-deficiency constipation.

results.translator_notes: The original source segment supplied is a structured label rather than a verbatim classical manuscript passage. The original_text field therefore reconstructs the standard Shennong Bencaojing entry for Mai Dong as preserved in the Mori Tateyuki recension and the Zhongjing-era citations, which represents the authoritative classical wording for this herb. The Four Natures entry in the Shennong Bencaojing proper reads 'ping 平' (neutral/balanced); the 'wei han 微寒' (slightly cold) qualification is a post-Han consensus found in the Bencao Jing Jizhu (陶弘景, c. 500 CE) and universally accepted in both TCM and Kampo — this divergence is standard and is flagged here for scholarly transparency. The Five Flavors entry 'gan 甘' (sweet) is unanimous across all recensions; some secondary sources add a slight 'wei ku 微苦' (slightly bitter) quality, but this is not classical. Channel tropism to the Heart is a Tang/Song dynasty addition not present in the Shennong Bencaojing original.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Mai Dong 麥冬

results.title_inferred: Tian Dong (Heavenly Asparagus Root): Classical Yin Tonic for Lung and Kidney
results.original_text: 天冬 — 味苦平。主諸暴風濕偏痹，強骨髓，殺三蟲，去伏尸。久服輕身益氣延年。一名顛勒。生山谷。
results.translation:
Tian Dong [Heavenly Winter / Asparagus Root] — Flavor: bitter, with sweet undertones; Nature: greatly cold. It is classed among the Superior Herbs [shang pin 上品] in the Shennong Bencaojing tradition. It governs [zhu 主]: sudden [violent] wind-damp painful obstruction [feng shi pian bi 風濕偏痹], strengthens the bone marrow [gu sui 骨髓], eliminates the three worms [san chong 三蟲], and expels hidden corpse-[toxin] [fu shi 伏尸]. Prolonged consumption lightens the body [qing shen 輕身], augments qi [yi qi 益氣], and extends years [yan nian 延年]. An alternate name is Dian Le [顛勒]. It grows in mountain valleys.

— FIVE FLAVORS [wu wei 五味]: Bitter, sweet.
— FOUR NATURES [si qi 四氣]: Greatly cold [da han 大寒].
— CHANNEL TROPISM [gui jing 歸經]: Lung [fei 肺], Kidney [shen 腎].

— CLASSICAL THERAPEUTIC ACTIONS: (1) Nourishes yin and moistens dryness [zi yin run zao 滋陰潤燥]: indicated for lung-kidney yin deficiency with vacuous heat [xu re 虛熱], dry cough, expectoration of blood-streaked sputum, and consumptive taxation [lao zhai 勞瘵]. (2) Clears lung heat and downbears fire [qing fei jiang huo 清肺降火]: addresses fire flaming upward due to yin deficiency, manifesting as sore throat, dry mouth, and afternoon tidal fever. (3) Moistens the intestines and frees the stool [run chang tong bian 潤腸通便]: used for constipation arising from fluid insufficiency or yin depletion in the elderly. (4) Strengthens bone marrow and sinews: the classical text attributes tonification of the marrow and elimination of wind-damp obstruction, reflecting its deep action on the Kidney essence [jing 精].

— JAPANESE KAMPO ELABORATION [Kanpō 漢方]: Known in Kampo as Ten-Mon-Dō [天門冬, Tenmondō]. Classified as a superior-grade yin tonic. Kampo physicians of the Edo period (notably in the Kohō-ha [古方派] tradition) employed it primarily for lung-dryness cough, night sweats, steaming bone disorder [bone-steaming taxation heat], and senile constipation. It is distinguished from Mai Dong [麥冬, Bakumondō] in that Tian Dong acts more powerfully on the Kidney and is more cold in nature, making it suited to more severe yin deficiency with substantial fire.

— KEY CLASSICAL FORMULAS: (1) San Cai Feng Sui Dan [三才封髓丹] — combines Tian Dong, Di Huang [地黃], and Ren Shen [人參] to replenish the 'three treasures' of Heaven, Earth, and Humanity, tonifying yin and anchoring ministerial fire. (2) Tian Wang Bu Xin Dan [天王補心丹] — a principal formula for heart-kidney yin deficiency with palpitations, insomnia, and anxiety; Tian Dong serves as a key yin-nourishing constituent alongside Sheng Di Huang, Dan Shen, and Suan Zao Ren. (3) Yue Hua Wan [月華丸] — classical formula for lung consumption [fei lao 肺癆] and blood-streaked sputum; Tian Dong moistens and cools the lung while arresting cough. (4) Er Dong Gao [二冬膏] — a prepared syrup pairing Tian Dong and Mai Dong, used in Kampo and late imperial Chinese medicine for chronic dry cough and yin-deficiency thirst.

— PREPARATION: The fresh tuberous roots are harvested in autumn or winter, steamed or blanched to remove the outer skin, and dried. They may be used raw [sheng yong 生用] to emphasize clearing and cooling, or prepared with honey-frying [mi zhi 蜜炙] to augment their moistening and tonifying properties. Contraindicated in patterns of cold from deficiency of the Spleen and Stomach, or in diarrhea due to yang deficiency.

entities_mentioned.name: Tian Dong 天冬 (also 天門冬, Tian Men Dong)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asparagus cochinchinensis (Lour.) Merr. — the tuberous root; family Asparagaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The dried tuberous root of Asparagus cochinchinensis is the standard pharmaceutical material (Radix Asparagi) in the Chinese Pharmacopoeia. Rich in steroidal saponins, mucilaginous polysaccharides, and asparagine. The classical alternate name Dian Le [顛勒] appears in the Shennong Bencaojing and several Bencao commentaries without further botanical clarification.

entities_mentioned.name: Mai Dong 麥冬 (Bakumondō in Kampo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ophiopogon japonicus (Thunb.) Ker Gawl. — tuber; family Asparagaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned comparatively: Mai Dong and Tian Dong are frequently paired or contrasted in classical literature. Mai Dong is considered milder, primarily acting on the Lung and Stomach, while Tian Dong more deeply reaches the Kidney.

entities_mentioned.name: San Cai Feng Sui Dan 三才封髓丹
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical compound formula; 'San Cai' (Three Powers / Three Talents) refers to Heaven (Tian Dong), Earth (Di Huang), and Humanity (Ren Shen). Attributed to the Danxi school and appearing in various Ming dynasty compilations.

entities_mentioned.name: Tian Wang Bu Xin Dan 天王補心丹
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most important classical formulas for heart-kidney yin deficiency; widely used in both Chinese medicine and Kampo. Tian Dong is a named ingredient in most versions of the formula.

entities_mentioned.name: Fu Shi 伏尸
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely refers to latent or chronic consumptive diseases, possibly including tuberculosis or parasitic conditions
entities_mentioned.notes: Fu shi is a classical Chinese medical concept denoting a hidden or lodged pathogen causing chronic wasting; interpretations vary across commentators. In the Bencaojing context it may overlap with 'ghost [gui 鬼] pathogens' or chronic infectious wasting.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided is a brief modern annotation rather than a verbatim classical text; accordingly, the 'original_text' field reconstructs the canonical Shennong Bencaojing entry for Tian Men Dong as transmitted in standard editions (e.g., the Mori Tateyuki [森立之] reconstruction and the Gu Ben Bencaojing edition), supplemented by elaborations from the Bencao Gangmu [本草綱目] and Kampo tradition. The translation and therapeutic expansion draw on the Bencao Jing Shu [本草經疏] (Miu Xiyong, 1625), the Bencao Beiyao [本草備要] (Wang Ang, 1694), and the Kampo pharmacopoeial tradition as codified in Yoshimasu Todo's [吉益東洞] and subsequent Meiji-era Kampo texts. Where the original Bencaojing text uses archaic disease terminology (pian bi, fu shi, san chong), period-appropriate equivalents have been retained in brackets. The channel tropism (Lung, Kidney) is a post-Han systematization not explicit in the 200 CE text itself but firmly established by the Song-Jin-Yuan period and accepted across Kampo tradition.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Tian Dong 天冬

results.title_inferred: Gou Ji (Wolfberry / Goji Berry): Classical Chinese and Kampo Monograph
results.original_text: 枸杞 — 味苦，寒。主五內邪氣，熱中消渴，周痹，久服堅筋骨，輕身不老，耐寒暑。生平澤及丘陵阪岸。
results.translation:
Gou Ji [枸杞; pinyin: gǒu qǐ; Japanese Kampo: Kukoshi / 枸杞子 (Kukoshi) for the fruit, Jikoppi / 地骨皮 (Jikoppi) for the root bark]

**Classical Category:** Superior Herb [上品, shàng pǐn] — suitable for prolonged use to promote health and longevity without toxicity.

**Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]:** Sweet [甘, gān] (fruit / berry); Bitter [苦, kǔ] (root bark, per earliest Shennong tradition); slightly acrid in some later commentaries.

**Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]:** Neutral to slightly Cool [平/微寒, píng / wēi hán]. The fruit (Gou Qi Zi) is generally considered neutral-warm; the root bark (Di Gu Pi, 地骨皮) is cold.

**Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]:** Liver [肝, gān], Kidney [腎, shèn], Lung [肺, fèi].

**Shennong Bencaojing (c. 200 CE) Core Statement:** 'Flavor: bitter; nature: cold. Governs the evil qi [pathogenic influences] of the five viscera [五內, wǔ nèi], heat in the center [熱中, rè zhōng], wasting-thirst [消渴, xiāo kě], and generalized painful obstruction [周痹, zhōu bì]. Taken over a long period, it strengthens the sinews and bones, lightens the body, prevents aging, and confers resistance to cold and heat. It grows in level marshes and on hillside banks.'

**Therapeutic Actions (Shennong Bencaojing tradition):**
1. Nourishes Liver and Kidney yin [滋補肝腎]: Addresses deficiency patterns of the liver and kidney, including lumbar soreness, weakness of the knees, and diminished vitality.
2. Benefits essence and brightens the eyes [益精明目]: Classical primary indication for blurred vision, diminished visual acuity, and floaters arising from liver-kidney yin deficiency failing to nourish the eyes.
3. Moistens the Lung [潤肺]: Used in patterns of lung yin deficiency with dry cough or consumptive disorders.
4. Clears deficiency heat [清虛熱] (root bark / Di Gu Pi): The root cortex is specifically directed at steaming bone fever [骨蒸潮熱, gǔ zhēng cháo rè], night sweats, and childhood nutritive heat.
5. Relieves wasting-thirst [消渴, xiāo kě]: Corresponds broadly to diabetic or hyperglycemic presentations in modern clinical correlation.
6. Expels pathogenic qi from the five viscera and governs generalized painful obstruction [周痹].

**Kampo (Japanese) Elaboration:**
In the Japanese Kampo tradition, Kukoshi [枸杞子] is classified as a tonic [補益薬] herb, primarily employed to supplement liver and kidney yin-essence [精血]. It appears in formulas addressing visual deterioration in the elderly, reproductive insufficiency, and debility following chronic illness. Jikoppi [地骨皮] (root bark) is separately employed as a heat-clearing, yin-preserving agent.

**Key Classical Formulas:**
- **Qi Ju Di Huang Wan [杞菊地黄丸]**: 'Wolfberry and Chrysanthemum Rehmannia Pill' — principal formula for liver-kidney yin deficiency with visual disturbance, dry eyes, and dizziness. Gou Qi Zi and Ju Hua [菊花] are added to the foundational Liu Wei Di Huang Wan.
- **Wu Zi Yan Zong Wan [五子衍宗丸]**: 'Five-Seed Progeny Pill' — classical male reproductive tonic featuring Gou Qi Zi alongside Tu Si Zi, Wu Wei Zi, Fu Pen Zi, and Che Qian Zi.
- **Di Gu Pi Tang [地骨皮湯]** (various): Root bark decoctions for febrile and consumptive heat disorders.

**Modern Identification:** Lycium barbarum L. (family Solanaceae) — Barbary wolfberry / Goji berry — for the fruit and shoot; Lycium chinense Mill. is the alternate species accepted in Chinese Pharmacopoeia for both fruit and root bark.

**Preparation Methods (classical):**
- Fruit (Gou Qi Zi): Dried ripe berries, taken in decoction [煎服, jiān fú], infused in medicinal wine [藥酒], or consumed directly as a food-medicine.
- Root bark (Di Gu Pi): Dried and decocted, often combined with heat-clearing and yin-nourishing herbs.
- Tender shoots and leaves: Also used as a vegetable and tonic food in classical dietetics [食療].

**Dosage (classical range):** Fruit: 6–18 g per decoction; Root bark: 6–15 g per decoction.

entities_mentioned.name: 枸杞子 (Gou Qi Zi) — Wolfberry / Goji Berry fruit
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lycium barbarum L. or Lycium chinense Mill. (Solanaceae) — ripe fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: The fruit is the most commonly used part in contemporary practice; the Shennong Bencaojing entry covers the whole plant. Chinese Pharmacopoeia (2020) recognizes both L. barbarum and L. chinense as official sources.

entities_mentioned.name: 地骨皮 (Di Gu Pi) — Root bark of Lycium
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lycium barbarum L. or Lycium chinense Mill. — root cortex
entities_mentioned.notes: Classified separately from the fruit in later materia medica; cold in nature, directed at heat-clearing and yin-preservation. Major indication: steaming bone fever and night sweats.

entities_mentioned.name: 杞菊地黄丸 (Qi Ju Di Huang Wan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula — not a single plant
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most important formulas featuring Gou Qi Zi; widely used in Kampo (Kigiku Jioganh) for age-related visual decline.

entities_mentioned.name: 五子衍宗丸 (Wu Zi Yan Zong Wan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula — not a single plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Prominent male reproductive tonic in both TCM and Kampo traditions; Gou Qi Zi serves as a kidney-essence supplementing agent within this formula.

results.translator_notes: The source text supplied is a modern editorial summary rather than a verbatim classical passage. The 'original_text' field above reconstructs the core Shennong Bencaojing sentence (味苦，寒。主五內邪氣…) as preserved in authoritative recensions (e.g., Sun Xingyan's 1799 reconstruction and Mori Tateyuki's Honzō Keimō). The flavor descriptor shifts from 'bitter/cold' in the earliest stratum to 'sweet/neutral' for the fruit in Tang–Song materia medica (e.g., Xinxiu Bencao, 659 CE; Bencao Yanyi); this reflects part-specific differentiation over time. The Japanese Kampo names Kukoshi (枸杞子) and Jikoppi (地骨皮) are standard on'yomi readings used in contemporary Kampo pharmaceutical nomenclature. 'Wasting-thirst' (消渴, xiāo kě) is the classical correlate most often mapped to diabetes mellitus in modern comparative studies, though the classical concept is broader. No genuinely illegible passages encountered; source was an editorial prompt rather than OCR of a manuscript.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Gou Ji 枸杞

results.title_inferred: Shan Zhu Yu (山茱萸) — Cornelian Cherry Fruit: Kidney and Liver Tonic, Astringent
results.original_text: 山茱萸 (Shān Zhū Yú) — 味酸，平。主心下邪氣，寒熱，溫中，逐寒濕痹，去三蟲，久服輕身。一名蜀酸棗。生山谷。
results.translation:
Shan Zhu Yu [Mountain Cornelian Cherry, 山茱萸]. Flavor: sour; Nature: neutral [ping, 平; later classical tradition refines this to slightly warm]. 

Primary actions according to the Shennong Bencaojing: Governs [zhǔ, 主] evil qi [xié qì] below the heart; dispels cold-heat [hán rè] alternation; warms the center [wēn zhōng]; expels cold-damp impediment [hán shī bì, 寒濕痹]; eliminates the three worms [sān chóng, 三蟲]; prolonged use lightens the body [jiǔ fú qīng shēn, 久服輕身].

Alternate classical name: Shǔ Suān Zǎo [蜀酸棗, 'Sichuan sour jujube']. Habitat: grows in mountain valleys [shān gǔ, 山谷].

--- CLASSICAL CATEGORY ---
Superior Herb [shàng pǐn, 上品]: classed among tonifying, life-prolonging substances. Non-toxic.

--- FIVE FLAVORS (Wǔ Wèi, 五味) ---
Sour [suān, 酸]: primary flavor. Sour flavor governs astriction and consolidation [shōu sè, 收澀], anchoring essence [jīng, 精] and preventing leakage.

--- FOUR NATURES (Sì Qì, 四氣) ---
Slightly warm [wēi wēn, 微溫] (as standardized in post-Bencaojing tradition, e.g., the Bencao Gangmu and Kampo scholarship, correcting the earlier 'neutral' designation). Non-toxic.

--- CHANNEL TROPISM (Guī Jīng, 歸經) ---
Enters the Kidney [shèn jīng, 腎經] and Liver [gān jīng, 肝經] channels. Some Kampo sources additionally note an affinity for the Pericardium channel in the context of essence-securing formulas.

--- THERAPEUTIC ACTIONS (Shennong / Kampo synthesis) ---
1. Tonifies and supplements the Liver and Kidney [bǔ yì gān shèn, 補益肝腎]: indicated for deficiency patterns of both organs, including lumbar soreness, weak knees, dizziness, and tinnitus arising from liver-kidney yin or yang insufficiency.
2. Astringes essence and stops seminal emission [sè jīng gù tuō, 澀精固脫]: classical use for spermatorrhea [yí jīng, 遺精], premature ejaculation, and urinary frequency due to kidney vacuity failing to consolidate.
3. Stabilizes menses and stops bleeding [gù jīng zhǐ xuè, 固經止血]: for excessive uterine bleeding [bēng lòu, 崩漏] and irregular menstruation from kidney-liver deficiency.
4. Restrains sweating and stems desertion [liǎn hàn gù tuō, 斂汗固脫]: used in critical patterns of spontaneous sweating [zì hàn] or night sweats [dào hàn], and in collapse syndromes. This is the action flagged in the source label ('sweating').
5. Warms the center and expels cold-damp impediment [wēn zhōng qū hán shī bì]: archaic Bencaojing indication, less emphasized in mature Kampo but retained in cold-pattern bi-syndrome contexts.
6. Eliminates the three worms [qū sān chóng]: archaic antiparasitic indication from the Shennong corpus; rarely foregrounded in Kampo clinical use.

--- KAMPO (Japanese) ELABORATION ---
Japanese Kampo name: Sanshuyu [サンシュユ / 山茱萸]. Regarded as a critical kidney-essence astringent in Japanese Kampo tradition, particularly via the formulas Rokumigan [六味丸, Liù Wèi Wán] and Hachimijiogan [八味地黄丸, Bā Wèi Dì Huáng Wán], where Sanshuyu provides the consolidating, essence-retaining counterweight to the formula's yin-supplementing and yang-warming actions. Kampo texts emphasize its dual capacity to nourish liver blood while astringing kidney essence, making it indispensable in age-related deficiency disorders.

--- KEY CLASSICAL FORMULAS ---
1. Liù Wèi Dì Huáng Wán [六味地黄丸] (Six-Ingredient Pill with Rehmannia, Song dynasty, Qian Yi): foundational kidney-yin tonic; Shan Zhu Yu is one of the 'three tonics' [sān bǔ]. Kampo: Rokumigan.
2. Bā Wèi Dì Huáng Wán [八味地黄丸] (Eight-Ingredient Pill with Rehmannia, attributed to Zhang Zhongjing, c.220 CE): kidney yang tonic; Shan Zhu Yu stabilizes essence. Kampo: Hachimijiogan.
3. Zuǒ Guī Wán [左归丸] (Restore the Left Kidney Pill, Zhang Jiebin, Ming dynasty): deep yin and essence supplementation.
4. Shēn Qì Wán [肾气丸] (Kidney Qi Pill, Jin Gui Yao Lüe): treats kidney qi deficiency with urinary dysfunction, lower back pain, and exhaustion.
5. Gù Chōng Tāng [固冲汤] (Stabilize Gushing Decoction, Zhang Xichun, Qing dynasty): for uterine bleeding from kidney deficiency.

entities_mentioned.name: 山茱萸 (Shān Zhū Yú)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cornus officinalis Sieb. & Zucc. — Cornelian cherry; dried ripe sarcocarp (fruit pulp) of the family Cornaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Well-established botanical identification consistent across Chinese pharmacopoeia (ChP 2020), Japanese pharmacopoeia, and Western botanical literature. The alternate classical name Shǔ Suān Zǎo [蜀酸棗] reflects early geographic association with Sichuan. The drug part used is the sarcocarp (flesh) of the dried ripe fruit, with the seed removed. Active constituents include iridoid glycosides (morroniside, loganin), ursolic acid, and tannins, which support classical astringent and tonic actions.

entities_mentioned.name: 六味地黄丸 (Liù Wèi Dì Huáng Wán)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical Chinese herbal formula, Six-Ingredient Pill with Rehmannia
entities_mentioned.notes: Composed by Qian Yi (钱乙) in the Song dynasty (c. 1119 CE) as a pediatric adaptation of the older Shen Qi Wan. Contains Rehmannia, Shan Zhu Yu, Dioscorea (the 'three tonics') plus Alisma, Moutan, and Poria (the 'three drains'). Canonical kidney-yin deficiency formula in both TCM and Kampo.

entities_mentioned.name: 八味地黄丸 / 腎気丸 (Bā Wèi Dì Huáng Wán / Hachimijiogan)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical formula: Eight-Ingredient Pill with Rehmannia (= Kidney Qi Pill plus cinnamon and aconite)
entities_mentioned.notes: Traced to Zhang Zhongjing's Jin Gui Yao Lüe (金匱要略, c.220 CE). One of the most frequently prescribed Kampo formulas in contemporary Japanese clinical practice, particularly for geriatric deficiency, diabetic nephropathy support, and lower urinary tract symptoms.

entities_mentioned.name: 三蟲 (Sān Chóng) — Three Worms
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Archaic Chinese medical concept referring to intestinal parasites (broadly: large worms/Ascaris, tapeworm, and pinworm in some taxonomies)
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'three worms' is a classical Bencaojing category that appears in descriptions of numerous upper-class herbs but does not correspond precisely to a single modern parasitological category. The antiparasitic claim for Shan Zhu Yu is not foregrounded in Kampo or modern TCM clinical use and may reflect archaic formulaic language rather than primary empirical observation for this particular herb.

results.translator_notes: 1. SOURCE TEXT QUALITY: The provided source text is a brief modern summary label rather than a verbatim classical Chinese passage. This entry has been reconstructed and expanded from the primary classical corpus (Shennong Bencaojing, Bencao Gangmu, Jin Gui Yao Lüe, and Kampo secondary literature) to fulfill the Plantacopia schema requirements. The reconstructed original_text field gives the standard Bencaojing entry wording as preserved in extant recensions. 2. NATURE DISCREPANCY: The Shennong Bencaojing records the nature as 'ping' [平, neutral]; subsequent materia medica tradition (Tang, Song, Ming, and Kampo sources) consistently refines this to 'wēi wēn' [微溫, slightly warm], which better matches clinical application. Both readings are noted. 3. MODERN PHARMACOPOEIA STATUS: Shan Zhu Yu (Corni Fructus) is official in the Chinese Pharmacopoeia (2020 ed.), the Japanese Pharmacopoeia (JP), and the Korean Pharmacopoeia, attesting to continued pharmaceutical relevance. 4. SAFETY: Classical sources mark this herb as non-toxic [wú dú]. Contraindicated in patterns of interior heat or difficult urination; seed must be removed before use as it is considered to cause urinary retention.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Shan Zhu Yu 山茱萸

results.title_inferred: Shan Yao (Chinese Yam): Classical Materia Medica Entry
results.original_text: 山藥 — 薯蕷 (shǔ yù) — 脾、腎、肺 — 甘、平 — 補中益氣，長肌肉，久服耳目聰明，輕身不飢延年。
results.translation:
Shan Yao (Mountain Medicine / Chinese Yam; classical name Shǔ Yù 薯蕷)

**Chinese Name:** Shān Yào 山藥 (lit. 'mountain medicine'); classical designation Shǔ Yù 薯蕷
**Pinyin:** shān yào
**Japanese Kampo Name:** Sanyaku (山薬, やまいも / さんやく)
**Modern Identification:** Dioscorea opposita Thunb. (syn. D. polystachya; Chinese yam, family Dioscoreaceae)
**Classical Category:** Superior Herb [上品, shàngpǐn] — substances taken long-term without toxicity, said to prolong life and harmonize the body.

---

**Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]:** Sweet [甘, gān]

**Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]:** Neutral / Mild [平, píng] — neither warming nor cooling; considered gentle and restorative.

**Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]:** Spleen [脾], Kidney [腎], Lung [肺]

---

**Therapeutic Actions (Shennong Bencaojing tradition, c. 200 CE):**
'Supplements the center and augments qi [補中益氣, bǔ zhōng yì qì]; builds and strengthens muscle tissue [長肌肉, zhǎng jī ròu]. Prolonged consumption sharpens hearing and brightens vision [耳目聰明, ěr mù cōng míng], lightens the body, eliminates hunger [輕身不飢, qīng shēn bù jī], and extends the years [延年, yán nián].'

In the classical Shennong schema, Shan Yao is accordingly classed among the superior, life-nourishing drugs [養命之藥] taken continuously to cultivate vitality rather than to treat acute disease.

---

**Elaborated Therapeutic Actions (Post-classical & Kampo tradition):**

1. **Tonifies Spleen and Stomach [健脾益胃]:** Used for spleen-stomach vacuity [脾胃虛弱] presenting as fatigue, loose stools, poor appetite, and abdominal distension. Considered one of the mildest and safest spleen tonics.

2. **Supplements Lung Qi and Nourishes Lung Yin [補肺氣、養肺陰]:** Indicated for lung vacuity cough, chronic dry cough, and consumptive disorders [虛勞] with deficient lung fluids.

3. **Strengthens Kidney and Secures Essence [補腎固精]:** Used for kidney vacuity [腎虛] patterns manifesting as spermatorrhea [遺精, yí jīng], frequent or turbid urination [尿頻、小便渾濁], and lumbar weakness. Especially valued for its ability to 'consolidate and astringe' [固澀] without being overly drying.

4. **Nourishes Yin and Generates Fluids [滋陰生津]:** Relevant in wasting-thirst disorder [消渴, xiāo kě], the classical antecedent of diabetes mellitus, where Shan Yao addresses yin depletion with thirst, polyuria, and emaciation.

5. **Supports Correct Qi without Retaining Pathogenic Factors:** Its neutral nature makes it suitable for prolonged use and compatible with both tonifying and clearing formulas.

---

**Key Classical and Kampo Formulas:**

- **Liuwei Dihuang Wan [六味地黃丸]** (Six-Ingredient Pill with Rehmannia): Foundational kidney-yin tonic formula from the Song dynasty (Qian Yi, c. 1119 CE); Shan Yao supplements spleen and secures kidney essence alongside Rehmannia, Cornus, and others. Used in Kampo as Rokumi-jiō-gan (六味地黃丸).

- **Shenqi Wan [腎氣丸]** (Kidney Qi Pill; Zhang Zhongjing, Jingui Yaolue c. 220 CE): Shan Yao contributes to the yin-tonic base within this yang-warming formula. Kampo: Hachimijiō-gan (八味地黃丸).

- **Wan Dai Tang [完帶湯]** (Decoction to End Discharge; Fu Qingzhu, c. 17th c.): Shan Yao in large dose [重用山藥] tonifies spleen and transforms dampness to address leukorrhea [帶下] from spleen vacuity.

- **Shen Ling Baizhu San [參苓白朮散]** (Ginseng, Poria, and Atractylodes Macrocephala Powder): Spleen tonic formula for chronic digestive weakness and diarrhea; Shan Yao augments the spleen-supplementing action. Kampo: Jinrei-byakujutsu-san.

- **Yisui Wan / Yi Qi Wan variants:** Various longevity formulas from Daoist and court medical traditions, in which Shan Yao served as a primary life-prolonging ingredient.

---

**Preparation Notes:**
Classically used dried [乾山藥] or fresh [鮮山藥]. Stir-frying with bran [麩炒, fū chǎo] enhances the spleen-supplementing action and reduces cloying; raw form more strongly nourishes yin and generates fluids. Standard decoction dose in modern Kampo/TCM: 10–30 g dried rhizome. Large doses (up to 60–120 g) employed in certain formulas such as Wan Dai Tang. May also be taken as food-medicine [藥食同源].

---

**Translator / Compiler Note:** The name Shǔ Yù 薯蕷 is the original Shennong Bencaojing designation; Shān Yào 山藥 became the dominant term from the Song dynasty onward, reportedly altered to avoid a naming taboo [避諱] relating to imperial personal names. Both names refer to the same substance.

entities_mentioned.name: 山藥 / 薯蕷 (Shān Yào / Shǔ Yù)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dioscorea opposita Thunb. (syn. Dioscorea polystachya Turcz.; Chinese yam, family Dioscoreaceae); the edible rhizome is the medicinal part used.
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most securely identified classical materia medica entries. Consistently identified across Chinese, Japanese, and Western botanical literature. The name change from Shǔ Yù to Shān Yào is historically documented in Song-dynasty sources.

entities_mentioned.name: Sanyaku 山薬 (Japanese Kampo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dioscorea opposita / D. polystachya — same botanical source as above.
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard Kampo designation. Appears in the Japanese Pharmacopoeia (JP) as Sanyaku (山薬), defined as the cortex-removed rhizome of Dioscorea japonica Thunb. or D. opposita Thunb., dried. Dioscorea japonica is a closely related native Japanese species sometimes substituted in Kampo practice.

entities_mentioned.name: Liuwei Dihuang Wan 六味地黃丸
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound classical formula; not a single plant.
entities_mentioned.notes: Attributed to Qian Yi (錢乙, c. 1032–1113 CE), Xiaoer Yaozheng Zhijue. Foundational kidney-yin formula; Shan Yao is one of the 'three supplementing' [三補] ingredients.

entities_mentioned.name: Shenqi Wan 腎氣丸
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound classical formula; not a single plant.
entities_mentioned.notes: Attributed to Zhang Zhongjing (張仲景, c. 150–219 CE), Jingui Yaolue (金匱要略). One of the oldest extant TCM/Kampo formulas still in clinical use.

entities_mentioned.name: Wan Dai Tang 完帶湯
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound classical formula; not a single plant.
entities_mentioned.notes: From Fu Qingzhu Nü Ke (傅青主女科), c. 17th century. Notable for its unusually large dose of Shan Yao (typically 30 g) as the principal herb.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source segment provided only a brief label ('Plant: Shan Yao 山藥 — Chinese yam — spleen, kidney, lung') rather than a full classical Chinese passage; accordingly, this entry has been compiled and reconstructed from the Shennong Bencaojing textual tradition, subsequent Ben Cao commentaries (notably Li Shizhen's Bencao Gangmu, 1596), and established Kampo sources, following the instructed methodology. The 'original_text' field presents the core classical description in Chinese as it appears in the Bencao tradition. 2. The name taboo (bìhuì 避諱) explanation for the shift from Shǔ Yù to Shān Yào is recorded in the Song-dynasty Bencao Yanyi (本草衍義, Kou Zongshi, 1116 CE) and is widely accepted by scholars, though the specific emperor involved is debated. 3. Japanese Kampo distinguishes between D. opposita and the native D. japonica; both are accepted under the JP monograph for Sanyaku. Clinically they are used interchangeably in most contexts. 4. 消渴 (xiāo kě) is translated as 'wasting-thirst disorder' rather than 'diabetes' to preserve the classical diagnostic category while acknowledging the modern correlation; the two are not fully coextensive.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Shan Yao 山藥

results.title_inferred: Ze Xie (Water Plantain Rhizome): Classical and Kampo Materia Medica Entry
results.original_text: 澤瀉，味甘，寒。主風寒濕痹，乳難，消水，養五臟，益氣力，肥健。久服耳目聰明，不飢，延年輕身，面生光，能行水上。一名水瀉，一名芒芋，一名鵠瀉。生池澤。
results.translation:
Ze Xie [澤瀉; pinyin: zé xiè; Japanese Kampo: Takusha 沢瀉]. Rhizome of Alisma plantago-aquatica var. orientale (Samuels) Sam. (Family: Alismataceae).

**Classical Category:** Superior Herb [上品, shàng pǐn] — among the highest-ranked herbs in the Shennong Bencaojing, associated with longevity and non-toxicity.

**Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]:** Sweet [甘, gān]; some later sources also note a mild blandness [淡, dàn].

**Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]:** Cold [寒, hán].

**Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]:** Kidney [腎, shèn] and Urinary Bladder [膀胱, páng guāng] channels; later elaborations in the Kampo and Jin-Yuan traditions additionally note affinity for the Spleen [脾] and Liver [肝] in certain contexts.

**Classical Therapeutic Actions (Shennong Bencaojing, c. 200 CE):**
'Ze Xie: flavor sweet, [nature] cold. Governs [wind-cold-damp impediment [風寒濕痹, fēng hán shī bì]], difficult lactation [乳難, rǔ nán], disperses water-accumulation [消水, xiāo shuǐ], nourishes the five viscera [養五臟, yǎng wǔ zàng], augments qi and strength [益氣力, yì qì lì], and promotes robust health [肥健, féi jiàn]. Prolonged consumption sharpens the hearing and vision [耳目聰明, ěr mù cōng míng], eliminates hunger [不飢, bù jī], prolongs years, lightens the body [延年輕身, yán nián qīng shēn], imparts a luminous complexion [面生光, miàn shēng guāng], and enables one to walk upon water [能行水上, néng xíng shuǐ shàng — a Daoist immortality trope, not to be taken literally]. Alternate names: Shuǐ Xiè [水瀉], Máng Yù [芒芋], Hú Xiè [鵠瀉]. Grows in ponds and marshes [池澤].'

**Expanded Actions (Later Classical and Kampo Elaboration):**
1. Promotes urination and drains dampness [利水滲濕, lì shuǐ shèn shī]: The principal action; indicated for difficult, scanty, or painful urination, edema [水腫], and phlegm-fluid accumulation [痰飲].
2. Discharges heat from the Kidney [泄腎火, xiè shèn huǒ] / Clears heat in the lower burner [清下焦濕熱]: Used in patterns of kidney-fire flaring upward, manifesting as dizziness, tinnitus, and seminal emission with heat sensation.
3. Lowers turbid [turbid-lipid] accumulation: Later Kampo practitioners noted its use in conditions involving accumulation of turbid fluids, corresponding loosely to modern concepts of hyperlipidemia or fatty liver.
4. Harmonizes the Middle Burner and Spleen-Stomach damp stagnation.

**Key Classical Formulas:**
- **Ze Xie Tang [澤瀉湯]** (Jinkui Yaolue, Zhang Zhongjing, c. 220 CE): Ze Xie with Bai Zhu [白朮]; treats phlegm-dizziness [痰飲眩暈] caused by water retention in the epigastrium.
- **Wu Ling San [五苓散]** (Shanghanlun, Zhang Zhongjing): Quintessential formula for urinary obstruction and fluid metabolism disorder; Ze Xie is the principal [君, jūn] herb, paired with Zhu Ling, Fu Ling, Bai Zhu, and Gui Zhi.
- **Liu Wei Di Huang Wan [六味地黄丸]** (Song Dynasty, Qian Yi): Ze Xie serves as one of the three 'draining' [瀉, xiè] herbs, draining kidney-fire and preventing stagnation induced by the enriching herbs.
- **Zhu Ling Tang [猪苓湯]** (Shanghanlun): Used in yin deficiency with water accumulation and heat; Ze Xie contributes diuretic and heat-clearing action.

**Kampo (Japanese) Notes:**
In the Japanese Kampo tradition, Takusha [沢瀉] is standardized as the dried rhizome of Alisma orientale. It remains a key ingredient in Goreisan [五苓散], Rokumigan [六味丸], and Hachimijiogan [八味地黄丸]. Kampo pharmacognosy places particular emphasis on its diuretic [利尿, riyō] and anti-dizziness [めまい] effects, and clinical Kampo use frequently targets fluid metabolism disorders, hypertension with dampness patterns, and inner-ear fluid conditions (e.g., Ménière's disease analog).

**Preparation Methods:**
- Dried and sliced rhizome, typically decocted [煎, jiān] in water.
- Salt-processed Ze Xie [鹽澤瀉, yán zé xiè]: Light salt-frying directs the herb more specifically to the Kidney channel.
- Bran-fried [麩炒]: Moderates the cold nature and strengthens spleen-harmonizing action.

**Cautions:** Contraindicated in patterns of kidney deficiency without dampness-heat; prolonged high-dose use discouraged as later sources note potential for leaching essence [精, jīng].

entities_mentioned.name: Ze Xie 澤瀉 / Takusha 沢瀉
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alisma plantago-aquatica var. orientale (Samuels) Sam. (syn. Alisma orientale); Family Alismataceae; rhizome used medicinally
entities_mentioned.notes: Well-established pharmacognostic identity confirmed by Chinese Pharmacopoeia and Japanese Pharmacopoeia. The plant grows in shallow freshwater marshes and rice paddies across East Asia. Modern phytochemical studies confirm alisol triterpenes as key active constituents with demonstrated diuretic and lipid-modulating activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Wu Ling San 五苓散
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical Chinese formula (Shanghanlun, c. 200 CE); five-herb decoction/powder
entities_mentioned.notes: Ze Xie is the principal herb (largest dose) in this formula. Widely used in both TCM and Kampo for fluid metabolism disorders.

entities_mentioned.name: Liu Wei Di Huang Wan 六味地黄丸
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical six-herb formula, Song Dynasty origin, attributed to Qian Yi (钱乙)
entities_mentioned.notes: Ze Xie plays the 'draining' counterbalancing role to Shu Di Huang, preventing cloying stagnation.

entities_mentioned.name: Ze Xie Tang 澤瀉湯
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Two-herb formula from Jinkui Yaolue (金匱要略), Zhang Zhongjing
entities_mentioned.notes: Minimal but potent formula specifically targeting phlegm-dizziness; studied in modern Kampo for vestibular and inner-ear disorders.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source text as supplied is a metadata stub rather than a verbatim classical passage; the original_text field above reconstructs the standard Shennong Bencaojing entry for Ze Xie from the canonical Mori Tateyuki (森立之) recension and cross-references the Zhenghe Bencao and Bencao Gangmu for completeness. Scholars should verify against critical editions.
2. The phrase 能行水上 ('able to walk upon water') is a standard Daoist hyperbolism attached to superior-class herbs in the Bencaojing and should not be read as a pharmacological claim. It signals the herb's association with longevity cultivation [養生, yǎngshēng] discourse.
3. The 'difficult lactation' [乳難] indication is rarely invoked in modern practice and may reflect early classical usage of 'ru' (乳) in a broader sense encompassing fluid secretion generally.
4. Identification_confidence for Ze Xie is high; the Alismataceae identification is uncontested across Chinese, Japanese, and Western pharmacobotanical traditions.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Ze Xie 澤瀉

results.title_inferred: Moutan Bark (Mu Dan Pi): Classical and Kampo Entry on Blood-Heat Clearing and Stasis Resolution
results.original_text: 牡丹皮，味辛苦，微寒。主寒熱，中風，瘛瘲，痙，驚癇邪氣，除癥堅，瘀血留舍腸胃，安五臟，療癰瘡。一名鹿韭，一名鼠姑。生山谷。
results.translation:
Mu Dan Pi [牡丹皮, Moutan Bark]. Flavor: acrid [xin 辛] and bitter [ku 苦]. Nature: slightly cold [wei han 微寒]. Channel tropism [gui jing 歸經]: Heart [xin 心], Liver [gan 肝], Kidney [shen 腎] (as elaborated in post-classical and Kampo tradition; not explicitly enumerated in the Shennong Bencaojing proper).

Classical category [ben jing fen lei]: Middle herb [zhong pin 中品].

Primary therapeutic actions per the Shennong Bencaojing tradition: Governs [zhu 主] alternating sensations of cold and heat [han re 寒熱]; wind-stroke [zhong feng 中風]; convulsive spasm [chi zong 瘛瘲] and tetanic rigidity [jing 痙]; fright epilepsy [jing xian 驚癇] and pathogenic qi [xie qi 邪氣]. Eliminates [chu 除] firm abdominal masses [zheng jian 癥堅] and static blood lodged in the intestines and stomach [yu xue liu she chang wei 瘀血留舍腸胃]. Quiets [an 安] the five viscera [wu zang 五臟]. Treats [liao 療] abscesses and sores [yong chuang 癰瘡].

Alternate classical names: Lu Jiu [鹿韭, 'deer chive'] and Shu Gu [鼠姑, 'mouse dame']. Habitat: mountain valleys [sheng shan gu 生山谷].

Japanese Kampo name: Botanpi [牡丹皮, ボタンピ].

Kampo elaborations (post-Shennong, principally through the Jinkui Yaolue [金匱要略] and Kampo systematization): Mu Dan Pi is the principal herb for clearing heat from the blood aspect [qing xue fen re 清血分熱] without producing dryness, and for invigorating blood [huo xue 活血] and dispersing static blood [san yu xue 散瘀血]. It is distinguished from Huang Bai [黃柏] and Zhi Mu [知母] in addressing latent or steaming heat [gu zheng 骨蒸] arising from yin deficiency, particularly in the Kidney and Liver channels. It also cools ministerial fire [xiang huo 相火] in the Liver and Kidney.

Key classical formulas: (1) Gui Zhi Fu Ling Wan [桂枝茯苓丸] — resolves blood stasis, used for uterine masses and obstetric blood stasis conditions; (2) Da Huang Mu Dan Tang [大黃牡丹湯] — purges intestinal abscess [chang yong 腸癰], combining heat-clearing with stasis resolution; (3) Liu Wei Di Huang Wan [六味地黃丸] — nourishes Kidney and Liver yin, with Mu Dan Pi cooling ministerial fire; (4) Wen Jing Tang [溫經湯] — warms the channels and disperses cold, addresses gynecological cold-stasis patterns. In Kampo practice, Botanpi appears prominently in formulas addressing gynecological disorders, menstrual irregularity, and post-partum blood stasis.

Preparation: Dried root bark [gen pi 根皮] of cultivated or wild Paeonia suffruticosa. Decocted [shui jian 水煎] in standard herbal formulae; also used in pill [wan 丸] form. The outer cork layer is sometimes removed (termed 'scraped moutan' [gua pi mu dan 刮皮牡丹]) for reduced astringency. Raw form preferred for cooling blood-heat; dry-fried [chao 炒] or charred [tan 炭] form used to moderate cooling action and enhance hemostasis.

entities_mentioned.name: 牡丹皮 Mu Dan Pi (Moutan Bark)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia suffruticosa Andrews — root bark (Ranunculaceae / Paeoniaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most firmly identified classical herbs. The dried root bark of tree peony. Primary bioactive constituents include paeonol (丹皮酚), paeoniflorin, and galloylglucose derivatives. Well documented in both Chinese Pharmacopoeia (ChP) and Japanese Pharmacopoeia (JP).

entities_mentioned.name: 鹿韭 Lu Jiu (alternate name)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia suffruticosa — same plant, alternate classical designation
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical synonym recorded in both Shennong Bencaojing and Bencao Gangmu. Literally 'deer chive,' likely descriptive of leaf appearance.

entities_mentioned.name: 鼠姑 Shu Gu (alternate name)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia suffruticosa — same plant, alternate classical designation
entities_mentioned.notes: Second classical synonym. Literally 'mouse dame' or 'mouse matron.' Etymology uncertain; possibly folk nomenclature.

entities_mentioned.name: 桂枝茯苓丸 Gui Zhi Fu Ling Wan
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical Kampo formula (Jinkui Yaolue 金匱要略, Zhang Zhongjing, c. 220 CE)
entities_mentioned.notes: Composed of Gui Zhi, Fu Ling, Mu Dan Pi, Tao Ren, Shao Yao. Standard formula for uterine masses, blood stasis with heat in obstetric and gynecological contexts. One of the most frequently prescribed Kampo formulas in Japan today.

entities_mentioned.name: 大黃牡丹湯 Da Huang Mu Dan Tang
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical formula (Jinkui Yaolue)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classically indicated for early-stage intestinal abscess (appendicitis analog). Combines Da Huang, Mu Dan Pi, Tao Ren, Dong Gua Ren, Mang Xiao.

entities_mentioned.name: 六味地黃丸 Liu Wei Di Huang Wan
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical formula (attributed to Qian Yi 錢乙, Song Dynasty, elaborating on Jinkui Shenqi Wan)
entities_mentioned.notes: Archetypal Kidney-Liver yin nourishing formula. Mu Dan Pi serves to cool and move within a predominantly tonifying prescription. Widely used in both TCM and Kampo.

entities_mentioned.name: 溫經湯 Wen Jing Tang
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical formula (Jinkui Yaolue)
entities_mentioned.notes: Complex formula warming channels and dispersing cold-blood stasis; contains Mu Dan Pi alongside warming herbs (Wu Zhu Yu, Gui Zhi) to prevent excessive heat generation.

results.translator_notes: The source segment as supplied is a modern descriptor rather than a verbatim classical passage. The original_text field above reconstructs the canonical Shennong Bencaojing locus for 牡丹皮 from standard editions (Mori Tateyuki [森立之] reconstitution; Shang Zhijun [尚志鈞] critical edition), which is the appropriate primary source text for this entry. The translation and entry integrate both the Shennong Bencaojing stratum (c. late Han, compiled/attributed c. 200 CE) and subsequent Kampo elaborations, with the channel tropism (Heart, Liver, Kidney) representing post-Bencaojing systematization (formalized principally in the Ming-Qing period and adopted into Kampo). The Kampo name ボタンピ (Botanpi) is the standard Japanese pharmaceutical reading. Paeonol (丹皮酚) has been pharmacologically validated for anti-inflammatory, antipyretic, and antiplatelet aggregation activity, broadly consistent with classical indications. The distinction between 'scraped' and 'unscraped' forms (去心 / 不去心) noted in some editions refers to removal of the woody xylem core, not the cork layer — this variant reading has been silently normalized here to the more common pharmacopoeial convention.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Mu Dan Pi 牡丹皮

results.title_inferred: Di Gu Pi (Ground Bone Skin) — Lycium Root Bark: Classical Materia Medica Entry
results.original_text: 地骨皮 — 味苦，寒。主五內邪氣，熱中，消渴，周痹。久服堅筋骨，輕身不老。一名杞根，一名地輔，一名羊乳，一名地節。生常山平澤及丘陵阪岸。
results.translation:
**Di Gu Pi** (地骨皮, 'Ground Bone Skin') — the root bark of the Gou Qi [Lycium] plant.

**Flavor [Wei] and Nature [Qi]:** Bitter [ku 苦] in flavor; Cold [han 寒] in nature.

**Channel Tropism [Gui Jing]:** Enters the Lung [Fei], Liver [Gan], and Kidney [Shen] channels. In Kampo elaboration, primary affinity is recognized for the Lung and Kidney, corresponding to its action of clearing vacuity-heat [xu re] from the lower burner and nourishing yin.

**Classical Category:** Superior herb [shang pin 上品] in the Shennong Bencaojing classification. Regarded as non-toxic and suitable for extended use.

**Shennong Bencaojing Therapeutic Actions:** 'Governs [zhu 主] the evil qi [xie qi] of the five viscera [wu nei]; heat within [re zhong]; dispersion-thirst [xiao ke, corresponding to wasting-thirst/diabetes-like presentations]; and generalized painful obstruction [zhou bi]. Prolonged use consolidates the sinews and bones [jian jin gu], lightens the body [qing shen], and arrests aging [bu lao].'

**Alternative Classical Names:** Also called Qi Gen [杞根, 'Lycium Root'], Di Fu [地輔, 'Earth's Helper'], Yang Ru [羊乳, 'Goat's Milk'], and Di Jie [地節, 'Earth's Nodes']. Said to grow in the level marshes, hills, and slopes of Changshan.

**Kampo (Japanese) Name:** ジコッピ (*Jikoppi*); written 地骨皮. In Kampo medicine, Di Gu Pi is classified as a 'yin-fire' [陰火] clearing herb, targeting specifically the condition of yin-deficiency fire effulgence [yin xu huo wang], where heat arises from constitutional depletion rather than exogenous pathogen.

**Expanded Kampo Therapeutic Profile:** (1) Clears vacuity-heat and steaming bone disorder [gu zheng 骨蒸] — the sensation of heat radiating from within the bones, associated with pulmonary tuberculosis, chronic febrile illness, and late-stage yin depletion; (2) Cools the Blood [liang xue] and arrests bleeding, especially hemoptysis and epistaxis arising from heat forcing blood upward; (3) Clears Lung heat and stops cough — used in presentations of Lung fire with cough, wheezing, and blood-streaked sputum; (4) Generates fluids and alleviates dispersion-thirst [xiao ke], supporting its use in heat-wasting conditions.

**Key Classical Formulas [Fang Ji]:**
- **Di Gu Pi Tang** (地骨皮湯): Principal formula deploying this herb for steaming bone fever with night sweating and emaciation.
- **Qing Gu San** (清骨散, 'Clear the Bones Powder'): Contains Di Gu Pi as a core ingredient; used for yin-deficiency steaming bone fever, afternoon tidal fever [chao re 潮熱], and spontaneous sweating.
- **Xie Bai San** (瀉白散, 'Drain the White Powder'): Pairs Di Gu Pi with Sang Bai Pi [Morus root bark] and Gan Cao; a classical pediatric formula for Lung heat with cough and dyspnea. Attributed to Qian Yi (錢乙, c. 1032–1113 CE).
- **Di Gu Pi Yin** (地骨皮飲): Used in Kampo for heat diabetes patterns and yin-vacuity thirst.

**Preparation Notes:** Typically decocted [jian 煎] as dried root bark, 9–15 g per dose. The outer cork layer is sometimes scraped away before use. Not suitable for patients with Spleen-Stomach cold deficiency or without internal heat signs. Kampo texts warn against use in exterior [biao] conditions where heat has not yet entered the interior.

entities_mentioned.name: Di Gu Pi 地骨皮
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lycium chinense Mill. or Lycium barbarum L. — root cortex (root bark); family Solanaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The dried root bark of Lycium species, distinct from the fruit (Gou Qi Zi 枸杞子). Modern pharmacological research has identified betaine, kukoamine, phenolic acids, and flavonoids as active constituents. Anti-pyretic, hypoglycemic, and antioxidant effects are documented in contemporary studies, broadly consistent with classical yin-fire and xiao ke indications.

entities_mentioned.name: Sang Bai Pi 桑白皮
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Morus alba L. — root bark; family Moraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Co-ingredient in the classical formula Xie Bai San; also cold in nature and clears Lung heat.

entities_mentioned.name: Qing Gu San 清骨散
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical multi-ingredient formula; not a plant entity but included as a key formula reference. Contains Di Gu Pi alongside Yin Chai Hu, Hu Huang Lian, Qin Jiao, and others.

entities_mentioned.name: Xie Bai San 瀉白散
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical pediatric formula recorded in Qian Yi's Xiao Er Yao Zheng Zhi Jue (小兒藥證直訣, c. 1119 CE); among the most frequently cited formulas in Kampo pulmonology.

results.translator_notes: The source segment was a brief descriptor tag ('yin fire, fever') rather than a full classical text passage; the original_text field has been reconstructed from standard Shennong Bencaojing received text for Di Gu Pi, clearly flagged as such. The translation and entry expand upon this with Kampo elaborations drawn from the standard Kampo materia medica tradition (Honzō Kōmoku Japanese reception, Yakuchū Honzōkōmoku, and standard contemporary Kampo reference works). The term 'yin fire' (陰火) is a technical classical concept originating with Li Dongyuan (李東垣, Jin-Yuan dynasty); its application to Di Gu Pi in the Kampo tradition reflects the herb's specific indication for heat arising from yin deficiency rather than true excess fire — a distinction critical for clinical differentiation. 'Zhou bi' (周痹, generalized painful obstruction) in the original Shennong text likely refers to systemic painful bi-syndrome rather than a discrete modern diagnosis. 'Xiao ke' (消渴) is translated as 'dispersion-thirst' with a bracketed note, as it partially but imperfectly overlaps with diabetes mellitus.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Di Gu Pi 地骨皮

results.title_inferred: Chai Hu (Bupleurum): Classical Entry on Liver Qi Regulation and Alternating Fever
results.original_text: 柴胡，味苦，平。主心腹，去腸胃中結氣，飲食積聚，寒熱邪氣，推陳致新。久服輕身，明目，益精。一名地薰。生川谷。
results.translation:
Chai Hu [Bupleurum root]. Flavor: bitter [ku 苦]. Nature: balanced/neutral [ping 平].

Primary actions [zhu zhi 主治]: Governs the chest and abdomen; disperses bound and stagnant qi [jie qi 結氣] in the intestines and stomach; resolves food and drink accumulations [yin shi ji ju 飲食積聚]; expels cold-heat evil qi [han re xie qi 寒熱邪氣]; expels the old and generates the new [tui chen zhi xin 推陳致新].

Prolonged use [jiu fu 久服] lightens the body, brightens the eyes, and augments vital essence [jing 精].

Alternate name [yi ming 一名]: Di Xun [地薰, 'earth fumigant']. Grows in river valleys [sheng chuan gu 生川谷].

---
CLASSIFICATION SUMMARY:
• Chinese name: Chái Hú (柴胡)
• Japanese Kampo name: Saiko (柴胡)
