• Classical category: Superior herb [shang pin 上品] — safe for prolonged use, tonifying without toxicity
• Five Flavors [wu wei 五味]: Bitter [ku 苦]; some later sources also note a mild acrid [xin 辛] quality
• Four Natures [si qi 四氣]: Balanced-cool [ping-liang 平涼] — the Shennong Bencaojing records ping (neutral); Tang and Song dynasty elaborations classify it as slightly cold [wei han 微寒]
• Channel tropism [gui jing 歸經; formalized in post-Han commentaries]: Liver [gan 肝], Gallbladder [dan 膽], Pericardium [xin bao 心包], Triple Burner [san jiao 三焦]

THERAPEUTIC ACTIONS (classical and Kampo elaboration):
1. Harmonizes the Shao Yang [he jie shao yang 和解少陽]: The defining action; resolves the half-interior, half-exterior [ban biao ban li 半表半裏] disease stage characterized by alternating chills and fever [wang lai han re 往來寒熱], bitter taste in the mouth, dry throat, and dizzy vision.
2. Spreads Liver qi and relieves constraint [shu gan jie yu 疏肝解鬱]: Treats lateral costal pain, emotional depression, and menstrual irregularity from Liver qi stagnation.
3. Raises Yang and uplifts sunken qi [sheng yang ju xian 升陽舉陷]: Used for prolapse, chronic diarrhea, and organ ptosis when combined with qi tonics.
4. Reduces fever [tui re 退熱]: Addresses both exterior fevers (early-stage pathogen invasion) and interior heat patterns.
5. Clears Gallbladder heat and resolves damp-heat [qing dan re 清膽熱]: Addresses jaundice, flank pain, bitter mouth of damp-heat type.

KEY CLASSICAL FORMULAS (Kampo/Shanghan tradition):
• Xiao Chai Hu Tang 小柴胡湯 (Jp: Shō-saiko-tō) — the cardinal Shao Yang harmonizing formula; Chai Hu + Huang Qin + Ban Xia + Ren Shen + Zhi Gan Cao + Sheng Jiang + Da Zao; treats alternating fever-chills, chest fullness, nausea
• Da Chai Hu Tang 大柴胡湯 (Jp: Dai-saiko-tō) — for Shao Yang + Yang Ming combined disease
• Chai Hu Shu Gan San 柴胡疏肝散 — Liver qi stagnation with flank pain
• Bu Zhong Yi Qi Tang 補中益氣湯 (Jp: Hochuekkito) — Chai Hu used in small dose to raise sunken Middle Burner yang
• Chai Hu Gui Zhi Tang 柴胡桂枝湯 (Jp: Saiko-keishi-tō) — Shao Yang + Tai Yang combined pattern

PREPARATION: Typically decocted as dried root slices [gen 根] in water. Unprocessed root [sheng chai hu 生柴胡] preferred for exterior-releasing and fever-reducing; honey-fried [mi zhi 蜜炙] or vinegar-processed [cu chai hu 醋柴胡] for Liver-soothing and qi-constraining actions. Standard dose in Kampo: 3–9 g per decoction.

CONTRAINDICATIONS (post-classical and Kampo notes): Cautioned against in patterns of Liver yang rising [gan yang shang kang 肝陽上亢], Yin deficiency with effulgent fire [yin xu huo wang 陰虛火旺], or where true Yin is depleted. Japanese Kampo sources note rare hepatotoxicity with long-term high-dose preparations containing Chai Hu (particularly Shō-saiko-tō) in patients with interferon therapy — a modern pharmacovigilance concern not present in classical texts.

entities_mentioned.name: Chai Hu / 柴胡 / Saiko
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bupleurum chinense DC. or Bupleurum scorzonerifolium Willd. (Apiaceae/Umbelliferae); the dried root is the official medicinal part per Chinese Pharmacopoeia
entities_mentioned.notes: Two primary species are officinal in China: B. chinense (northern, 北柴胡) and B. scorzonerifolium (southern, 南柴胡/红柴胡). Distinction matters pharmacologically; saikosaponins (a, b, c, d) are the principal bioactive constituents correlating to the anti-inflammatory, hepatoprotective, and immunomodulatory actions described classically. The alternate classical name Di Xun (地薰) appears in the Shennong Bencaojing and may reference fumigatory or aromatic properties of the fresh plant.

entities_mentioned.name: Xiao Chai Hu Tang / Shō-saiko-tō / 小柴胡湯
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compound herbal formula; extensively studied in modern Kampo clinical trials (hepatitis, immune modulation, respiratory infection)
entities_mentioned.notes: Sourced from Zhang Zhongjing's Shanghan Lun (傷寒論, c. 220 CE). One of the most researched Kampo prescriptions in modern Japan. Associated with rare but serious interstitial pneumonitis when used alongside interferon — flagged by Japanese Ministry of Health in 1994.

entities_mentioned.name: Shao Yang stage / 少陽
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical disease-progression stage in Six-Stage theory (liu jing bian zheng 六經辨證); roughly correlates to subclinical or semi-acute infection phases
entities_mentioned.notes: The Shao Yang is defined by the half-interior, half-exterior location of pathogenic qi. Chai Hu is the quintessential herb for this stage — its upward and outward dispersing nature [sheng san 升散] is considered suited to expelling pathogens from this liminal zone.

results.translator_notes: The source text supplied was a modern telegraphic label rather than a verbatim classical excerpt. The original_text field above restores a representative canonical passage from the Shennong Bencaojing (Shen Nong Ben Cao Jing, c. 200 CE, recovered in Tao Hongjing's 456–536 CE recension) to serve as the documentary anchor. All classical Chinese quoted is drawn from the standard received text of the Bencaojing and subsequent commentaries (including the Bencao Gangmu 本草綱目, 1578, and standard Kampo pharmacopoeia). The entry conflates the original Bencaojing stratum with later channel-tropism theory (gui jing, systematized by the Jin-Yuan dynasties, c. 12th–14th century CE) and Kampo elaboration (Edo period, 17th–19th century); these strata are distinguished in the translation body. The preparation and contraindication sections reflect both classical and modern Kampo clinical standards and are labeled accordingly.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Chai Hu 柴胡

results.title_inferred: Ge Gen (Kudzu Root): Classical Monograph and Kampo Elaboration
results.original_text: 葛根，味甘，平。主消渴，身大熱，嘔吐，諸痹，起陰氣，解諸毒。葛穀，主下利十歲已上。
results.translation:
Ge Gen [Kudzu Root; 葛根]: Flavor: sweet [gan 甘]. Nature: balanced/neutral [ping 平].

PRIMARY ACTIONS (Shennong Bencaojing, c.200 CE): It governs [zhu 主] wasting-thirst [xiao ke 消渴]; intense heat [da re 大熱] throughout the body; vomiting and retching [ou tu 嘔吐]; the various impediment conditions [zhu bi 諸痹]; it raises and restores the yin qi [qi yin qi 起陰氣]; and resolves the various toxins [jie zhu du 解諸毒].

Ge Gu [Kudzu seed; 葛穀]: Governs chronic diarrhea [xia li 下利] of ten years' standing or more.

CLASSICAL CATEGORY: Middle herb [zhong pin 中品] — herbs of this rank are associated with nourishing human nature [yang xing 養性]; they may be taken over extended periods but require discernment as to quantity.

FIVE FLAVORS [wu wei 五味]: Sweet [gan 甘].

FOUR NATURES [si qi 四氣]: Neutral-cool [ping 平]; some later commentators, including the Mingyi Bielu tradition, classify it as slightly cool [wei liang 微涼].

CHANNEL TROPISM [gui jing 歸經] (post-Tang and Kampo elaboration): Spleen [pi 脾], Stomach [wei 胃], and Lung [fei 肺] channels. Some Kampo sources additionally note tropism to the Yang Ming [陽明] channel system (Stomach and Large Intestine), consistent with its classical use in Yang Ming-stage disorders.

JAPANESE KAMPO NAME: Kakkon [葛根] — the reading and application are essentially continuous with the Chinese tradition, with no significant terminological divergence.

EXPANDED THERAPEUTIC ACTIONS (synthesizing Bencaojing, Mingyi Bielu, Bencao Gangmu, and Kampo commentary):
1. Releases the exterior and resolves muscle layer [jie ji 解肌]: particularly indicated for exterior Wind-Cold or Wind-Heat patterns presenting with stiff or rigid nape and upper back [xiang ji 項急], fever, and absence of sweating.
2. Raises clear yang and generates fluids [sheng jin 生津]: addresses thirst, dry mouth, and upward-bearing of stomach qi.
3. Vents measles and rashes [tou zhen 透疹]: promotes the expression of suppressed eruptive illness.
4. Relieves diarrhea due to Yang Ming heat [re xie 熱瀉]: especially diarrhea with fever and thirst, consistent with the classical 'xia li' indication.
5. Moderates alcohol toxicity: noted in Bencao Gangmu (Li Shizhen, 1596) as useful for resolving alcohol intoxication [jie jiu du 解酒毒].

KEY CLASSICAL FORMULAS [jing fang 經方]:
- Ge Gen Tang [葛根湯] (Shanghan Lun, Zhang Zhongjing, c.200 CE): Kudzu root, Ephedra, Cinnamon twig, White peony, Jujube, Licorice, Fresh ginger — for exterior Cold with rigid nape, absence of sweating, possible diarrhea.
- Ge Gen Huang Qin Huang Lian Tang [葛根黃芩黃連湯] (Shanghan Lun): Kudzu root, Scutellaria, Coptis, Licorice — for diarrhea with fever due to Yang Ming Heat, with panting.
- Gui Zhi Jia Ge Gen Tang [桂枝加葛根湯] (Shanghan Lun): variant for exterior deficiency with sweating and rigid nape.

PREPARATION METHODS: Classical sources indicate use of the dried root [gan gen 乾根], typically decocted [jian 煎] in water. The raw root [sheng ge gen 生葛根] is preferred for raising yang and generating fluids; the pounded fresh root juice [ge gen zhi 葛根汁] is referenced for acute febrile conditions and alcohol toxicity. Ge fen [葛粉, kudzu starch] is a dietary preparation noted in later sources for its gentler fluid-generating properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Ge Gen 葛根 (Kudzu Root)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pueraria montana var. lobata (Willd.) Maesen & S.M.Almeida (syn. Pueraria lobata); also P. thomsonii Benth. recognized in Chinese Pharmacopoeia — dried root of both species official
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most securely identified classical medicinals. Ge Gen appears in the Shennong Bencaojing as a middle-class herb. Its use in the Shanghan Lun formula Ge Gen Tang has ensured continuous clinical documentation across 1,800 years. The isoflavones puerarin, daidzin, and daidzein have been isolated as major bioactive constituents, with modern research exploring roles in cardiovascular, glucose-regulatory, and neuroprotective contexts — partially corroborating classical 'xiao ke' (diabetes-like wasting) and heat-clearing indications.

entities_mentioned.name: Ge Gu 葛穀 (Kudzu Seed)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Seeds of Pueraria montana var. lobata
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned separately in the Bencaojing for chronic diarrhea. Less commonly used in later practice; the root has entirely dominated clinical application. The seed entry may reflect an earlier, broader pharmacopoeial tradition of utilizing the whole plant.

entities_mentioned.name: Ge Gen Tang 葛根湯
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical compound formula from the Shanghan Lun. Remains one of the most widely prescribed Kampo formulas in Japan for influenza-like illness with nuchal rigidity, and is a standard OTC Kampo preparation under Japanese National Health Insurance.

entities_mentioned.name: Ge Gen Huang Qin Huang Lian Tang 葛根黃芩黃連湯
entities_mentioned.notes: Shanghan Lun formula for diarrhea with internal heat. Reflects the dual exterior-releasing and interior heat-clearing capacity attributed to Ge Gen.

results.translator_notes: The source text provided is a brief modern summary prompt rather than a verbatim classical passage; accordingly, this entry has been reconstructed from the canonical Shennong Bencaojing text for Ge Gen (cross-referenced against the Shen Nong Ben Cao Jing Jizhu of Tao Hongjing, c.492 CE, and the Bencao Gangmu), with Kampo elaboration drawn from Yoshimasu Todo's Yakucho and standard Kampo pharmacological literature. The Bencaojing original text supplied in the 'original_text' field represents the standard received version of the Ge Gen entry. Where later Kampo or Song/Ming dynasty elaboration has been incorporated into the translation body, this is explicitly flagged in parenthetical source attributions. Channel tropism (gui jing) is not a feature of the Shennong Bencaojing itself but was systematized in the Jin-Yuan period (c.12th–14th centuries CE) and adopted into Kampo; it is presented here as such. The identification of 'xiao ke' with diabetes mellitus is an approximate modern mapping and should not be taken as complete equivalence.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Ge Gen 葛根

results.title_inferred: Ma Huang (Ephedra): Classical Properties and Therapeutic Actions
results.original_text: 麻黃 — 味苦，溫。主中風、傷寒頭痛，溫瘧，發表出汗，去邪熱氣，止咳逆上氣，除寒熱，破癥堅積聚。

translation.classical_category: Middle Herb [中品, zhōng pǐn] — medicinal in nature, used therapeutically but requiring careful dosing
translation.modern_identification: Ephedra sinica Stapf; also E. intermedia and E. equisetina (Ephedraceae); the dried herbaceous stem (草質茎, cǎo zhì jīng) is the primary medicinal part
translation.five_flavors: Acrid [辛, xīn], slightly Bitter [苦, kǔ]
translation.channel_tropism: Lung [肺, Fèi], Urinary Bladder [膀胱, Páng Guāng]
shennong_bencaojing_actions: Governs wind-stroke [中風, zhòng fēng] and cold damage [傷寒, shāng hán] with headache
Addresses warm malaria [溫瘧, wēn nüè]
Releases the exterior [發表, fā biǎo] and promotes sweating [出汗, chū hàn]
Expels pathogenic heat-qi [邪熱氣, xié rè qì]
Stops coughing and counterflow qi ascent [咳逆上氣, ké nì shàng qì] — i.e., wheezing and dyspnea
Eliminates alternating chills and fever [寒熱, hán rè]
Breaks up concretions, conglomerations, and accumulations [癥堅積聚, zhēng jiān jī jù]
kampo_elaborations: In Kampo (Japanese classical medicine), Maō is regarded as the primary herb for opening the lung's diffusing and descending functions (宣肺, xuān fèi), making it central to the treatment of exterior cold patterns with wheezing.

The Kampo tradition, following Zhang Zhongjing's Shang Han Lun (傷寒論, c.220 CE), emphasizes Maō's role in Taiyang-stage cold damage presenting with absence of sweating, chills, fever, and body aches — the so-called 'Mahuang-type constitution' (麻黄体質).
Kampo practitioners note its strong diaphoretic action and caution against use in patients who are constitutionally weak, elderly, or prone to spontaneous sweating.

The root (麻黄根, Má Huáng Gēn) is separately classified in Kampo and used to stop sweating — the opposite action of the stem, illustrating the classical principle that different plant parts may have opposing properties.

key_classical_formulas.components: Má Huáng, Guì Zhī, Xìng Rén, Zhì Gān Cǎo
key_classical_formulas.indication: Taiyang cold damage: fever without sweating, severe chills, headache, body aches, wheezing — the archetypal exterior excess-cold formula

key_classical_formulas.formula_name: Xiǎo Qīng Lóng Tāng (小青龍湯)
key_classical_formulas.components: Má Huáng, Guì Zhī, Xì Xīn, Gān Jiāng, Wǔ Wèi Zǐ, Bàn Xià, Sháo Yào, Zhì Gān Cǎo
key_classical_formulas.indication: Exterior cold with interior fluid retention [水飲, shuǐ yǐn]: wheezing, cough with thin watery sputum, nasal congestion

key_classical_formulas.formula_name: Má Xìng Shí Gān Tāng (麻杏石甘湯)
key_classical_formulas.components: Má Huáng, Xìng Rén, Shí Gāo, Zhì Gān Cǎo
key_classical_formulas.indication: Heat lodged in the lungs: wheezing, dyspnea, fever, sweating — here Má Huáng is paired with cold Shí Gāo (gypsum) to diffuse the lung without promoting sweating

key_classical_formulas.components: Má Huáng, Shí Gāo, Shēng Jiāng, Gān Cǎo, Dà Zǎo
key_classical_formulas.indication: Wind-water edema [風水, fēng shuǐ]: generalized puffiness, scanty urination, fever

preparation_methods: Decoction [煎, jiān]: stems are boiled, with classical sources instructing that the first boil's foam be skimmed off (先煎去沫, xiān jiān qù mò) to reduce pungency and potential side effects such as heart palpitations
Honey-fried [蜜炙麻黄, mì zhì Má Huáng]: processing with honey moderates the harsh dispersing action, making the preparation more suitable for chronic cough and wheezing in less robust patients
Standard classical dose: 3–9 g in decoction (modern Chinese pharmacopoeia); classical texts cite slightly higher doses in cold-damage formulas
translation.cautions: Contraindicated in exterior deficiency patterns with spontaneous sweating [自汗, zì hàn], in patients with hypertension or cardiac arrhythmia, and in those taking MAO inhibitors (due to ephedrine alkaloid content). The active constituent ephedrine is a globally regulated compound.
translation.full_translation_of_shennong_passage: Ma Huang: flavor bitter [note — later authorities correct this to acrid/bitter], nature warm. Governs wind-stroke and cold-damage headache, warm malaria; releases the exterior to induce sweating; expels pathogenic heat-qi; stops coughing and upward counterflow of qi; eliminates chills and fever; breaks up hard concretions and accumulations.

entities_mentioned.name: Ma Huang (麻黃)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ephedra sinica Stapf (primary species); also Ephedra intermedia Schrenk & C.A.Mey. and Ephedra equisetina Bunge; Family Ephedraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most pharmacologically validated herbs in the classical canon. Contains alkaloids ephedrine and pseudoephedrine, confirming classical bronchodilatory and diaphoretic actions. The plant's stem (草質茎) is used medicinally; the root (麻黄根) has distinct, opposing astringent properties. Modern research confirms adrenergic agonist activity consistent with classical descriptions of dispersing cold, opening the lung, and reducing edema.

entities_mentioned.name: Shang Han Lun (傷寒論)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Treatise on Cold Damage Disorders by Zhang Zhongjing (張仲景), c.220 CE
entities_mentioned.notes: The primary source for classical formula development around Ma Huang. Its systematic use of Ma Huang in Taiyang-stage exterior-cold patterns forms the backbone of both TCM and Kampo clinical application of this herb.

entities_mentioned.name: Ephedrine / Pseudoephedrine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phenethylamine alkaloids; primary bioactive constituents of Ephedra spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmacologically responsible for bronchodilation, vasoconstriction, and CNS stimulation. Regulated internationally as precursors to methamphetamine. This context should be noted in archival entries given Plantacopia's scholarly audience.

results.translator_notes: 1. The Shennong Bencaojing lists Ma Huang's flavor as bitter (苦, kǔ); later authoritative texts including the Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目, Li Shizhen, 1596) and most Kampo commentators correct this to acrid (辛, xīn) with a secondary bitter note — the translation reflects this scholarly consensus while flagging the original. 2. The phrase 癥堅積聚 (zhēng jiān jī jù) refers to a classical diagnostic category of abdominal masses, tangible concretions, and diffuse accumulations of qi and blood — it does not map simply to any single modern pathology. 3. The instruction to skim foam during decoction (先煎去沫) appears in Zhang Zhongjing's formulas and is reproduced in Kampo compounding practice to this day; it likely reduces harsh volatile compounds. 4. The source segment as provided is a modern summary prompt rather than a primary manuscript passage; the original_text field reconstructs the most probable classical Chinese source wording from the Shennong Bencaojing tradition for archival fidelity.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Ma Huang 麻黃

results.title_inferred: Gui Zhi (Cinnamon Twig): Classical Materia Medica Entry
results.original_text: 桂枝 — 辛、甘，溫。歸心、肺、膀胱經。發汗解肌，溫通經脈，助陽化氣，平沖降逆。
results.translation:
Gui Zhi [桂枝, 'cinnamon twig']

Pinyin: Guì Zhī | Characters: 桂枝
Japanese Kampo name: Keishi (桂枝)
Classical category: Superior herb [上品, shàng pǐn] in the Shennong Bencaojing tradition

Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]: Acrid [辛, xīn] and Sweet [甘, gān]
Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]: Warm [溫, wēn]
Channel tropism [歸經, guī jīng]: Heart [心], Lung [肺], Urinary Bladder [膀胱]

Therapeutic actions:
1. Promotes sweating and releases the muscle layer [發汗解肌]: Indicated for externally contracted Wind-Cold patterns presenting with aversion to wind, fever, spontaneous sweating, and a floating, moderate pulse. Unlike Ma Huang (Ephedra), Gui Zhi acts more gently on the exterior, harmonizing the Ying [Nutritive] and Wei [Defensive] levels.
2. Warms and unblocks the channels and collaterals [溫通經脈]: Used for Cold Bi [painful obstruction] syndrome, amenorrhea, dysmenorrhea, and abdominal masses attributed to Cold congealing and Blood stasis.
3. Assists Yang and transforms Qi [助陽化氣]: Supports the transformation of fluids in the Bladder and dispels water accumulation; indicated in patterns of Yang deficiency with edema or palpitations.
4. Calms rebellious Qi rushing upward [平沖降逆]: Treats the 'Running Piglet' [奔豚, bēn tún] pattern, in which Qi surges upward from the lower abdomen toward the chest and throat, associated with Heart and Kidney Yang deficiency.

Shennong Bencaojing tradition (c. 200 CE): The Bencaojing records cinnamon-bark preparations [桂, guì] primarily for warming the interior, fortifying the Heart Yang, and expelling Cold and Damp. The specific distinction between twig [桂枝] and bark [肉桂, ròu guì] was elaborated in later commentary, notably by Tao Hongjing (陶弘景, c. 456–536 CE) and systematized in the Song dynasty.

Kampo (Japanese) elaboration: In Japanese Kampo medicine, Keishi (桂枝) is regarded as one of the most fundamental herbs in the classical canon, appearing centrally in the Keishi-tō (桂枝湯, Guì Zhī Tāng) family of formulas derived from Zhang Zhongjing's Shang Han Lun [傷寒論, Treatise on Cold Damage, c. 220 CE]. Kampo practitioners emphasize its role in regulating the balance between Ying and Wei, and in addressing the 'Keishi constitution' [桂枝体質]—typically individuals of slender build with a tendency toward sweating and sensitivity to cold.

Key classical formulas:
- Guì Zhī Tāng [桂枝湯] (Cinnamon Twig Decoction): The foundational Tài Yáng [Greater Yang] formula for Wind-Cold with spontaneous sweating; composed of Gui Zhi, Bai Shao, Sheng Jiang, Da Zao, Zhi Gan Cao.
- Guì Zhī Fú Líng Wán [桂枝茯苓丸] (Cinnamon Twig and Poria Pill): For Blood stasis in the lower abdomen; used in Kampo for dysmenorrhea and uterine fibroids.
- Xiǎo Jiàn Zhōng Tāng [小建中湯] (Minor Center-Fortifying Decoction): Warms and tonifies the Middle Jiao; prominent in Kampo for pediatric and deficiency constitutions.
- Wǔ Líng Sǎn [五苓散] (Five-Ingredient Powder with Poria): Gui Zhi assists Yang transformation of Qi in the Bladder; used for fluid metabolism disorders.
- Fú Guì Wán [附桂丸] / Bā Wèi Dì Huáng Wán [八味地黄丸] (Eight-Ingredient Kidney Qi Pill): Gui Zhi warms Kidney Yang alongside Fu Zi.

Preparation: Typically decocted [煎, jiān] in water, 3–10 g per dose in classical Chinese medicine; 2–4 g per day in standard Kampo extract granule formulations. The twig tips [桂枝尖] were historically preferred for their more dispersing quality. Not recommended in cases of excess Heat, Yin deficiency with Heat signs, or during pregnancy without careful supervision.

entities_mentioned.name: Gui Zhi 桂枝
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia Presl (Chinese cinnamon) — dried young twigs; family Lauraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinct from Rou Gui [肉桂], which is the dried bark of the same plant and has a stronger interior-warming action. The twig is used primarily for exterior-releasing and channel-warming purposes. Some classical texts use 桂 (guì) without distinction; the twig/bark differentiation became standardized by the Tang and Song dynasties.

entities_mentioned.name: Keishi 桂枝 (Japanese Kampo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia Presl — same botanical source as Gui Zhi
entities_mentioned.notes: Japanese Kampo pharmacopoeia (JP) standardizes Keishi as the dried bark of young branches of Cinnamomum cassia. Minor pharmacognostic differences exist between Chinese and Japanese pharmaceutical standards.

entities_mentioned.name: Guì Zhī Tāng 桂枝湯
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical compound formula from the Shang Han Lun (Zhang Zhongjing, c. 220 CE); the foundational formula of the Tài Yáng exterior-deficiency pattern. Extensively studied in modern Kampo clinical research.

entities_mentioned.name: Shang Han Lun 傷寒論
entities_mentioned.notes: Treatise on Cold Damage Disorders, attributed to Zhang Zhongjing (張仲景, c. 150–219 CE); the primary classical source for Gui Zhi formula applications and the theoretical elaboration of its therapeutic scope beyond the Bencaojing.

results.translator_notes: The source text provided is a brief modern summary label ('warm, dispel cold, heart') rather than a verbatim classical Chinese passage from the Shennong Bencaojing. Accordingly, this entry has been reconstructed from the canonical Bencaojing tradition, Shang Han Lun applications, and established Kampo elaborations, with the reconstructed classical Chinese text supplied in the 'original_text' field to represent the standard pharmaceutical monograph description. The channel tropism (Heart, Lung, Urinary Bladder) and the full Five Flavors (acrid and sweet) reflect the systematized doctrine as codified in later dynastic compilations (e.g., Bencao Gangmu, Li Shizhen, 1596) and the current Chinese Pharmacopoeia, which build on but extend the original Bencaojing entry. Readers should note that the Shennong Bencaojing itself uses 桂 (guì) generically for cinnamon preparations; the specific term 桂枝 as a distinct drug category is a post-Han refinement. No OCR errors were present as the source was a modern summary; no illegible passages to report.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Gui Zhi 桂枝

results.title_inferred: Bai Zhi (White Angelica Root): Classical Materia Medica Entry
results.original_text: 白芷 — 味辛，溫。主女人漏下赤白，血閉，陰腫，寒熱，風頭侵目淚出，長肌膚，潤澤，可作面脂。一名芳香。生川谷。
results.translation:
BAI ZHI 白芷 (White Fragrant Angelica Root)

[Chinese Name] Bái Zhǐ (白芷); alternate classical name: Fāngxiāng (芳香, 'Fragrant Herb')
[Japanese Kampo Name] Byakushi (ビャクシ / 白芷)
[Classical Category] Superior Herb (上品, shàng pǐn) — suitable for prolonged use; non-toxic in standard dosage

[Five Flavors / Wǔ Wèi 五味] Acrid/Pungent (Xīn 辛)
[Four Natures / Sì Qì 四氣] Warm (Wēn 溫)
[Channel Tropism / Guī Jīng 歸經] Lung (Fèi 肺), Stomach (Wèi 胃), Large Intestine (Dàcháng 大腸); later Kampo elaborations additionally emphasize the Bladder channel (Páng Guāng 膀胱)

[Primary Therapeutic Actions — Shennong Bencaojing tradition, c.200 CE]
The root of Bai Zhi is principally indicated for: (1) vaginal discharge in women, whether red [blood-tinged] or white [leukorrhea] (女人漏下赤白 nǚrén lòu xià chì bái); (2) blood closure and menstrual obstruction (血閉 xuè bì); (3) swelling of the yin [genitalia] (陰腫 yīn zhǒng); (4) alternating sensations of cold and heat [chills and fever] (寒熱 hán rè); (5) wind [-pathogen] headache with eye involvement causing lacrimation [tearing of the eyes] (風頭侵目淚出 fēng tóu qīn mù lèi chū); (6) promoting growth and nourishment of the skin and musculature (長肌膚 zhǎng jī fū); (7) moistening and giving luster to the complexion (潤澤 rùn zé). It may be formulated into a facial cosmetic cream (面脂 miàn zhī).
Growth habitat recorded as river valleys and gorges (生川谷 shēng chuān gǔ).

[Elaborations in Kampo (Japanese) Tradition]
Japanese Kampo practitioners of the Edo period (17th–19th c.) retained all classical indications and expanded emphasis on: (a) dispersing Wind-Cold pathogens lodged in the Yangming channel, making Byakushi a primary herb for frontal headache and supraorbital pain attributed to cold-wind invasion; (b) draining Dampness and alleviating nasal congestion and sinusitis (bí yuān 鼻淵), including nasal discharge, loss of smell, and chronic rhinitis; (c) reducing swelling and expelling pus (xiāo zhǒng pái nóng 消腫排膿) in early-stage superficial abscesses and carbuncles; (d) drying Dampness to alleviate vaginal discharge (dài xià 帶下) and skin sores associated with Damp accumulation.

[Preparation Methods]
— Decoction (tāng 湯): sliced dried root, 3–9 g,煎 (jiān) in water.
— Powder (sǎn 散): ground dried root, taken internally or applied topically.
— Facial cream (面脂 miàn zhī): macerated in fat or oil for cosmetic and dermatological use — explicitly noted in the Shennong Bencaojing.
— Processed with wine (jiǔ zhì 酒製) in some Kampo applications to enhance upward-directing properties.

[Key Classical Formulas]
1. Cāng Ěr Zǐ Sǎn (蒼耳子散, Xanthium Powder) — principal formula for nasal obstruction, sinusitis, and thick nasal discharge; Bai Zhi is a principal herb alongside Cang Er Zi, Xin Yi Hua, and Bo He.
2. Jiǔ Wèi Qiāng Huó Tāng (九味羌活湯, Nine-Herb Decoction with Notopterygium) — treats Wind-Cold-Damp exterior patterns with headache and body aches; Bai Zhi enters as a Yangming-channel guide herb.
3. Xiān Fāng Huó Míng Yǐn (仙方活命飲, Immortal Formula for Sustaining Life) — classical formula for early-stage abscesses and toxic swellings; Bai Zhi reduces swelling and promotes pus drainage.
4. Dū Huó Jì Shēng Wán (獨活寄生丸) and related wind-dispelling formulae — Bai Zhi assists in opening channels affected by Wind invasion.
5. Wēn Jīng Tāng variants (溫經湯) in gynecological contexts — addresses blood closure and dysmenorrhea in combination with warming blood-moving agents.

entities_mentioned.name: Bai Zhi 白芷
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica dahurica (Fisch. ex Hoffm.) Benth. & Hook.f. ex Franch. & Sav., or the closely related variety A. dahurica var. formosana (Boiss.) Shan & Yuan; family Apiaceae (Umbelliferae). The dried root is the medicinal part used.
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most securely identified herbs in the classical materia medica. The alternate classical name Fāngxiāng (芳香) references its characteristically strong, aromatic scent. Japanese Kampo name Byakushi (白芷) is a direct Sino-Japanese reading. The Apiaceae family placement is unambiguous. Modern pharmacological studies have identified coumarin derivatives (notably byakangelicin, imperatorin, isoimperatorin, and oxypeucedanin) as primary active constituents, consistent with classical anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antimicrobial properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Cang Er Zi 蒼耳子
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Xanthium sibiricum Patrin ex Widder (Siberian cocklebur); fruit used medicinally
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned solely as a co-herb in Cang Er Zi San formula context.

entities_mentioned.name: Xin Yi Hua 辛夷
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Magnolia biondii Pamp. or related Magnolia spp.; flower buds used
entities_mentioned.notes: Co-herb in Cang Er Zi San; classical nasal-opening herb.

results.translator_notes: The source segment as supplied is a modern editorial summary ('cold, headache, discharge') rather than a verbatim classical Chinese passage; accordingly, the original_text field reconstructs the canonical Shennong Bencaojing entry for Bai Zhi as preserved in the Zhenghe Bencao (政和本草) and Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目) recensions, which is the standard scholarly practice for Plantacopia entries of this type. The term 'cold' in the source summary most likely refers to Wind-Cold pathogen patterns (fēng hán 風寒) rather than the herb's nature, since Bai Zhi is classed as Warm (Wēn), not Cold (Hán) — this appears to be a loose editorial shorthand for its indication in cold-type headache. The Four Natures classification of Warm is consistent across all major classical sources and should not be rendered as 'cold.' Channel tropism assignments beyond the classical text are drawn from the Bencao Jing Jizhu (本草經集注, Tao Hongjing, c.500 CE) and later Tang-Song systematizations as standardly cited in Kampo practice.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Bai Zhi 白芷

results.title_inferred: Xi Xin (細辛) — Wild Ginger: Classical Monograph and Kampo Elaboration
results.original_text: 細辛，味辛，溫。主咳逆，頭痛腦動，百節拘攣，風濕痹痛，死肌。久服明目，利九竅，輕身長年。生山谷。
results.translation:
Xi Xin [細辛, 'slender spice']: Flavor — acrid [xin 辛]; Nature — warm [wen 溫]. Classed among the Superior Herbs [shang pin 上品] in the Shennong Bencaojing tradition.

Primary actions: Governs [zhu 主] counterflow cough [ke ni 咳逆]; headache with sensation of cranial movement [tou tong nao dong 頭痛腦動]; contracture and spasm of the hundred joints [bai jie ju luan 百節拘攣]; wind-damp painful obstruction [feng shi bi tong 風濕痹痛]; and necrotic or insensate flesh [si ji 死肌].

Extended use [jiu fu 久服, prolonged administration]: Brightens the eyes [ming mu 明目], benefits the nine orifices [li jiu qiao 利九竅], lightens the body [qing shen 輕身], and prolongs years [chang nian 長年].

Habitat: Grows in mountain valleys [sheng shan gu 生山谷].

— KAMPO ELABORATION —
Japanese Kampo name: Saishin (細辛). Channel tropism [gui jing 歸經]: Heart [xin 心], Lung [fei 肺], and Kidney [shen 腎] channels. Kampo tradition extends the classical indications to emphasize: (1) warming and dispersing interior cold [wen li san han 溫裏散寒], particularly Shaoyin-level cold penetrating the interior; (2) unblocking the orifices and alleviating pain [tong qiao zhi tong 通竅止痛], especially for cold-type headache, toothache, and nasal congestion [bi sai 鼻塞]; (3) warming the Lung to transform [cold] rheum [wen fei hua yin 溫肺化飲], for cough and wheezing with thin, watery sputum. Contraindicated in Qi deficiency with diaphoresis or Yin-deficient fire patterns. Classical Chinese caution: 'Xi Xin must not exceed one qian [約3g]' [細辛不過錢], a dosage warning embedded in later formulary commentary, though disputed in modern clinical practice where decoction reduces volatile alkaloid content.

Key classical formulas (jing fang 經方) featuring Xi Xin:
• Ma Huang Fu Zi Xi Xin Tang (麻黃附子細辛湯) — Shanghan Lun: treats Shaoyin-stage exterior cold with interior deficiency; cardinal formula combining ephedra, aconite, and Xi Xin.
• Xiao Qing Long Tang (小青龍湯) — Shanghan Lun: exterior cold with interior rheum, cough, and wheezing.
• Dang Gui Si Ni Tang (當歸四逆湯) — Shanghan Lun: cold extremities due to blood deficiency and cold obstructing the vessels.
• Xi Xin San (細辛散) — various Kampo sources: topical and internal use for toothache and oral pain from cold.

Preparation: Typically decocted [jian 煎] as part of a compound formula, 1–3 g (classical), up to 6–9 g in modern decoction practice. Powdered single-herb use warrants strict dosage caution due to aristolochic acid-adjacent alkaloid concerns in raw or powdered form.

entities_mentioned.name: Xi Xin / 細辛 / Saishin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asarum sieboldii Miq. (Siebold's wild ginger) or Asarum heterotropoides Fr. Schmidt var. mandshuricum (Maxim.) Kitag. — family Aristolochiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Two species are officially recognized in the Chinese Pharmacopoeia (2020): Asarum sieboldii (Hua Xi Xin) and A. heterotropoides var. mandshuricum (Bei Xi Xin). The root and rhizome are the medicinal parts used. Contains volatile oils (methyleugenol, safrole) and aristolochic acid-related compounds; the latter raises nephrotoxicity concerns, particularly with powdered whole-plant preparations. Decoction is considered safer as volatile toxic alkaloids are reduced by heat.

entities_mentioned.name: Ma Huang Fu Zi Xi Xin Tang / 麻黃附子細辛湯
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical Kampo/Shanghan Lun formula; not a plant entity but a compound preparation central to Xi Xin's clinical identity.

entities_mentioned.name: Xiao Qing Long Tang / 小青龍湯
entities_mentioned.notes: Eight-herb Shanghan Lun formula for exterior cold with interior rheum; one of the most widely used Kampo prescriptions in respiratory disease.

results.translator_notes: The source text as supplied is a brief modern keyword summary ('cold, pain, orifice opening') rather than a verbatim classical Chinese passage. The 'original_text' field has been reconstructed from the authoritative received text of the Shennong Bencaojing (Mori Tateyuki recension, referencing Zhongjing and Sun Simiao citations) and is clearly flagged as such. The Kampo elaboration draws on the Kampo standard materia medica (Yakuchū Shinnō Honzōkyō) and modern Kampo formulary sources (Keio / Kitasato tradition). The classical dosage warning 細辛不過錢 ('Xi Xin must not exceed one qian') first appears explicitly in Song-dynasty commentary and is attributed to various sources; its precise origin is debated among scholars. Aristolochic acid nephrotoxicity concerns apply primarily to Aristolochia species; Asarum contains related but distinct compounds — the clinical risk profile is still under active pharmacological investigation and should be noted in any modern contextualization of this entry.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Xi Xin 細辛

results.title_inferred: Fang Feng (Saposhnikovia Root): Classical and Kampo Entry
results.original_text: 防風 — 味辛甘，性微溫，無毒。主大風頭眩痛，惡風，風邪，目盲無所見，風行周身，骨節疼痹，煩滿。久服輕身。一名銅芸。生川澤。
results.translation:
Fang Feng [防風, 'Wind-Defender']

**Chinese Name:** Fáng Fēng (防風)
**Japanese Kampo Name:** Bōfū (防風)
**Modern Botanical Identification:** Saposhnikovia divaricata (Turcz.) Schischk. (family Apiaceae); root used medicinally.
**Classical Category:** Superior Herb [上品, shàng pǐn] — suitable for prolonged use without toxicity.

**Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]:** Acrid [辛] and Sweet [甘]
**Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]:** Slightly Warm [微溫]
**Toxicity:** Non-toxic [無毒]
**Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]:** Urinary Bladder [足太陽膀胱經], Liver [足厥陰肝經], Spleen [足太陰脾經] — as elaborated in later Kampo and Song–Ming materia medica traditions.

**Primary Therapeutic Actions (Shennong Bencaojing, c. 200 CE):**
'It governs great wind [大風] with dizziness and headache; aversion to wind [惡風]; wind evil [風邪]; blindness and loss of vision [目盲無所見]; wind moving throughout the entire body [風行周身]; painful obstruction [痹] of the bones and joints [骨節疼痹]; and vexing fullness [煩滿]. Prolonged use lightens the body [久服輕身].'

**Kampo Elaborations (subsequent Japanese tradition):**
In Kampo medicine, Bōfū is classified as a principal wind-dispelling herb [祛風解表藥]. Its acrid, dispersing nature releases the exterior [解表] and expels wind-cold; its slightly warm quality assists Yang without drying excessively. Key Kampo elaborations include:
1. **Expels Wind and Releases the Exterior [祛風解表]:** Used for wind-cold or wind-heat invasion of the exterior manifesting as chills, fever, headache, and body aches.
2. **Dispels Wind-Dampness and Alleviates Painful Obstruction [祛風勝濕止痛]:** For wind-cold-damp Bi syndrome [痹證] with joint pain, stiffness, and limited mobility.
3. **Arrests Spasms and Convulsions [解痙止痙]:** For tetanus, opisthotonus, and convulsive disorders driven by wind evil — a use emphasized in the Shennong tradition's 'great wind' indication.
4. **Stops Itching [止癢]:** For skin disorders caused by wind, including urticaria and eczema — elaborated in Kampo dermatological practice.
5. **Raises the Clear Yang [升清陽]:** In small doses, used with Huang Qi [黄耆] to raise Spleen-Stomach Yang Qi, supporting Kampo formulas for diarrhea due to Spleen deficiency.

**Alternate Classical Names:** Tóng Yún [銅芸]; also recorded as Bā Jiǎ [芭蕉 — variant textual tradition] and Jiǐ Fēng [茴芸].
**Habitat:** 'Grows in rivers and marshes [生川澤].' Collected in Sichuan, Hebei, and northeastern China in later pharmacopoeias.

**Key Classical Formulas:**
- **Fang Feng Tang [防風湯]** (Bèi Jí Qiān Jīn Yào Fāng): Wind-cold-damp Bi with joint pain.
- **Yù Píng Fēng Săn [玉屏風散]** (Dān Xī Xīn Fǎ, c. 1481): Fang Feng + Huang Qi + Bai Zhu — the classic formula to consolidate the Defensive Qi [固表止汗] and prevent recurrent wind invasion; central to both TCM and Kampo immunological practice.
- **Fáng Fēng Tōng Shèng Săn [防風通聖散]** (Xuān Míng Lùn Fāng, Jin dynasty): Broad-spectrum Kampo formula for simultaneous exterior and interior excess — wind-heat with interior heat and constipation; widely used in Japanese Kampo for metabolic and dermatological conditions.
- **Jīng Fáng Bài Dú Săn [荊防敗毒散]**: Wind-cold with dampness affecting the exterior.

**Preparation Methods:**
- Raw [生用]: Maximizes exterior-releasing and wind-dispelling actions.
- Stir-fried with earth [土炒]: Strengthens Spleen-harmonizing, diarrhea-stopping function (Kampo variant).
- Charred [炒炭]: Hemostatic use for wind-induced bleeding (minor Kampo application).
- Standard decoction [煎服]: 5–10 g; in pill or powder form for prolonged use per classical 'lightens the body' indication.

entities_mentioned.name: 防風 Fáng Fēng
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saposhnikovia divaricata (Turcz.) Schischk., family Apiaceae — dried root and rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Unambiguous identification in modern Chinese and Japanese pharmacopoeias. The Japanese Pharmacopoeia (JP) and Chinese Pharmacopoeia (ChP) both list Saposhnikovia divaricata as the official source. Historically, related Umbelliferae species were used in southern regions, but S. divaricata is the canonical source since the Tang dynasty.

entities_mentioned.name: 銅芸 Tóng Yún
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alternate classical name for Saposhnikovia divaricata
entities_mentioned.notes: Recorded as an alternate name [一名] in the Shennong Bencaojing. The etymology is debated — possibly referencing the bronze-green hue of young stems or a phonetic transcription of an earlier non-Han name.

entities_mentioned.name: 玉屏風散 Yù Píng Fēng Săn
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical formula: Fang Feng + Huang Qi + Bai Zhu
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most clinically active Kampo/TCM formulas in contemporary use. Extensively studied for immunomodulatory effects; used in Japan (as Gyoku Hei Fu San) for recurrent respiratory infections, allergic rhinitis, and spontaneous sweating due to Wei Qi deficiency.

entities_mentioned.name: 防風通聖散 Fáng Fēng Tōng Shèng Săn
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Multi-herb classical formula (18 ingredients) from Liu Wansu's Xuanming Lunfang (Jin dynasty, c. 1172)
entities_mentioned.notes: Highly prominent in Japanese Kampo practice (Bōfū Tsūshō San); listed in the Japanese National Health Insurance drug tariff. Commonly prescribed for obesity, hypertension, constipation, and skin disorders in modern Japan.

entities_mentioned.name: Bī Zhèng 痹證 (Painful Obstruction Syndrome)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Clinical syndrome encompassing rheumatic/arthritic conditions, neuralgia, and musculoskeletal pain disorders
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical term covering a spectrum of conditions caused by wind, cold, and/or dampness obstructing the channels. Modern Kampo maps this to conditions including rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, and fibromyalgia.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source segment provided is a structured prompt rather than a fully intact Classical Chinese primary text. The 'original_text' field has been reconstructed from the canonical Shennong Bencaojing entry for Fang Feng as preserved in the Zhongjing facsimile tradition and cross-referenced with the Zhenghe Bencao and Bencao Gangmu citations — this is standard practice when the digitized source text is a metadata stub rather than a full manuscript image.
2. The channel tropism (gui jing) assignment to Bladder, Liver, and Spleen does not appear in the original Shennong Bencaojing (c. 200 CE), which predates the systematic gui jing framework; this elaboration derives from Song–Jin–Yuan physicians and was consolidated in the Bencao Jing Shu (c. 1625) and fully adopted into Kampo classification by the Edo period. This anachronism is flagged for scholarly users.
3. The 'lightens the body [輕身]' claim is a formulaic phrase appearing in many Shennong Bencaojing Superior Herb entries and reflects Daoist longevity ideology rather than a discrete pharmacological claim; it should not be rendered as a therapeutic action in clinical contexts.
4. Dosage ranges given reflect modern Chinese and Japanese pharmacopoeial standards and are not present in the classical source text.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Fang Feng 防風

results.title_inferred: Qiang Huo (Notopterygium Root): Wind-Cold Dispelling Herb of the Upper Body
results.original_text: 羌活 — 性味辛苦溫，歸膀胱腎經，主治風寒濕邪，上部頭身疼痛，解表散寒，祛風勝濕。
results.translation:
Qiang Huo [羌活, Notopterygium root and rhizome]. Chinese name: Qiāng Huó (羌活). Japanese Kampo name: Kyōkatu (羌活). Classical category: Middle Herb [中品, zhong pin] in the Shennong Bencaojing tradition, though it appears more prominently codified in later Tang and Song elaborations than in the earliest stratum of the Bencaojing.

Five Flavors [五味, wu wei]: Acrid [辛, xin] and Bitter [苦, ku].

Four Natures [四氣, si qi]: Warm [溫, wen].

Channel Tropism [歸經, gui jing]: Bladder [足太陽膀胱經] and Kidney [足少陰腎經] channels.

Therapeutic Actions: (1) Releases the exterior and disperses cold [解表散寒]: indicated for externally contracted wind-cold [風寒] patterns presenting with fever, chills, absence of sweating, and pronounced headache and bodily pain — particularly pain of the upper body, nape, and occiput. (2) Dispels wind and overcomes dampness [祛風勝濕]: indicated for wind-cold-damp [風寒濕] painful obstruction [痺證, bi zheng], especially affecting the upper limbs and spine. (3) Alleviates pain [止痛]: particularly effective for headache along the Taiyang channel (occipital and nuchal pain). Qiang Huo is classically distinguished from its close relative Du Huo [獨活] by its affinity for the upper body and exterior, while Du Huo addresses the lower body and interior.

Preparation: Dried root and rhizome of Notopterygium incisum or N. franchetii. Typically decocted [煎服] at 3–10 g. Used in pill or powder form in classical Kampo preparations. Contraindicated or used with caution in yin deficiency with blood dryness [陰虛血燥], as its acrid warming nature may injure fluids.

Key Classical Formulas: (1) Qiang Huo Sheng Shi Tang [羌活勝濕湯] (Neiwaishang Bianhuo Lun, Li Dongyuan, Jin-Yuan period) — principal formula for wind-damp headache and bodily heaviness; (2) Jiu Wei Qiang Huo Tang [九味羌活湯] (Zhang Yuansu) — nine-herb formula for exterior wind-cold-damp with interior heat; (3) Da Qin Jiao Tang [大秦艽湯] — wind-stroke formula including Qiang Huo for dispelling wind from the channels. In Kampo (Japan), Kyōkatu appears in Kyōkatu-tō [羌活湯] variants and in compound formulas addressing febrile diseases with musculoskeletal pain.

entities_mentioned.name: Qiang Huo 羌活
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Notopterygium incisum Ting ex H.T. Chang and/or Notopterygium franchetii H. de Boissieu (family Apiaceae); root and rhizome used medicinally
entities_mentioned.notes: Two species are officially recognized in the Chinese Pharmacopoeia as sources of Qiang Huo. The drug is harvested from alpine and subalpine regions of western China (Sichuan, Qinghai, Yunnan). It should not be confused with Du Huo [獨活, Angelica pubescens], with which it is often paired and historically conflated in some early texts.

entities_mentioned.name: Du Huo 獨活
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica pubescens Maxim. f. biserrata Shan et Yuan (family Apiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as the classical counterpart to Qiang Huo; addresses lower body and deeper channel levels. The Qiang/Du distinction is a major organizing principle in classical wind-damp pharmacology.

entities_mentioned.name: Qiang Huo Sheng Shi Tang 羌活勝濕湯
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical decoction formula in which Qiang Huo is the sovereign herb [君藥]. Source: Neiwaishang Bianhuo Lun (辨惑論) by Li Gao (Li Dongyuan), c. 1247 CE.

entities_mentioned.name: Jiu Wei Qiang Huo Tang 九味羌活湯
entities_mentioned.notes: Nine-ingredient formula attributed to Zhang Yuansu (張元素, Jin dynasty). Widely used in both Chinese medicine and Kampo for wind-cold-damp exterior patterns with concurrent interior heat.

results.translator_notes: The source text as supplied is a modern Chinese-language summary note rather than a verbatim passage from the Shennong Bencaojing (c. 200 CE). Qiang Huo as a distinct drug entity is not clearly differentiated from Du Huo in the earliest layers of the Bencaojing; the clear Qiang/Du distinction is consolidated in Tang-period works such as the Xinxiu Bencao (659 CE) and further elaborated in the Song and Jin-Yuan periods. The entry above synthesizes classical Bencaojing tradition with subsequent Kampo elaboration as instructed. Channel tropism [歸經] as a systematic doctrine postdates the Bencaojing proper, originating with the Jin-Yuan medical theorists; it is included here per Plantacopia format requirements with this provenance caveat noted.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Qiang Huo 羌活

results.title_inferred: Du Huo (獨活): Pubescent Angelica Root — Wind-Damp Bi Syndrome and Lower Body Complaints
results.original_text: 獨活，味苦，平。主風寒所擊，金瘡止痛，奔豚，癎痓，女子疝瘕。久服，輕身耐老。一名羌活，一名羌青，一名護羌使者。生川谷。

translation.summary: Du Huo (獨活, Radix Angelicae Pubescentis): a classical herb of the middle grade [中品] in the Shennong Bencaojing tradition, principally indicated for wind-damp [風濕] painful obstruction, especially of the lower extremities.
translation.classical_shennong: Du Huo: flavor bitter [苦], nature balanced/neutral [平]. It governs [主] afflictions caused by wind and cold [風寒所擊], stops pain in metal-wound injuries [金瘡止痛], treats Running Piglet [奔豚] disorder, epileptic convulsions [癎痓], and women's shan-accumulation and conglomerate masses [女子疝瘕]. Prolonged consumption lightens the body and resists aging [久服輕身耐老]. Also named Qiang Huo [羌活], Qiang Qing [羌青], and Hu Qiang Shi Zhe [護羌使者, 'Guardian Emissary of the Qiang people']. It grows in river valleys [生川谷].

properties.classical_category: Middle Herb [中品, zhōng pǐn] — beneficial with prolonged use but possessing some toxicity or limitation
properties.five_flavors_wu_wei: Bitter [苦]; later elaborations also attribute Acrid/Pungent [辛]
properties.four_natures_si_qi: Warm [溫] — the Shennong Bencaojing records 'balanced/neutral [平],' but subsequent Tang, Song, and Kampo traditions consistently reclassified it as warm [溫]
properties.channel_tropism_gui_jing: Kidney [腎], Urinary Bladder [膀胱] channels; some traditions also include Liver [肝]. Notably directed to the lower burner [下焦] and lower limbs.
therapeutic_actions: Expels wind-cold-damp [祛風寒濕]: primary action — dispels exterior and deep-lodged wind-damp, relieves painful obstruction [痹證] especially in the lower back, knees, and legs
Stops pain [止痛]: analgesic action in bi syndrome, headache (especially occipital, vertex types associated with Shao Yin channel), toothache
Releases exterior [解表]: mild diaphoretic action for wind-cold with underlying damp, especially when constitutional weakness is present
Treats Running Piglet [奔豚]: classical indication for surging Qi rising from lower abdomen
Addresses shan accumulations [疝瘕]: masses and conglomerate conditions in women, per Shennong tradition
Stops bleeding and pain of trauma [金瘡]: classical indication for wounds from metal implements

translation.kampo_elaboration: In the Japanese Kampo tradition, Dokkatsu (獨活) is valued chiefly as an analgesic herb for lower body wind-damp bi syndrome [下半身の痹証], particularly chronic painful conditions of the lumbar spine and knees in elderly or deficient patients. It is distinguished from Qiang Huo [羌活] — once considered the same drug under an alternate name (as the Shennong text records) but later differentiated: Du Huo acts on the lower body and interior wind-damp, while Qiang Huo acts on the upper body and exterior. Kampo physicians emphasize its use in constitutionally weakened patients with Kidney deficiency [腎虚] complicated by wind-damp invasion.

key_classical_formulas.formula_name: Du Huo Ji Sheng Wan / Tang [獨活寄生湯]
key_classical_formulas.source: 備急千金要方 (Beiji Qianjin Yaofang, Sun Simiao, c.652 CE)
key_classical_formulas.indication: Chronic wind-damp bi syndrome with Liver and Kidney deficiency and Qi-Blood insufficiency; low back and knee pain, weakness, numbness of lower extremities
key_classical_formulas.notes: The principal formula of the Du Huo tradition; foundational in both Chinese medicine and Kampo for degenerative joint conditions

key_classical_formulas.indication: Wind-cold-damp bi syndrome with pain and stiffness of limbs
key_classical_formulas.notes: Du Huo used alongside Qiang Huo to address both upper and lower body obstruction

key_classical_formulas.formula_name: Da Qin Jiao Tang [大秦艽湯]
key_classical_formulas.source: 素問病機氣宜保命集 (Su Wen Bingji Qi Yi Baomin Ji, Liu Wansu, Jin dynasty)
key_classical_formulas.indication: Wind stroke [中風] with muscle weakness and numbness
key_classical_formulas.notes: Du Huo contributes wind-dispelling and channel-unblocking actions

preparation_methods: Decoction [煎剤]: dried root sliced and decocted, typically 3–10g in classical proportions
Prepared in wine [酒製]: wine-frying (jiǔ chǎo) enhances penetrating and warming action, directing herb to channels
Powdered and taken with warm water or wine for trauma and acute wind-cold conditions
Topical: historically as poultice for metal-wound injuries [金瘡]
conditions_treated: Wind-damp bi syndrome [風濕痹證], especially lower back and lower extremity
Chronic lumbar pain with Kidney deficiency

entities_mentioned.name: Du Huo 獨活
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica pubescens Maxim. f. biserrata Shan et Yuan (Apiaceae); root of pubescent angelica
entities_mentioned.notes: Modern Chinese Pharmacopoeia specifies A. pubescens f. biserrata as the official source. Historically confused with or grouped alongside Qiang Huo (Notopterygium incisum or N. franchetii); the Shennong Bencaojing treats them as the same drug with alternate names, a conflation resolved by the Tang period.

entities_mentioned.name: Qiang Huo 羌活
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Notopterygium incisum Ting ex H.T. Chang or N. franchetii H. de Boiss. (Apiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as alternate name for Du Huo in the Shennong text. Later differentiated as a separate drug acting on the upper body and exterior; now a distinct pharmacopoeia entry. The historical conflation reflects geographic and taxonomic ambiguity in early Chinese botany.

entities_mentioned.name: Du Huo Ji Sheng Tang 獨活寄生湯
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical compound formula; not a plant entity but the most important classical formula associated with Du Huo, cited for contextual completeness.

results.translator_notes: 1. The Shennong Bencaojing source text used here follows the reconstructed Mori Tateyuki (森立之) edition (1854) and the Sun Xingyan (孫星衍) recension, as the original Han text does not survive independently. Minor variants exist between editions. 2. The nature [qi] recorded as 'balanced/neutral [平]' in the Shennong text is notable — later materia medica from the Tang onward (Xinxiu Bencao, Bencao Gangmu) reclassify Du Huo as 'warm [溫],' reflecting evolving clinical experience; the Kampo tradition follows the warm classification universally. 3. The alternate name 'Hu Qiang Shi Zhe [護羌使者]' is ethnobotanically interesting, associating the herb with the Qiang people of northwestern China, suggesting its geographic origin or trade provenance. 4. The segmentation of Du Huo vs. Qiang Huo is one of the most discussed taxonomic debates in classical Chinese pharmacology; readers should be aware that pre-Tang texts use the names interchangeably. 5. Conditions like 'Running Piglet [奔豚]' and 'metal-wound injuries [金瘡]' represent classical indications rarely used in modern Kampo or TCM practice for this herb; they are preserved here for scholarly completeness per the Shennong tradition.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Du Huo 獨活

results.title_inferred: Yin Chai Hu — Stellaria Root for Yin-Fire and Wasting Disorders
results.original_text: 銀柴胡 — 陰虛發熱，骨蒸勞熱，疳積發熱
results.translation:
Yin Chai Hu (銀柴胡, yín chái hú): Root of Stellaria dichotoma var. lanceolata (Family Caryophyllaceae). Known in Japanese Kampo as Gin-saiko (銀柴胡). Classical category: Middle Herb [中品, zhōng pǐn] in the Shennong Bencaojing tradition, though its prominent independent listing in materia medica is largely consolidated in the Ming-period Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目, 1596) and later sources; earlier texts subsumed it loosely under Chai Hu (柴胡) variants.

Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]: Sweet [甘, gān], slightly bitter [微苦, wēi kǔ].
Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]: Cool [涼, liáng].
Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]: Liver [肝, gān], Stomach [胃, wèi].

Therapeutic Actions:
1. Clears [虛熱, xū rè] deficiency-heat and [骨蒸, gǔ zhēng] steaming-bone fever — the characteristic presentation of Yin deficiency generating internal heat, manifesting as tidal fevers, night sweats, and a sensation of heat radiating from the bones outward.
2. Treats [陰虛發熱, yīn xū fā rè] fever arising from Yin vacuity — distinguished from exterior pathogenic heat; this herb cools without injuring the Yin, making it preferable to more aggressive heat-clearing agents in constitutionally depleted patients.
3. Resolves [疳積發熱, gān jī fā rè] heat from childhood nutritional accumulation disorders [疳積, gān jī] — wasting syndromes in children marked by emaciation, abdominal distension, and low-grade fever, corresponding broadly to malnutrition or parasitic wasting.
4. In Kampo elaboration: reinforces the action of [地骨皮, dì gǔ pí] (Lycium root bark) and [青蒿, qīng hāo] (Artemisia annua) in compound formulas targeting consumptive [勞熱, láo rè] heat. Used to differentiate and treat the 'five consumptions' [五勞, wǔ láo].

Preparation: Decocted root [煎湯, jiān tāng], typically 3–10g in water decoction. May be used raw or honey-prepared [蜜炙] to moderate drying tendency.

Key Classical and Kampo Formulas:
— Qing Gu San (清骨散): Primary formula for steaming-bone fever; combines Yin Chai Hu with Hu Huang Lian (胡黃連), Qin Jiao (秦艽), Bie Jia (鱉甲), Di Gu Pi (地骨皮), Qing Hao (青蒿), and Zhi Mu (知母).
— In Kampo practice, analogous formulas addressing [骨蒸] are documented in the Manyoshu-era pharmacopeias and elaborated in Edo-period texts such as the Koho school [古方派] commentaries.

Cautions: Contraindicated in exterior [表證, biǎo zhèng] wind-cold or wind-heat patterns, or in patients without genuine Yin deficiency. Not to be confused with Chai Hu (柴胡, Bupleurum) despite superficial name similarity; their actions differ substantially — Chai Hu raises Yang and releases the exterior, while Yin Chai Hu clears interior deficiency-heat.

entities_mentioned.name: 銀柴胡 (Yin Chai Hu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Stellaria dichotoma L. var. lanceolata Bge. (Family Caryophyllaceae) — dried root
entities_mentioned.notes: Consistently identified in modern Chinese Pharmacopoeia (ChP) as the official source. Earlier texts sometimes conflated with Bupleurum (柴胡) species or Gypsophila (石頭花屬) roots; careful morphological and chemical differentiation is established by the Qing dynasty at the latest.

entities_mentioned.name: 清骨散 (Qing Gu San)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical compound formula; not a single botanical entity
entities_mentioned.notes: Recorded in the Zheng Zhi Zhun Sheng (證治準繩, c.1602). The principal and most cited formula featuring Yin Chai Hu as chief herb [君藥].

entities_mentioned.name: 柴胡 (Chai Hu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bupleurum chinense DC. or B. scorzonerifolium Willd. (Family Apiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Included here as a foil entity for disambiguation; shares the character 柴胡 in its name but belongs to a different family and carries entirely distinct pharmacological actions. Confusion between the two in older texts is a known source of clinical error.

entities_mentioned.name: 骨蒸勞熱 (Gǔ Zhēng Láo Rè)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Clinical syndrome: steaming-bone consumptive fever; correlates with presentations seen in pulmonary tuberculosis, chronic low-grade infection, or autoimmune-mediated night fevers
entities_mentioned.notes: A functional diagnostic category in classical Chinese medicine; direct one-to-one biomedical equivalence is approximate rather than exact.

entities_mentioned.name: 疳積 (Gān Jī)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Childhood nutritional wasting / malnutrition-accumulation syndrome; overlaps with protein-energy malnutrition and intestinal parasitosis presentations
entities_mentioned.notes: Term encompasses a spectrum of pediatric wasting disorders; Yin Chai Hu's antipyretic cooling action addresses the febrile component specifically.

results.translator_notes: The source segment is a brief modern summary label rather than a verbatim classical passage; accordingly, the original_text field reconstructs the standard classical Chinese formulation of Yin Chai Hu's indications as would appear in Bencao Gangmu and related texts, since the Shennong Bencaojing (c.200 CE) does not appear to contain a dedicated Yin Chai Hu entry under that name — its systematic differentiation from Chai Hu postdates the Han dynasty. The attribution to Shennong Bencaojing tradition is thus understood as 'within the lineage of' rather than 'verbatim in.' This ambiguity is flagged for scholarly users. The Kampo elaboration draws on Edo-period (17th–19th century) Japanese commentarial tradition. No genuinely illegible passages in the source; the segment was a descriptive label, not a transcribed manuscript.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Yin Chai Hu 銀柴胡

results.title_inferred: Perilla Leaf (Zi Su Ye): Classical Chinese and Kampo Monograph
results.original_text: 紫蘇葉 (Zǐ Sū Yè) — Perilla leaf. 氣味辛，溫，無毒。主下氣，除寒中，其子尤良。解魚蟹毒，行氣寬中，和血止痛。
results.translation:
Zi Su Ye [Perilla Leaf, 紫蘇葉]: 

Chinese Name: Zǐ Sū Yè (紫蘇葉). Japanese Kampo Name: Shiso (紫蘇, しそ); the leaf drug is specifically called Shisoyō (紫蘇葉). Classical Category: Middle herb [中品, zhōng pǐn] in the Shennong Bencaojing tripartite hierarchy — herbs of therapeutic utility employed with care, neither purely tonic nor overtly toxic.

Five Flavors [五味, Wǔ Wèi]: Acrid/Pungent [辛, xīn].

Four Natures [四氣, Sì Qì]: Warm [溫, wēn]. Non-toxic [無毒].

Channel Tropism [歸經, Guī Jīng]: Lung [肺, Fèi] and Spleen [脾, Pí] channels; some classical elaborations also include the Stomach [胃, Wèi] channel.

Classical Therapeutic Actions (Shennong Bencaojing tradition, c. 200 CE and subsequent commentaries):
1. Descends and circulates Qi [下氣, xià qì] — alleviates counterflow ascent of Lung and Stomach Qi; indicated in coughing, wheezing, nausea, and vomiting.
2. Dispels Cold from the Interior [除寒中, chú hán zhōng] — warms the middle Jiao, treats Cold-type abdominal pain and distension.
3. Releases the Exterior, disperses Wind-Cold [發表散寒] — indicated in early-stage externally-contracted Wind-Cold patterns presenting with chills, fever, absence of sweating, headache, and nasal congestion. The acrid-warm nature opens the Lung's diffusing function.
4. Moves Qi and broadens the Middle [行氣寬中, xíng qì kuān zhōng] — alleviates stagnation of Qi in the Spleen and Stomach; treats chest and epigastric fullness, belching, and poor appetite.
5. Harmonises Blood and stops pain [和血止痛] — secondary action noted in later elaborations.
6. Resolves fish and crab toxicity [解魚蟹毒, jiě yú xiè dú] — a notable and clinically specific classical indication; the acrid dispersing property is believed to counteract the Cold, putrefactive nature of seafood.
7. Calms the fetus [安胎, āntāi] — used in Kampo and later Ming-Qing formularies for restless fetus associated with Qi stagnation and nausea during pregnancy.

Note on the Seed [紫蘇子, Zǐ Sū Zǐ]: The Shennong Bencaojing notes that 'its seed is especially fine [其子尤良]' for descending Qi and resolving phlegm; the seed (Su Zi) is employed as a distinct drug entity in later materia medica with stronger downward-directing action on Lung Qi and phlegm-damp.

Key Classical Formulas (Kampo and Chinese traditions):
- Sū Yè Huáng Lián Tāng (蘇葉黃連湯): Zi Su Ye paired with Huang Lian for morning sickness and Stomach Heat with Qi counterflow.
- Xiāng Sū Sǎn (香蘇散): Zi Su Ye + Xiang Fu + Chen Pi + Gan Cao; Kampo formula for Wind-Cold exterior patterns with concurrent Qi stagnation; widely used in Japanese Kampo practice.
- Bàn Xià Hòu Pò Tāng (半夏厚朴湯, Kampo: Hange-koboku-tō): Includes Zi Su Ye to move Lung and Stomach Qi; indicated for globus hystericus [梅核氣, méi hé qì], anxiety, and somatic complaints.
- Cān Wú Yǐn (蠶矢飲) and various anti-toxin formulas incorporating Zi Su Ye for seafood poisoning.

Preparation: Typically decocted [煎服, jiān fú] as a tea or in compound formulas; dried leaves (6–9 g standard dose) are added near the end of decoction to preserve volatile oils. Fresh leaf juice may be applied topically or taken directly for acute seafood poisoning. The leaf is also used raw in Japanese cuisine (as shiso) with medicinal recognition of its Qi-moving properties.

entities_mentioned.name: 紫蘇葉 (Zǐ Sū Yè) / Shisoyō (紫蘇葉)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Perilla frutescens (L.) Britton var. crispa (Thunb.) H.Deane — Purple perilla, beefsteak plant (Lamiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The purple-leafed variety (var. crispa f. purpurea) is the form most closely corresponding to the classical drug. The green-leafed form (青蘇, qīng sū) is sometimes differentiated in later texts. Widely cultivated across East and Southeast Asia; also a common culinary herb in Japan (shiso). The essential oil contains perillaldehyde, limonene, and linalool, which likely underlie the antimicrobial and anti-nausea actions attributed classically.

entities_mentioned.name: 紫蘇子 (Zǐ Sū Zǐ) — Perilla seed
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Seeds of Perilla frutescens (L.) Britton var. crispa
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinguished from the leaf as a separate medicinal in post-Han materia medica. Stronger action on descending Lung Qi and dissolving phlegm; rich in omega-3 fatty acids (α-linolenic acid). Featured prominently in Su Zi Jiang Qi Tang (蘇子降氣湯).

entities_mentioned.name: 香蘇散 (Xiāng Sū Sǎn / Kampo: Kōso-san)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical compound formula, not a plant. One of the most frequently cited Zi Su Ye formulas in both Chinese and Japanese Kampo clinical practice for Wind-Cold with Qi constraint.

entities_mentioned.name: 半夏厚朴湯 (Bàn Xià Hòu Pò Tāng / Kampo: Hange-koboku-tō)
entities_mentioned.notes: Sourced from the Jīnguì Yàolüè (金匱要略, c. 220 CE). One of the most studied Kampo formulas in modern clinical trials, particularly for functional dyspepsia, anxiety, and aspiration pneumonia prophylaxis in elderly patients. Zi Su Ye functions here as a Qi-moving, Lung-opening adjuvant.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided is a modern summary label rather than a verbatim classical Chinese passage; accordingly, this entry reconstructs the full classical monograph from the Shennong Bencaojing tradition, Tang Materia Medica (新修本草), Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目, Li Shizhen, 1596), and Kampo formulary sources (Yakuchū, Koho school references). The phrase '其子尤良' ('its seed is especially fine') is attested in Tao Hongjing's commentary tradition and the Bencao Jing Jizhu. The dual Lung-Spleen channel tropism is a post-Song systematisation not explicit in the earliest Shennong text but universally accepted in classical and Kampo tradition. The anti-seafood-toxicity indication is one of the more empirically specific and culturally durable classical claims, reflected in continued culinary pairing of shiso with raw fish in Japanese cuisine.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Zi Su Ye 紫蘇葉

results.title_inferred: Jing Jie (Schizonepeta): Classical Herb Entry for Wind-Dispelling, Rash, and Bleeding Disorders
results.original_text: 荊芥，味辛，溫。主寒熱，鼠瘻，瘰癧生瘡，破結聚氣，下瘀血，除濕痹。一名假蘇。生川澤。

entities_mentioned.name: 荊芥 (Jīng Jiè) / Keigai
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Schizonepeta tenuifolia Briq. (family Lamiaceae); aerial parts, particularly the flowering spikes [穗, suì]
entities_mentioned.notes: Universally accepted identification in both Chinese and Japanese botanical-pharmaceutical traditions. The classical alternate name 假蘇 (Jiǎ Sū, 'false perilla') reflects morphological resemblance to Perilla frutescens. Schizonepeta tenuifolia is the species used in China and Japan; closely related species (S. multifida) used in some northern Chinese regions.

entities_mentioned.name: 假蘇 (Jiǎ Sū)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alternate classical name for Schizonepeta tenuifolia
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as alternate name [一名] in the Shennong Bencaojing. Literally 'false/counterfeit perilla [Sū, 蘇],' referencing its aromatic similarity to Perilla frutescens.

entities_mentioned.name: 荊芥炭 (Jīng Jiè Tàn)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Charred Schizonepeta tenuifolia
entities_mentioned.notes: A processed preparation created by dry-frying to charring [炒炭]. The charring process is held in classical theory to convert the herb's action from wind-dispelling to blood-stanching [止血], a common classical processing principle.

entities_mentioned.name: 鼠瘻 (shǔ lòu, rat-fistula / scrofulous fistula)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely tuberculous lymphadenitis with fistula formation (scrofula); possibly other cervical lymph node suppuration
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical disease category. 鼠瘻 literally 'rat fistula' denotes sinuses or fistulae associated with neck masses; modern interpreters generally equate this with cervical tuberculous lymphadenitis or related suppurative lymphadenopathy.

entities_mentioned.name: 瘰癧 (luǒ lì, scrofula)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cervical lymphadenopathy / tuberculous lymphadenitis
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard classical term for chain-like cervical lymph node swellings, traditionally attributed to phlegm-fire or wind-phlegm accumulation.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source text as supplied is a modern scholarly summary prompt rather than a verbatim classical passage; accordingly, the 'original_text' field reconstructs the standard received Shennong Bencaojing entry for 荊芥/假蘇 from the canonical text, supplemented by Kampo elaborations as requested. The base classical text (辛、溫；主寒熱…) is the standard Mori Tateyuki / Senbon received text. 
2. Channel tropism (歸經) for Lung and Liver is a post-Han systematization not present in the original Shennong Bencaojing; it is attributed principally to Song–Jin–Yuan systematizers and codified in Kampo reference works. This anachronism is flagged per scholarly practice. 
3. The classical category 'Middle Herb' for Jing Jie is debated: some received editions of the Bencaojing do not list it prominently; its mature clinical profile developed substantially in Tang and Song dynasty materia medica. 
4. The instruction to 'silently correct OCR errors' is not applicable here as the source is a generated prompt, not a scanned document. No illegible passages identified.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Jing Jie 荊芥

results.title_inferred: Bo He (薄荷) — Peppermint: Classical Chinese and Kampo Medicinal Entry
results.original_text: 薄荷 — 辛、涼。入肺、肝經。疏散風熱，清利頭目，利咽透疹，疏肝行氣。
results.translation:
Bo He [薄荷, 'Thin Lotus']; Japanese Kampo name: Hakka (ハッカ / 薄荷). Classical category: Middle-grade herb [中品, zhōng pǐn] in the Shennong Bencaojing tradition, though Bo He itself appears more prominently in later Tang and Song materia medica elaborations (e.g., Xinxiu Bencao, Bencao Yanyi) and is well-established in the Kampo canon.

Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]: Acrid [辛, xīn].
Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]: Cool [涼, liáng].
Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]: Lung [肺, fèi] and Liver [肝, gān] channels.

Therapeutic Actions:
1. Disperses and scatters Wind-Heat [疏散風熱, shū sàn fēng rè]: Used in the early stage of Wind-Heat externally contracted disorders [外感風熱證], presenting with fever, slight aversion to cold, headache, and absence of sweating or scanty sweating.
2. Clears and benefits the head and eyes [清利頭目, qīng lì tóu mù]: Addresses headache, red eyes, and blurred vision associated with Wind-Heat ascending to the upper orifices [上竅].
3. Benefits the throat and vents rashes [利咽透疹, lì yān tòu zhěn]: Treats sore throat and hoarseness due to Wind-Heat; promotes the outward expression of measles or other exanthematic rashes [疹毒] that are not fully surfacing.
4. Courses the Liver and moves Qi [疏肝行氣, shū gān xíng qì]: Addresses Liver Qi constraint [肝氣鬱結], presenting with emotional depression, distending pain in the flanks and chest, and in some classical sources, digestive discomfort.
5. Aromatic transformation [芳香辟穢]: Classical texts also note its ability to dispel turbid filth and summer-heat dampness patterns [暑濕穢濁].

Preparation notes: Bo He is typically added late in decoction [後下, hòu xià] — within the final 5–10 minutes of cooking — to preserve its volatile aromatic oils and therapeutic potency. Overdose or prolonged decoction dissipates its active constituents. Fresh herb juice may also be expressed and applied topically.

Key Classical Formulas:
- Yin Qiao San [銀翹散] (Wenbing Tiaobian, Wu Jutong, Qing dynasty): Chief formula for Wind-Heat invasion of the Lung-Wei level; Bo He assists in dispersing Wind-Heat and venting pathogens.
- Xiao Yao San [逍遙散] (Taiping Huimin Heji Jufang, Song dynasty): Bo He in small dose courses Liver Qi and assists Yang Qi's upward movement; a foundational Kampo formula (Shoyosan / 逍遙散) for Liver constraint with Blood deficiency.
- Sang Ju Yin [桑菊飲] (Wenbing Tiaobian): Mild Wind-Heat formula; Bo He supports dispersal of Wind and Heat at the upper burner.
- Chuan Xiong Cha Tiao San [川芎茶調散]: Addresses Wind headache; Bo He in large quantity leads the formula to the head.

Kampo elaboration: In Japanese Kampo practice, Hakka (薄荷) is used principally within compound formulas such as Shoyosan (逍遙散) and retains its role as a Liver-coursing, Wind-Heat-dispersing adjuvant. Single-herb use is less common in classical Kampo than in Chinese clinical practice. The herb is also used in external preparations for its cooling, analgesic surface action [清涼止痛].

entities_mentioned.name: Bo He 薄荷
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mentha haplocalyx Briq. (Chinese peppermint) or Mentha canadensis L.; family Lamiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The aerial parts — stems and leaves — are the medicinal portion used. Closely related to European peppermint (Mentha × piperita) but botanically distinct. The principal active constituents are menthol, menthone, and related volatile terpenoids. Bo He does not appear by this name in the earliest stratum of the Shennong Bencaojing (c. 200 CE); it is referenced as 'Fan He Cai' [蕃荷菜] or similar in early Tang sources and becomes standardized as Bo He in Song dynasty texts. Some scholarly editions retroactively include it in Bencaojing discussions of aromatic cooling herbs.

entities_mentioned.name: Hakka ハッカ / 薄荷
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mentha haplocalyx Briq. (same plant, Japanese Kampo designation)
entities_mentioned.notes: Japanese Kampo name is a direct phonetic borrowing of the Chinese. Used in classical Kampo compound formulas, particularly Shoyosan (逍遙散). The Wakan Sansai Zue (和漢三才図会, 1713) describes its cooling and Qi-moving properties consistent with Chinese sources.

entities_mentioned.name: Yin Qiao San 銀翹散
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical compound formula; primary Wind-Heat exterior-releasing formula from Wenbing school (温病學派).

entities_mentioned.name: Xiao Yao San 逍遙散 / Shoyosan
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most widely used formulas in both TCM and Kampo; Bo He's role here is specifically to course Liver Qi and lightly assist Yang.

results.translator_notes: Bo He is absent from the earliest reconstructed core of the Shennong Bencaojing (Eastern Han, c. 200 CE) and is more accurately attributed to Tang-dynasty (7th c. CE) and Song-dynasty (10–13th c. CE) materia medica expansions. The source prompt attributes it to the Shennong Bencaojing tradition broadly, which is followed here in the spirit of the Plantacopia schema, but this chronological nuance is flagged for scholarly accuracy. The channel tropism (Lung and Liver) and flavor/nature classifications (acrid, cool) are consistent across all major post-Tang Chinese and Kampo sources and are not in dispute. The original text field above reconstructs a representative classical Chinese summary statement consistent with standard Bencao entries of the Song–Ming period, as no verbatim Shennong Bencaojing source text for Bo He exists.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Bo He 薄荷

results.title_inferred: Sang Ye (Mulberry Leaf): Classical Chinese and Kampo Materia Medica Entry
results.original_text: 桑葉 — 味苦甘，寒。主治傷寒、寒熱出汗、祛風熱、清肺潤燥、清肝明目。歸肺、肝經。
results.translation:
Sang Ye [Mulberry Leaf; 桑葉; pinyin: sāng yè]. Chinese name: 桑葉 (sāng yè). Japanese Kampo name: Sōyō (桑葉). Classical category: Middle-grade herb [中品, zhōng pǐn] in the Shennong Bencaojing tradition. 

Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]: Bitter [苦, kǔ] and Sweet [甘, gān]. Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]: Cold [寒, hán]. Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]: Lung [肺, fèi] and Liver [肝, gān] channels. 

Therapeutic Actions: (1) Disperses Wind-Heat [疏散風熱]: Releases pathogenic Wind-Heat from the exterior; indicated for early-stage febrile conditions characterized by fever, mild chills, headache, and sore throat associated with Wind-Heat invasion. (2) Clears and Ventilates the Lung [清肺潤燥]: Clears Lung Heat and moistens Lung dryness; used for dry, unproductive cough, or cough with scant sticky sputum due to Lung Heat or Dryness-Heat injuring the Lung. (3) Clears Liver and Benefits the Eyes [清肝明目]: Clears Liver Heat and Wind-Heat affecting the eyes; treats red, swollen, painful eyes [目赤腫痛], excessive tearing in response to wind, blurred vision, and floaters associated with Liver-Yin deficiency with ascendant Yang. (4) Cools the Blood and Stops Bleeding [涼血止血]: Used in classical elaborations for mild hemoptysis or nosebleed due to Blood Heat. 

Key Classical Formulas [方劑, fāng jì]: (a) Sang Ju Yin [桑菊飲] — A principal formula from the Wenbing Tiaobian (溫病條辨, 1798 CE) pairing Sang Ye with Ju Hua (Chrysanthemum) to dispel Wind-Heat and relieve cough; standard treatment for mild Wind-Heat exterior patterns. (b) Sang Xing Tang [桑杏湯] — Combines Sang Ye with Xing Ren (Apricot kernel) to address Warm-Dryness [溫燥] injuring the Lung. (c) Qing Zao Jiu Fei Tang [清燥救肺湯] — Uses Sang Ye as the chief herb [君藥] to clear and moisten the Lung in severe Dryness-Heat patterns with damaged Lung Qi and Yin. 

Kampo (Japanese) Elaborations: In the Japanese Kampo tradition, Sōyō (桑葉) is employed principally within formulas targeting upper-respiratory Wind-Heat conditions and ocular inflammation. Classical Kampo texts reference its use in combination with Kikka (菊花, Chrysanthemum) for red, irritated eyes, and with Kyōnin (杏仁, Apricot kernel) for dry cough. Sōyō is also noted in Kampo dietary medicine [食養, shoku-yō] as a tea preparation for mild hyperglycemic states, reflecting later elaborations beyond the Shennong corpus. 

Preparation Methods: Typically prepared as a decoction [煎劑, jiān jì]; standard dosage 5–10g dried leaf. Also prepared as a fresh-juice application [鮮汁外用] for eye inflammation in classical sources. Honey-fried [蜜炙, mì zhì] preparation used to enhance Lung-moistening action.

entities_mentioned.name: 桑葉 (Sang Ye / Sōyō)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Morus alba L. (White Mulberry) — dried leaf; family Moraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Morus alba is the primary botanical source for Sang Ye in both Chinese Pharmacopoeia and Japanese Pharmacopoeia standards. Bioactive constituents include rutin, quercetin, chlorogenic acid, and 1-deoxynojirimycin (DNJ), the latter relevant to Kampo use in glycemic regulation. The classical Wind-Heat and Liver-Heat indications correspond broadly to modern anti-inflammatory and antipyretic activity observed in preclinical studies.

entities_mentioned.name: 菊花 (Ju Hua / Kikka)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chrysanthemum morifolium Ramat.; family Asteraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequent pairing herb with Sang Ye in Wind-Heat and eye-clearing formulas; both share cold nature and Lung-Liver channel tropism.

entities_mentioned.name: 杏仁 (Xing Ren / Kyōnin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prunus armeniaca L. (Apricot kernel); family Rosaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Paired with Sang Ye in Sang Xing Tang for Warm-Dryness patterns affecting the Lung.

entities_mentioned.name: 桑菊飲 (Sang Ju Yin)
