entities_mentioned.notes: Classical formula; not a plant entity but a compound prescription. Documented in Wu Jutong's Wenbing Tiaobian (1798). Widely used in both TCM and Kampo for early-stage Wind-Heat febrile disease.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided is a brief modern-style label ('Plant: Sang Ye 桑葉 — Mulberry leaf — wind heat, lung, liver') rather than a verbatim classical Chinese passage from the Shennong Bencaojing. Accordingly, the 'original_text' field supplies a reconstructed representative classical Chinese formulation consistent with the Bencaojing and Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目, Li Shizhen, 1596) traditions, clearly signaled as such. The comprehensive entry synthesizes the Shennong Bencaojing tradition (c. 200 CE), Bencao Gangmu (1596), Wenbing Tiaobian (1798), and Japanese Kampo pharmacopoeial sources. Sang Ye does not appear prominently as a named drug in the earliest recovered Shennong Bencaojing strata (where the focus is on Sang Gen Bai Pi [桑根白皮, Mulberry root bark]), but the leaf is elaborated extensively in later bencao literature and is fully integrated into the Kampo canon. This distinction is noted for scholarly accuracy. No OCR errors or illegible passages to report, as the source was a brief modern label rather than a scanned historical text.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Sang Ye 桑葉

results.title_inferred: Ju Hua (Chrysanthemum Flower) — Classical Herb Entry
results.original_text: 菊花，味苦，平。主風頭眩腫痛，目欲脫，淚出，皮膚死肌，惡風濕痹。久服利血氣，輕身耐老延年。一名節華。生川澤及田野。
results.translation:
Ju Hua [Chrysanthemum flower]. Flavor: bitter [ku]; Nature: balanced/neutral [ping]. Primary actions: governs wind-induced [feng] headache with dizziness, swelling and pain; sensation of the eyes protruding, with tearing; dead [necrotic] skin and muscle [si ji]; and obstruction by malign wind-damp [e feng shi bi]. Prolonged consumption [jiu fu] benefits the blood and qi [xue qi], lightens the body [qing shen], and confers resistance to aging, extending the years of life. Alternate classical name: Jie Hua [Node Flower]. Found growing in river valleys, marshes, and open fields.

— Kampo Elaboration (Japanese tradition): In Kampo practice, Ju Hua (Japanese: Kikkwa / 菊花, kikka) is classified among the Superior Herbs [shang pin] of the Shennong Bencaojing — herbs considered non-toxic and suitable for long-term use to nourish life. The flavor is refined in later commentary to sweet-bitter [gan ku]; nature remains cool to slightly cold [liang / wei han] in Tang and Song dynasty elaborations (Li Shizhen's Bencao Gangmu assigns it bitter, sweet, and cool). Channel tropism [gui jing]: Liver [gan jing] and Lung [fei jing] channels primarily; some sources also cite the Kidney channel for its long-term tonic effects on vision.

Therapeutic actions as elaborated in Kampo and later Chinese tradition: (1) Disperses wind-heat [shu san feng re] — used in externally-contracted wind-heat patterns presenting with fever, headache, and red or painful eyes. (2) Calms the liver and subdues yang [ping gan qian yang] — used for liver yang rising [gan yang shang kang] manifesting as vertex headache, dizziness, and tinnitus. (3) Clears the liver and brightens the eyes [qing gan ming mu] — classical and Kampo indication for red, swollen, painful eyes, excessive tearing, and diminished vision due to liver-kidney vacuity. (4) Clears heat and resolves toxicity [qing re jie du] — used for sores and swellings [chuang yong] in some classical formulas.

Preparation: Dried flowers decocted [jian] in water; also prepared as a long-term beverage infusion [pao cha] for prophylactic and tonic use. Dose typically 9–15g in decoction.

Key classical formulas featuring Ju Hua: (1) Ju Hua Cha Tiao San — modification of Chuan Xiong Cha Tiao San adding Ju Hua and Jiang Can for wind-heat headache and eye symptoms. (2) Qi Ju Di Huang Wan [Lycium Berry, Chrysanthemum, and Rehmannia Pill] — Kampo and Chinese internal medicine formula combining Ju Hua with Gou Qi Zi and Liu Wei Di Huang Wan base for liver-kidney yin vacuity with visual disturbance; one of the most commonly used Kampo formulas for eye health. (3) Sang Ju Yin [Mulberry Leaf and Chrysanthemum Drink, from Wen Bing Tiao Bian] — paired with Sang Ye for mild wind-heat exterior patterns with cough and eye irritation.

entities_mentioned.name: Ju Hua 菊花
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chrysanthemum morifolium Ramat. (family Asteraceae); dried capitula of the cultivated chrysanthemum. Multiple regional varieties recognized in Chinese pharmacopoeia: Hang Ju (Hangzhou), Bo Ju (Bozhou), Gong Ju, Chu Ju — differing somewhat in flavor profile and clinical emphasis.
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most unambiguous herb identifications in the classical materia medica. The Pharmacopoeia of the People's Republic of China (current edition) officially lists Chrysanthemum morifolium Ramat. as the source plant. Japanese Kampo uses the same source plant under the name Kikka (菊花). The classical alternate name Jie Hua (節華) refers to the flower's blooming at the time of seasonal nodes in autumn.

entities_mentioned.name: Qi Ju Di Huang Wan 杞菊地黃丸
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical Chinese / Kampo compound formula — not a single plant
entities_mentioned.notes: A major Kampo formula built on the Liu Wei Di Huang Wan base, adding Gou Qi Zi (Lycium barbarum fruit) and Ju Hua. Widely used in contemporary Kampo for age-related visual decline, dry eyes, and liver-kidney yin vacuity patterns.

entities_mentioned.name: Sang Ju Yin 桑菊飲
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical Chinese formula (Wenbing Tiaobian, Wu Jutong, 1798 CE) — not a single plant
entities_mentioned.notes: Representative formula of the Warm Disease [wen bing] school pairing Ju Hua with Sang Ye (Morus alba leaf) for early-stage wind-heat patterns. Marks the extension of Ju Hua's use beyond the Shennong Bencaojing's original scope into systematic febrile disease theory.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided is a brief modern summary label rather than a full verbatim classical text passage; accordingly, the 'original_text' field reconstructs the most widely cited Shennong Bencaojing base text for Ju Hua (as preserved in the Mori Tateyuki and Tao Hongjing recension traditions) and the translation and elaboration sections draw on the Bencaojing proper, Li Shizhen's Bencao Gangmu (1596), and standard Kampo pharmacological references (Yakucho, Kampo Igaku). Where the classical Shennong Bencaojing text assigns 'bitter and balanced [ku ping],' later Tang-Song and Edo-period Kampo authorities consistently refine the nature toward 'cool [liang]' or 'slightly cold [wei han],' reflecting accumulated clinical observation; both readings are noted. No genuinely illegible passages in the reconstructed source. The Kampo name 'Kikka' (菊花) is the direct Sino-Japanese reading of the Chinese characters and is in standard use in Japanese Kampo formularies (Hozai).
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Ju Hua 菊花

results.title_inferred: Man Jing Zi (Vitex Fruit): Classical Properties and Therapeutic Actions
results.original_text: 蔓荊子 — 味苦，微寒。主筋骨間寒熱，濕痹拘攣，明目堅齒，利九竅，去白蟲。久服輕身耐老。小荊實亦等。生山谷。
results.translation:
Man Jing Zi [Vitex fruit; 蔓荊子]

Chinese Name: Màn Jīng Zǐ (蔓荊子)
Japanese Kampo Name: Manjingshi (まんけいし / 蔓荊子)
Classical Category: Superior Herb [上品, shàng pǐn] — suitable for prolonged use without harm.

Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]: Bitter [苦, kǔ]; some classical sources also note a mild acrid/pungent quality.
Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]: Slightly Cold [微寒, wēi hán].
Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]: Enters the Bladder [膀胱], Liver [肝], and Stomach [胃] channels. Later Kampo elaborations additionally associate it with the Lung channel in the context of wind-heat dispersal.

Full Classical Translation (Shennong Bencaojing tradition, c. 200 CE):
'[Man Jing Zi is] bitter in flavor and slightly cold in nature. It governs cold and heat [pathogenic influences lodged] between the sinews and bones [筋骨間寒熱], damp painful obstruction [濕痹] and contracture/cramping [拘攣]. It brightens the eyes [明目] and firms the teeth [堅齒]. It benefits the nine orifices [利九竅] and expels white worms [去白蟲, i.e., intestinal parasites]. Prolonged consumption lightens the body and resists aging [久服輕身耐老]. The fruit of the small Vitex [小荊實] is considered equivalent. It grows in mountain valleys.'

Subsequent Elaborations and Kampo Tradition:
Later Chinese materia medica (notably the Bencao Gangmu of Li Shizhen, 1596 CE) and the Japanese Kampo tradition significantly elaborated on Man Jing Zi's role as a premier herb for wind-heat ascending to the head. Key elaborated actions include: (1) Dispersing wind-heat [疏散風熱, shū sàn fēng rè] — especially effective for exterior wind-heat patterns; (2) Clearing the head and brightening the eyes [清利頭目, qīng lì tóu mù] — classical indication for wind-heat headache, particularly affecting the vertex or temples, dizziness, and eye redness; (3) Expelling wind-damp [祛風濕, qū fēng shī] — for painful obstruction of the joints and sinews; (4) In the Kampo tradition, Man Jing Zi is classed as a 'head-reaching' [頭部引經藥] guide drug that directs other medicinals upward to the cephalic region.

Classical Therapeutic Indications:
— Wind-heat headache and migraine [風熱頭痛]
— Vertex heaviness and pressure sensation
— Eye disorders: redness, tearing, diminished visual acuity due to wind-heat or Liver-heat
— Tinnitus and ear pain from ascending wind-heat
— Damp-impediment [濕痹] with sinew contracture
— Early-stage superficial wind conditions

Key Classical Formulas (Fang Ji) Featuring Man Jing Zi:
1. Man Jing Zi Tang [蔓荊子湯] — for wind-heat headache with eye pain and photophobia.
2. Qiang Huo Sheng Shi Tang [羌活勝濕湯] — canonical formula for wind-damp headache and bodily heaviness; Man Jing Zi serves as an ascending, cephalic-directing agent alongside Qiang Huo and Du Huo.
3. Yi Qi Cong Ming Tang [益氣聰明湯] — a Kampo-favored formula addressing Qi deficiency with sensory dulling (poor vision, tinnitus); Man Jing Zi brightens the eyes and clears the upper orifices.
4. Chuan Xiong Cha Tiao San [川芎茶調散] — where Man Jing Zi may be added for wind-heat predominant presentations.

Preparation Notes:
Typically used as dried ripe fruit, 5–10 g in decoction [煎服]. May also be prepared as powder or pill. The fruit should be lightly crushed before decocting to improve extraction of active constituents.

entities_mentioned.name: 蔓荊子 (Màn Jīng Zǐ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vitex trifolia L. (three-leaf chaste tree) or Vitex rotundifolia L.f. (single-leaf chaste tree); family Lamiaceae (formerly Verbenaceae). The ripe fruit of either species is the official drug. V. rotundifolia is more commonly used in Japan (Kampo).
entities_mentioned.notes: The Shennong Bencaojing reference to '小荊實' (fruit of the small Vitex) likely refers to Vitex negundo var. cannabifolia or a related small-leafed Vitex species considered equivalent in potency. Modern pharmacopeial standards (Chinese Pharmacopoeia 2020) officially accept both V. trifolia and V. rotundifolia.

entities_mentioned.name: 小荊實 (Xiǎo Jīng Shí)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely Vitex negundo var. cannabifolia or Vitex rotundifolia; a smaller-fruited Vitex species referenced in the classical text as therapeutically equivalent.
entities_mentioned.notes: The exact species intended by 小荊 has been debated across commentarial traditions. Most Song dynasty and later editors treat it as a varietal synonym rather than a distinct drug.

entities_mentioned.name: 白蟲 (Bái Chóng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Intestinal parasites, most likely tapeworm (Taenia species) or pinworm (Enterobius vermicularis), referenced in the classical text as 'white worms.'
entities_mentioned.notes: The antiparasitic claim for Man Jing Zi is found in the Shennong Bencaojing but is rarely emphasized in later clinical tradition; modern phytochemical investigation has not prominently confirmed anthelminthic activity.

entities_mentioned.name: 羌活勝濕湯 (Qiāng Huó Shèng Shī Tāng)
entities_mentioned.notes: A canonical formula from the Neiwaishang Bianhuo Lun (內外傷辨惑論) of Li Dongyuan (c. 1247 CE). Man Jing Zi functions here as a cephalic guide drug [引經藥].

entities_mentioned.name: 益氣聰明湯 (Yì Qì Cōng Míng Tāng)
entities_mentioned.notes: Also attributed to Li Dongyuan. Particularly favored in the Kampo tradition for age-related sensory decline. 'Congming' (聰明) literally means 'acute hearing and bright vision,' encapsulating the formula's target symptoms.

results.translator_notes: The source text as supplied is a modern summary label rather than a verbatim classical passage; the original_text field above reconstructs the canonical Shennong Bencaojing entry for Man Jing Zi based on the standard received text (森立之 Mori Risshi 1845 critical edition and related recensions). The translation and entry accordingly synthesize the Shennong Bencaojing core text with documented Kampo elaborations, clearly distinguished. The antiparasitic indication (去白蟲) is preserved faithfully from the classical source despite its obsolescence in modern clinical use. Channel tropism assignments (Bladder, Liver, Stomach) reflect the post-Tang systematization not present in the original Shennong Bencaojing, which predates formal gui jing theory; these are drawn from the Bencao Jing Shu (本草經疏, Miao Xiyong, 1625 CE) and subsequent consensus.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Man Jing Zi 蔓荊子

results.title_inferred: Lian Qiao (Forsythia Fruit): Heat Toxin, Sores, and Scrofula
results.original_text: 連翹，味苦，平。主寒熱，鼠瘻，瘰癧，癰腫，惡瘡，癭瘤，結熱，蠱毒。一名異翹，一名蘭華，一名折根，一名軹，一名三廉。生山谷。
results.translation:
Lian Qiao [連翹, Forsythia]. Flavor: bitter [ku 苦]. Nature: neutral/balanced [ping 平].

Primary actions [zhu zhi 主治]: Treats alternating cold and heat [han re 寒熱]; rat-fistula [shu lou 鼠瘻] and scrofula [luo li 瘰癧, i.e., tubercular cervical lymphadenopathy]; carbuncles and swellings [yong zhong 癰腫]; malignant sores [e chuang 惡瘡]; goiter and tumors [ying liu 癭瘤]; accumulated heat [jie re 結熱]; and gu toxin [gu du 蠱毒, i.e., parasitic or malefic poisoning].

Alternate names [yi ming 一名]: Yi Qiao [異翹], Lan Hua [蘭華], Zhe Gen [折根], Zhi [軹], San Lian [三廉].

Habitat: Grows in mountain valleys [sheng shan gu 生山谷].

---

CLASSICAL CATEGORY: Middle herb [zhong pin 中品] in the Shennong Bencaojing three-tier classification. Middle-class herbs are associated with tonification but carry some inherent toxicity or limitation; they must be used with guidance and are not suited for indefinite long-term use.

FIVE FLAVORS [wu wei 五味]: Bitter [ku 苦]

FOUR NATURES [si qi 四氣]: Neutral to slightly cold [ping, tending liang/han in later elaborations]. Later Bencao systematists (Li Shizhen, Bencao Gangmu, 1596) clarified Lian Qiao's nature as slightly cold [wei han 微寒], consistent with its heat-clearing function.

CHANNEL TROPISM [gui jing 歸經] (from Kampo and Jin-Yuan elaborations): Heart [xin 心], Lung [fei 肺], Small Intestine [xiao chang 小腸]. Some traditions additionally include Gallbladder [dan 膽] and Triple Burner [san jiao 三焦].

THERAPEUTIC ACTIONS (classical and Kampo elaboration):
1. Clears heat and resolves toxin [qing re jie du 清熱解毒]: principal action against fire-toxin patterns [huo du 火毒] causing suppurative skin conditions.
2. Disperses nodules and dissipates swellings [san jie xiao zhong 散結消腫]: specific affinity for lymphatic swellings, scrofula, and goiter; considered a chief herb [jun yao 君藥] for treating lumps of the neck and throat region.
3. Expels wind-heat [qu feng re 祛風熱]: used in early-stage febrile illness with exterior wind-heat signs.
4. Clears Heart fire [qing xin huo 清心火]: bitter flavor and Heart tropism allow it to address Heart-fire excess manifesting as irritability, mouth sores, and scanty dark urine.
5. Anti-parasitic and detoxifying [jie gu du 解蠱毒]: classical indication for gu toxin, interpreted in modern ethnopharmacology as broad antimicrobial and antiparasitic properties.

KAMPO ELABORATION (日本漢方, Nihon Kampo):
In the Japanese Kampo tradition, Lian Qiao [連翹, J: Rengyo レンギョウ] is standardized as the dried fruit of Forsythia suspensa. Kampo physicians of the Edo period (especially the Kohō-ha 古方派, Classical Formula School) prized it as an indispensable component in formulas addressing thermal toxin with nodular swelling. It was viewed as having a particular affinity for the upper burner [上焦, jō-shō] and the skin surface, making it especially effective for dermatological and lymphatic conditions. The Kohō-ha valorized the Shennong and Shanghan Lun traditions over speculative theory, using Lian Qiao empirically in formulas matching pattern presentations rather than solely theoretical channel assignments.

KEY CLASSICAL FORMULAS:
- Yin Qiao San [銀翹散, Yín Qiào Sǎn]: Wind-heat exterior pattern; paired with Jin Yin Hua [金銀花, Lonicera japonica] as co-chief herbs. Source: Wen Bing Tiao Bian (1798, Wu Jutong). A foundational formula in warm-disease [wen bing 溫病] medicine; widely used in Kampo for early febrile illness, influenza, and tonsillitis.
- Lian Qiao Bai Du San [連翹敗毒散]: Clears toxin and disperses swellings; used for suppurative skin infections, furuncles, and carbuncles.
- Fang Feng Tong Sheng San [防風通聖散, J: Bōfū-tsūshō-san]: A broad-spectrum wind-heat and interior heat-clearing formula in Kampo; includes Lian Qiao among numerous herbs to address both exterior and interior heat with skin and lymphatic involvement. Official Kampo preparation in Japan's National Health Insurance formulary.
- Xiao Chai Hu Tang variants [小柴胡湯加減]: Occasionally supplemented with Lian Qiao in Kampo adaptations for alternating fever-chills with lymphadenopathy.

PREPARATION NOTES:
- Standard decoction [tang 湯]: 6–15 g dried fruit in water decoction. Lian Qiao is added mid-decoction; prolonged boiling may reduce volatile aromatic constituents.
- Powder [san 散]: Ground fruit used in compound powders for topical or internal application.
- Classical texts distinguish Lian Qiao shells [lian qiao ke 連翹殼] from Lian Qiao hearts [lian qiao xin 連翹心, i.e., seeds]; the heart (seeds) was held to be especially effective for clearing Heart fire and was used separately in some formulas.

entities_mentioned.name: Lian Qiao 連翹
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Forsythia suspensa (Thunb.) Vahl — Oleaceae family; dried fruit (both mature 'lao qiao' and immature 'qing qiao' forms used)
entities_mentioned.notes: The classical text lists multiple alternate names (Yi Qiao, Lan Hua, Zhe Gen, Zhi, San Lian), reflecting regional naming variation across early Chinese herbalism. Modern pharmacopeial standard (Chinese Pharmacopoeia 2020, Japanese Pharmacopoeia JP18) confirms Forsythia suspensa as the official botanical source. Contains forsythoside A and B, phillyrin, and rutin as principal bioactive constituents with demonstrated antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, and antiviral activity in modern pharmacological studies.

entities_mentioned.name: Shu Lou 鼠瘻 (rat-fistula)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scrofulous fistula; likely tubercular lymphadenitis with fistulous tracts; possibly other cervical lymph node suppurations
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical term for suppurating fistulous tracts, particularly in the neck region, associated with scrofula (tubercular cervical lymphadenopathy). The 'rat' in the name may reference the gnawing pain or the shape of the tracts. This is distinct from but closely related to the next term.

entities_mentioned.name: Luo Li 瘰癧 (scrofula)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tubercular cervical lymphadenopathy (scrofula); more broadly, any chronic lymph node enlargement of the neck
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most consistently recorded indications for Lian Qiao across the classical literature. The herb's nodule-dispersing action [san jie 散結] is considered particularly suited to this condition in both Chinese and Japanese traditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Gu Du 蠱毒
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Uncertain; variously interpreted as parasitic disease (e.g., schistosomiasis, fascioliasis), food poisoning, or malefic ritual poisoning
entities_mentioned.notes: Gu toxin is a notoriously complex classical category encompassing both physical pathogens (parasites, venoms) and culturally-specific concepts of intentional poisoning via ritual means. In pharmacological terms, the antimicrobial and antiparasitic effects of Lian Qiao constituents lend credibility to the classical indication against pathogenic organisms.

entities_mentioned.name: Ying Liu 癭瘤 (goiter and tumors)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Thyroid goiter (ying 癭) and soft-tissue tumors or cysts (liu 瘤)
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical paired term. Ying typically refers to swellings of the neck (goiter), while liu refers more broadly to lumps and growths elsewhere. Lian Qiao's phlegm-heat dispersing and nodule-softening properties underlie this indication.

entities_mentioned.name: Forsythia suspensa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Forsythia suspensa (Thunb.) Vahl
entities_mentioned.notes: Weeping forsythia; native to China, cultivated throughout East Asia including Japan. The dried fruit is the officinal part. Distinct from the ornamental Forsythia × intermedia common in Western gardens, though related. Japanese Kampo sources (J: Rengyo 連翹) use the same species.

results.translator_notes:
1. SOURCE TEXT NOTE: The segment as supplied is a modern scholarly summary label rather than a verbatim classical Chinese passage. The original_text field above has been reconstructed from the standard Shennong Bencaojing text for Lian Qiao as preserved in the Zhenghe Bencao (1116 CE) and other received editions, which is the scholarly convention for Bencaojing entries whose original Han-dynasty text does not survive independently but is preserved through citation in later works.

2. CLASSICAL CATEGORY CLARIFICATION: Lian Qiao's placement as a 'middle herb' [zhong pin] is consistent across received Bencaojing editions. Some later compilers (notably Tao Hongjing, 456–536 CE) reorganized herb categories, but the middle-class designation for Lian Qiao is stable.

3. NATURE DISCREPANCY: The original Shennong text gives 'ping' (neutral) as the nature. Subsequent systematic works, particularly the Bencao Yanyi (1116) and Bencao Gangmu (1596), refined this to 'slightly cold' [wei han] based on clinical observation of its heat-clearing effects. Kampo tradition generally follows the 'slightly cold' characterization. Both are noted here for scholarly completeness.

4. FORMULA ANACHRONISM: The key formulas cited (especially Yin Qiao San from 1798) post-date the Shennong Bencaojing by approximately 1,600 years. They are included per the entry schema's request for 'subsequent Kampo elaborations' and classical formula traditions, and are clearly attributed to their actual sources.

5. KAMPO STANDARDIZATION: In Japan, Rengyo (連翹) is listed in the Japanese Pharmacopoeia (JP18) and is an approved constituent of several Kampo preparations reimbursed under the National Health Insurance system, most notably Bōfū-tsūshō-san (防風通聖散).
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Lian Qiao 連翹

results.title_inferred: Jin Yin Hua (Honeysuckle Flower) — Heat Toxin and Infection
results.original_text: 金銀花 (Jīn Yín Huā) — 忍冬 (Rěn Dōng) — 味甘，性寒。歸肺、心、胃經。清熱解毒，疏散風熱。主治癰腫疔瘡，喉痹，丹毒，熱毒血痢，風熱感冒，溫病發熱。
results.translation:
Jin Yin Hua [Gold-Silver Flower; Lonicera japonica flower] — also known classically as Rěn Dōng [Winter Endurer, referring to the whole vine]. 

Five Flavors [wǔ wèi]: Sweet [gān].
Four Natures [sì qì]: Cold [hán].
Channel Tropism [guī jīng]: Lung [Fèi], Heart [Xīn], Stomach [Wèi] channels.
Classical Category: Middle herb [zhōng pǐn] in some Bencao traditions; later reclassified as superior [shàng pǐn] in several Kampo systematizations due to its broad clinical utility and relatively low toxicity.

Therapeutic Actions:
1. Clears heat and resolves toxins [qīng rè jiě dú]: The foremost herb for externally-contracted heat toxin patterns, including suppurative infections, furuncles [dīng chuāng], carbuncles [yōng], and boils [jū].
2. Disperses wind-heat [shū sàn fēng rè]: Used in early-stage warm-febrile disease [wēn bìng] and wind-heat common cold [fēng rè gǎn mào], characterized by fever, slight aversion to cold, sore throat, and superficial rapid pulse.
3. Cools the blood and stops dysentery [liáng xuè zhǐ lì]: Addresses hot-toxin bloody dysentery [rè dú xuè lì].
4. Clears throat impediment [hóu bì]: Reduces inflammation and toxicity in pharyngitis and tonsillitis.
5. Resolves erysipelas and cinnabar toxin [dān dú]: Addresses the red, hot, sharply demarcated skin lesions characteristic of streptococcal erysipelas in classical pathology.

Kampo (Japanese) Name: Nindō-ka [忍冬花] or Kinginка [金銀花]; the vine and stem are used separately as Nindō [忍冬] (Lonicera stem/leaf), a classical Kampo diaphoretic and anti-inflammatory drug. Japanese Kampo practitioners particularly emphasize Jin Yin Hua in formulas addressing suppurative skin conditions and early febrile illness.

Key Classical and Kampo Formulas:
- Yín Qiào Sǎn [銀翹散]: The most celebrated formula featuring Jin Yin Hua; pairs it with Lián Qiào [連翹, Forsythia] to resolve wind-heat, clear toxin, and vent the exterior. Standard treatment for early-stage warm disease with fever, sore throat, and headache. (Source: Wēn Bìng Tiáo Biàn, Wu Jutong, 1798 CE; adopted into Kampo practice.)
- Xiān Fāng Huó Mìng Yǐn [仙方活命飲]: Foundational formula for suppurative infections and early-stage abscesses; Jin Yin Hua serves as the principal [jūn] herb, clearing heat toxin and reducing swelling.
- Wǔ Wèi Xiāo Dú Yǐn [五味消毒飲]: Five-herb formula for multiple simultaneous furuncles and carbuncles with severe heat toxin; Jin Yin Hua leads the formula.
- Sì Miào Yǒng Ān Tāng [四妙勇安湯]: Uses Jin Yin Hua in large doses for vascular heat toxin patterns (classically described as gangrene or thromboangiitis).

Preparation:
- Decoction [tāng]: 6–15 g dried flower buds (harvested just before opening for maximal potency); up to 30–60 g in severe acute infections.
- Fresh herb [xiān yòng]: Crushed and applied topically to hot, swollen sores; juice expressed for oral administration.
- Jin Yin Hua Lù [金銀花露]: Distillate of fresh flowers; used as a cooling summer beverage to clear summerheat [shǔ rè] and mild heat toxin, particularly in children.
- Charred form [jīn yín huā tàn]: Prepared by dry-frying to charring; enhances hemostatic action for hot dysentery with bleeding.

Classical Shennong Bencaojing Note: The flower form (Jin Yin Hua proper) is not explicitly named in the original Shennong Bencaojing (c. 200 CE), where the plant appears primarily as Rěn Dōng [忍冬], emphasizing the vine and its property of 'enduring winter' [耐冬]. The specific promotion of the flower as the primary medicinal part developed through the Tang (Xīn Xiū Běncǎo, 659 CE), Song, and Ming dynasties, culminating in Li Shizhen's Běncǎo Gāngmù (1596 CE), which comprehensively established Jin Yin Hua as a paramount heat-clearing and toxin-resolving drug. Kampo incorporation followed via the transmission of Ming Bencao scholarship to Japan.

entities_mentioned.name: Jin Yin Hua 金銀花
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lonicera japonica Thunberg (Japanese Honeysuckle) — dried flower buds and newly opened flowers; family Caprifoliaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmacopoeia of the People's Republic of China (PPRC) officially lists Lonicera japonica as the primary source plant. Related species (L. hypoglauca, L. confusa, L. fulvotomentosa) are accepted as regional substitutes in Chinese standards. Active constituents include chlorogenic acid, luteolin, and various iridoid glycosides with documented antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antiviral properties in modern pharmacology.

entities_mentioned.name: Rěn Dōng 忍冬
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lonicera japonica Thunberg — vine/stem/leaf form of the same plant
entities_mentioned.notes: The classical name Rěn Dōng ('winter endurer') refers to the evergreen nature of the vine. Used as a separate drug entity (Rěn Dōng Téng, 忍冬藤) for joint pain with heat signs and infections affecting the limbs.

entities_mentioned.name: Lián Qiào 連翹
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Forsythia suspensa (Thunb.) Vahl — dried fruit; family Oleaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequent formula partner with Jin Yin Hua; shares heat-clearing and toxin-resolving properties with added strength in dispersing clumps [sàn jié].

entities_mentioned.name: Yín Qiào Sǎn 銀翹散
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical formula, not a plant; included as a primary classical context for Jin Yin Hua's clinical use.

results.translator_notes: 1. The Shennong Bencaojing (c. 200 CE) does not contain a dedicated entry for 'Jin Yin Hua' by that name; the source text as provided appears to be a composite drawing on post-Han elaborations, particularly the Bencao Gangmu (1596 CE) and Qing-dynasty warm disease [wēn bìng] literature. The entry has been constructed accordingly, with anachronistic elements (e.g., Yin Qiao San) clearly attributed to their actual historical sources. 2. The Five Flavors classification as exclusively 'sweet' is consistent across major Bencao sources; some secondary sources add a minor 'slightly acrid [wēi xīn]' quality to account for its exterior-releasing action, but this is not the mainstream classification and has been omitted to reflect the dominant tradition. 3. Channel tropism (Lung, Heart, Stomach) is the standard PPRC classification; classical Bencao sources are less systematic about channel assignment but describe clinical targets (throat, skin, intestines, febrile illness) consistent with this modern mapping. 4. Dosage ranges reflect both classical recommendations and contemporary clinical practice; the high-dose range (up to 60 g) for acute severe infections is attested in Qing-dynasty sources and modern Chinese hospital practice but would be unusual in standard Kampo prescription doses. 5. No genuinely illegible passages in the source material; the source segment was a prompt outline rather than a primary manuscript, so reconstruction from the tradition was required throughout.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Jin Yin Hua 金銀花

results.title_inferred: Ban Lan Gen (Isatis Root): Classical and Kampo Entry on Heat-Toxin Clearing
results.original_text: 板藍根 (Bǎn Lán Gēn) — 菘藍根 / 靛青根 — 性寒，味苦；入心、胃經；清熱解毒，涼血利咽。治溫毒發斑，痄腮，喉痹，丹毒，癰腫。
results.translation:
Ban Lan Gen [Root of the Isatis / Woad Plant]: 

**Chinese Name:** Bǎn Lán Gēn (板藍根); alternate classical names include Sōng Lán Gēn (菘藍根) and Diàn Qīng Gēn (靛青根, 'indigo-blue root').

**Japanese Kampo Name:** Banrankon (バンランコン / 板藍根).

**Classical Category (Shennong Bencaojing tradition):** While Ban Lan Gen as an independent entry does not appear by this precise name in the foundational Shennong Bencaojing (c. 200 CE), the parent plant Isatis indigotica and its preparations (particularly Da Qing Ye, 大青葉, the leaf) are referenced in early materia medica tradition under the category of heat-clearing, toxin-resolving drugs. Subsequent commentators, including Tao Hongjing (陶弘景, c. 456–536 CE) and Li Shizhen in the Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目, 1596 CE), codify its use more fully. Classified by later systematizers as a **Middle or Inferior herb [中品 / 下品]** depending on source, reflecting its potency and narrower therapeutic window compared to tonifying superior herbs.

**Five Flavors [Wǔ Wèi, 五味]:** Bitter [kǔ, 苦].

**Four Natures [Sì Qì, 四氣]:** Cold [hán, 寒].

**Channel Tropism [Guī Jīng, 歸經]:** Heart [Xīn, 心] and Stomach [Wèi, 胃] channels; some later Kampo and Chinese sources additionally note the Lung [Fèi, 肺] channel.

**Therapeutic Actions:**
1. Clears Heat and Resolves Toxin [qīng rè jiě dú, 清熱解毒]: Indicated for externally-contracted Heat toxin patterns, including febrile epidemic diseases [wēn yì, 溫疫] and warm toxin disorders [wēn dú, 溫毒]. Classical commentators associate this action with its bitter, cold nature penetrating the Blood level.
2. Cools the Blood [liáng xuè, 涼血]: Used when Heat toxin has entered the Blood aspect [xuè fèn, 血分], manifesting as macules and papules [fā bān, 發斑], petechiae, or hemorrhagic presentations.
3. Benefits the Throat [lì yān, 利咽]: Relieves painful throat obstruction [hóu bì, 喉痹], including suppurative tonsillitis and pharyngitis from wind-heat or heat-toxin.
4. Disperses Swelling and Reduces Inflammation: Applied to parotitis [zhà sāi, 痄腮; mumps-like presentations], erysipelas [dān dú, 丹毒], and carbuncles [yōng zhǒng, 癰腫].

**Preparation Methods (classical):**
- Decoction [tāng, 湯]: Dried root, typically 9–15 g (classical proportions vary; Kampo dosing often 6–12 g). Decocted in water and administered orally.
- Powder [sǎn, 散]: Ground root administered with warm water; used for throat conditions.
- Topical paste: Fresh root or powder mixed with water or vinegar and applied externally to swellings and erysipelas.
- Modern pharmaceutical elaboration (20th–21st c. Kampo and TCM): Standardized granule extract (板藍根顆粒, Bǎn Lán Gēn Kēlì), widely used in China for viral respiratory infections.

**Key Classical and Kampo Formulas:**
- **Pǔ Jì Xiāo Dú Yǐn (普濟消毒飲)** [Universal Benefit Toxin-Dispersing Decoction]: Major formula from Li Dongyuan (李東垣, Jin-Yuan dynasty) for epidemic parotitis, head and face heat toxin; Ban Lan Gen is a principal ingredient alongside Huang Qin, Huang Lian, and Xuán Shēn.
- **Ban Lan Gen Chong Ji (板藍根沖劑)**: Modern Kampo/TCM granule formula for viral infections including influenza, viral hepatitis prophylaxis, and epidemic prevention.
- Used adjunctively in formulas for **viral hepatitis (肝炎)** in modern Kampo practice, often combined with Yin Chen (茵陳) and Long Dan Cao (龍膽草).

**Modern Identification:** Isatis indigotica Fortune (Brassicaceae) — the root (dried). Occasionally Isatis tinctoria L. (European woad) is referenced in Western commentary as a botanical analog. The same plant yields Da Qing Ye (大青葉, the leaf) and Qing Dai (青黛, the processed indigo pigment), both sharing overlapping heat-toxin actions.

**Classical Contraindications and Cautions:** Contraindicated in Cold patterns [hán zhèng, 寒證], Spleen-Stomach deficiency cold [pí wèi xū hán, 脾胃虛寒], and patients without Heat-toxin presentation. Prolonged use cautioned against due to the herb's cold, bitter nature depleting Spleen Yang.

entities_mentioned.name: Ban Lan Gen 板藍根
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isatis indigotica Fortune — dried root (Brassicaceae / Mustard family)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most well-attested plant-drug identifications in Chinese materia medica. The root of Isatis indigotica is the standard source in mainland Chinese pharmacopoeia (ChP). Some older Japanese Kampo texts reference Isatis tinctoria (European woad) as a substitute, but I. indigotica is the canonical source.

entities_mentioned.name: Da Qing Ye 大青葉
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isatis indigotica Fortune — dried leaf
entities_mentioned.notes: The leaf of the same plant; shares heat-clearing, blood-cooling, and toxin-resolving properties with Ban Lan Gen but has slightly broader classical indications for Blood-aspect heat patterns. Often referenced alongside Ban Lan Gen in commentaries.

entities_mentioned.name: Qing Dai 青黛
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Indigo naturalis — processed pigment derived from Isatis indigotica and related species
entities_mentioned.notes: The processed indigo preparation from the same plant family; a third functional derivative alongside the root and leaf. Used particularly for heat in the Liver channel and childhood convulsions.

entities_mentioned.name: Pǔ Jì Xiāo Dú Yǐn 普濟消毒飲
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical formula (not a single plant); compound decoction
entities_mentioned.notes: Attributed to Li Dongyuan (李東垣, 1180–1251 CE). Canonical formula for epidemic head-and-face heat toxin and parotitis. Ban Lan Gen is a featured ingredient. Formula documented in Dongyuan's Pí Wèi Lùn (脾胃論) tradition and subsequent compendia.

entities_mentioned.name: Li Shizhen 李時珍
entities_mentioned.notes: Ming dynasty (1518–1593 CE) physician and naturalist; author of Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目, 1596 CE), the most comprehensive pre-modern Chinese materia medica. Consolidates and expands earlier entries on Isatis-family drugs.

results.translator_notes: 1. Ban Lan Gen does not appear as a discrete named entry in the original Shennong Bencaojing (c. 200 CE); its classical lineage traces more directly through Mingyi Bielu (名醫別錄, c. 3rd–5th c. CE), Xinxiu Bencao (新修本草, 659 CE), and most fully in the Bencao Gangmu (1596 CE). The heat-toxin and blood-cooling functions attributed to this herb in the Shennong tradition are reconstructed from the broader Da Qing (大青) drug category. Any Shennong Bencaojing attribution should be understood as tradition-of-record rather than verbatim textual citation. 2. The segment label describes this as a 'viral' application — this is a modern pharmacological gloss (antiviral activity of indirubin and related compounds has been studied in 20th–21st c. research) and has no direct classical equivalent; the classical framing is 'heat toxin / epidemic toxin [wēn dú / rè dú].' This has been preserved in the translation with appropriate framing. 3. Channel tropism to the Lung channel (in addition to Heart and Stomach) appears in some modern Kampo and TCM sources but is not uniformly attested in classical texts; flagged accordingly.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Ban Lan Gen 板藍根

results.title_inferred: Da Qing Ye (大青葉): Isatis Leaf — Heat Toxin and Fever
results.original_text: 大青葉 — 味苦，大寒。主治時氣頭痛，大熱，口瘡。解諸毒，殺蠱。
results.translation:
Da Qing Ye (大青葉, 'Great Blue Leaf') — Flavor: bitter [ku]. Nature: greatly cold [da han]. Channel tropism [gui jing]: Heart [xin], Stomach [wei], Lung [fei], Liver [gan]. Classical category: Middle Herb [zhong pin].

Therapeutic actions: Clears heat and resolves fire toxin [qingre jiedu]; cools the blood and disperses macules and blotches [liang xue xiao ban]; treats seasonal epidemic febrile disease [shi qi] with headache and high fever; relieves oral ulceration and sores of the mouth and tongue [kou chuang]; counteracts and resolves the hundred poisons [jie zhu du]; eliminates gu-toxin [sha gu — referring to parasitic or malefic poisoning in classical terminology].

Japanese Kampo name: Taisei-yo (タイセイヨウ / 大青葉). In Kampo tradition, Da Qing Ye is employed primarily as a heat-clearing, toxin-resolving herb in patterns of excess fire in the blood aspect [xue fen]. It is frequently combined with Huang Lian (黄連, Coptis) and Huang Bai (黄柏, Phellodendron) for severe febrile toxicity, and with Sheng Di Huang (生地黄) and Mu Dan Pi (牡丹皮) when cooling the blood and eliminating macules. A closely related drug, Ban Lan Gen (板藍根, the root of the same plant), shares its heat-toxin resolving properties and is often discussed in tandem in both Chinese and Japanese classical sources.

Key classical formulas: (1) Pu Ji Xiao Du Yin (普濟消毒飲) — Universal Benefit Decoction for Eliminating Toxin, used for epidemic parotitis and throat toxin; (2) Da Qing Ye Tang (大青葉湯) — a straightforward decoction for febrile exanthems with macules; (3) various Kampo fever-clearing formulations (seido-type, 清道) incorporating Taisei-yo alongside Gypsum [Sekko, 石膏] and Scutellaria [Ogon, 黄芩].

Preparation: Typically administered as a decoction [tang]; fresh juice [xian zhi] may be expressed and applied topically or taken internally for acute heat-toxin conditions; dried leaf powder can be applied externally to ulcers and toxic swellings. Standard dried dose: 9–15 g; fresh herb: 30–60 g.

entities_mentioned.name: Da Qing Ye 大青葉
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isatis indigotica Fortune (family Brassicaceae) — leaf; less commonly Strobilanthes cusia (Nees) Kuntze (family Acanthaceae) depending on regional sourcing
entities_mentioned.notes: The most widely accepted source in the Chinese Pharmacopoeia is Isatis indigotica. The same plant yields Ban Lan Gen (root) and Qing Dai (indigo pigment processed from the leaf). In pre-modern texts, 'Da Qing' could refer to multiple blue-leaved plants; by the Tang dynasty (Li Shizhen confirms in Bencao Gangmu) Isatis was the canonical source.

entities_mentioned.name: Ban Lan Gen 板藍根
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isatis indigotica root
entities_mentioned.notes: Sister drug from the same plant; shares heat-toxin clearing properties; frequently co-discussed in Kampo and TCM texts alongside Da Qing Ye.

entities_mentioned.name: Qing Dai 青黛
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Powdered indigo pigment derived from Isatis indigotica and related plants
entities_mentioned.notes: Third product of the Isatis plant; used for similar heat-toxin and blood-cooling indications; appears in Kampo as Seitan (青黛).

entities_mentioned.name: Pu Ji Xiao Du Yin 普濟消毒飲
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical formula (Yuan dynasty, attributed to Li Dongyuan)
entities_mentioned.notes: Canonical formula for wind-heat toxin epidemic conditions; features Da Qing Ye prominently alongside Huang Qin, Huang Lian, and Niu Bang Zi.

results.translator_notes: The source text supplied is a brief modern summary label rather than a full classical Chinese primary passage. The original_text field above reconstructs the core classical phrasing consistent with the Shennong Bencaojing tradition and Bencao Gangmu elaborations, as no verbatim classical excerpt was provided in the segment. Scholars should verify against the Zhenghe Bencao (政和本草) and Bencao Gangmu (本草綱目, 1596) for the full classical Chinese locus. The term 'gu' [蠱] translated as 'gu-toxin / parasitic poisoning' carries both literal (parasitic infestation) and culturally specific (malefic sorcery-related illness) connotations in classical Chinese medicine; both senses were operative in the Shennong Bencaojing period. Da Qing Ye does not appear as a named entry in the earliest received Shennong Bencaojing strata but is well attested in Tao Hongjing's (陶弘景) 5th-century commentary and subsequent materia medica traditions.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Da Qing Ye 大青葉

results.title_inferred: Pu Gong Ying (Dandelion): Classical Herb Entry for Heat Toxin and Breast Disorders
results.original_text: 蒲公英 (Pú Gōng Yīng) — 蒲公英屬 — 苦、甘，寒。歸肝、胃經。清熱解毒，消腫散結，利濕通淋。主治癰腫瘡毒，乳癰內癰，熱淋澀痛，濕熱黃疸，目赤腫痛。
results.translation:
Pu Gong Ying [蒲公英, lit. 'floating-seed elder-flower herb']

**Chinese Name:** Pú Gōng Yīng (蒲公英)
**Japanese Kampo Name:** Hokōei (蒲公英, ホコウエイ)
**Modern Botanical Identification:** Taraxacum officinale (common dandelion) or Taraxacum mongolicum (Chinese dandelion); the latter is the primary medicinal species in the Chinese pharmacopeia.
**Classical Category:** Middle Herb [中品, zhōng pǐn] — herbs of moderate potency used to supplement deficiency, treat illness, and support health when taken over sustained periods, balanced between tonic and corrective action.

**Five Flavors [五味, Wǔ Wèi]:** Bitter [苦, kǔ] and Sweet [甘, gān]
— The bitter flavor enables it to clear heat, drain fire, and resolve toxins; the sweet flavor moderates harshness and supports the middle burner.

**Four Natures [四氣, Sì Qì]:** Cold [寒, hán]
— Its cold nature makes it suited to conditions arising from excess heat, fire toxin, and damp-heat accumulation.

**Channel Tropism [歸經, Guī Jīng]:** Liver [肝, gān] and Stomach [胃, wèi] channels.
— Entry into the Liver channel directs its action toward the breast (a Liver-governed region) and the eyes; entry into the Stomach channel addresses epigastric heat, abscesses arising from Stomach fire, and damp-heat in the middle burner.

**Therapeutic Actions [功效, Gōng Xiào]:**
1. Clears heat and resolves toxins [清熱解毒, qīng rè jiě dú] — Principal action; counteracts fire-toxin patterns manifesting as carbuncles, furuncles, and suppurative sores [癰腫瘡毒].
2. Disperses swelling and dissipates nodules [消腫散結, xiāo zhǒng sàn jié] — Particularly indicated for breast abscess [乳癰, rǔ yōng], whether in early-stage inflammation or suppuration; also for internal abscesses [內癰] of the Lung or Intestines.
3. Promotes urination and clears damp-heat [利濕通淋, lì shī tōng lín] — Used in hot painful urinary dysfunction [熱淋澀痛], damp-heat jaundice [濕熱黃疸].
4. Brightens the eyes [明目] — Addresses red, swollen, painful eyes [目赤腫痛] arising from Liver fire or wind-heat.

**Classical and Kampo Formulas:**
- *Wǔ Wèi Xiāo Dú Yǐn* [五味消毒飲] — 'Five-Ingredient Decoction to Eliminate Toxin': cardinal formula for fire-toxin carbuncles and furuncles; combines Pu Gong Ying with Jin Yin Hua [金銀花], Ye Ju Hua [野菊花], Zi Hua Di Ding [紫花地丁], and Tian Kui Zi [天葵子]. This formula appears in the Qing-dynasty *Yī Zōng Jīn Jiàn* [醫宗金鑑, 1742] and remains a standard in both TCM and Kampo breast-disease protocols.
- *Pu Gong Ying Tang* [蒲公英湯] — Simple decoction of Pu Gong Ying alone, or with Gua Lou [瓜蔞] and Lian Qiao [連翹], used in Kampo practice for early-stage mastitis [乳腺炎] and lactation-associated breast abscesses.
- As a single-herb wash or poultice [外敷]: fresh crushed herb applied topically to snake bites, insect stings, and hot sores — referenced in the *Bencao Gangmu* [本草綱目, Li Shizhen, 1596] and carried into Kampo external-medicine traditions.

**Shennong Bencaojing Tradition Note:** Pu Gong Ying does not appear by this name in the original *Shennong Bencaojing* (c. 200 CE) as presently reconstructed; it rises to pharmacopoeial prominence in the *Xīn Xiū Běncǎo* [新修本草, Tang dynasty, 659 CE] and is extensively elaborated by Li Shizhen. Classical Shennong categories are applied here by tradition and later scholastic attribution.

**Preparation Methods:**
- Decoction [煎服]: 10–30 g dried herb; up to 60 g fresh herb.
- Fresh juice [鮮汁]: expressed and administered orally or applied topically.
- Poultice [外敷]: fresh herb pounded and applied directly to affected area.
- Kampo granule extract [エキス顆粒]: standardized preparations used in modern Japanese clinical Kampo.

**Cautions:** Contraindicated in cases of cold from deficiency [虛寒] of the Spleen and Stomach; excessive use may cause mild diarrhea. Not to be used long-term without monitoring in constitutionally cold patients.

entities_mentioned.name: 蒲公英 (Pú Gōng Yīng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Taraxacum mongolicum Hand.-Mazz. (primary); Taraxacum officinale Weber (secondary/Western equivalent)
entities_mentioned.notes: Taraxacum mongolicum is the official species listed in the Chinese Pharmacopoeia (2020 edition). Taraxacum officinale is the species most familiar in Western herbal traditions and is used interchangeably in some Kampo contexts. Both share the key bitter-cold, heat-clearing profile.

entities_mentioned.name: 乳癰 (rǔ yōng) — Breast Abscess / Mastitis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acute mastitis; breast abscess (Staphylococcal or other bacterial etiology)
entities_mentioned.notes: The classical indication of 乳癰 (rǔ yōng) maps closely to modern acute mastitis, particularly lactation mastitis. Pu Gong Ying's anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties (demonstrated in modern pharmacology via taraxacin, chlorogenic acid, luteolin) support this classical use.

entities_mentioned.name: 五味消毒飲 (Wǔ Wèi Xiāo Dú Yǐn)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical TCM/Kampo formula — Five-Ingredient Decoction to Eliminate Toxin
entities_mentioned.notes: Sourced from the Qing-dynasty Yī Zōng Jīn Jiàn (醫宗金鑑, Imperial Golden Mirror of Medicine, 1742). Still prescribed in TCM and Kampo for skin infections, furuncles, and early-stage breast inflammation.

entities_mentioned.name: 本草綱目 (Běncǎo Gāngmù)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Compendium of Materia Medica, Li Shizhen (李時珍), 1596 CE
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary post-Shennong source for Pu Gong Ying's expanded indications. Li Shizhen records its use for breast disorders, snake bites, and as a diuretic — all consistent with modern pharmacological findings.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source segment provided is a brief keyword prompt rather than a verbatim classical Chinese passage; accordingly, this entry has been generated as a comprehensive scholarly reconstruction drawing on the Shennong Bencaojing tradition, the Bencao Gangmu, the Tang-dynasty Xin Xiu Bencao, and Kampo (Japanese) elaborations, per the segment instructions. The original_text field presents the canonical Chinese pharmacopoeial summary as it would appear in a modern critical edition. 2. Pu Gong Ying's absence from the reconstructed Shennong Bencaojing (the Mori Tateyuki and Tao Hongjing recensions) is flagged explicitly in the translation; attributing it to the Shennong tradition reflects common scholastic convention but should be understood as representing the broader shen-nong lineage rather than a specific textual citation. 3. The Japanese Kampo name Hokōei (蒲公英) uses the same characters as the Chinese, consistent with on'yomi reading conventions in Kampo pharmacopoeial texts (e.g., the Japanese Pharmacopoeia, JP XVIII). 4. Modern pharmacological correlates (taraxacin, luteolin, chlorogenic acid) are noted parenthetically to assist contemporary researchers but are not attributed to classical sources.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Pu Gong Ying 蒲公英

results.title_inferred: Yu Xing Cao (Houttuynia cordata): Fish-Smell Herb — Heat-Clearing, Lung Abscess Treatment
results.original_text: 魚腥草 — 蕺菜（じゅうさい / Jú cài）。味辛，微寒。入肺經。清熱解毒，消癰排膿，利尿通淋。主治肺癰吐膿，痰熱喘咳，熱毒瘡瘍，濕熱淋證。
results.translation:
Yu Xing Cao [Fish-Smell Herb; lit. 'fishy-odor herb'], also recorded under the alternate classical name Jú cài [蕺菜, 'crowding vegetable']. 

Flavor [Wei 味]: Acrid/Pungent [Xin 辛]. 
Nature [Qi 氣]: Slightly Cold [Wei Han 微寒]. 
Channel Tropism [Gui Jing 歸經]: Enters the Lung channel [Fei Jing 肺經]; later elaborations also attribute affinity for the Large Intestine and Bladder channels. 

Therapeutic Actions [Zhu Zhi 主治]: 
1. Clears Heat and resolves toxin [Qing Re Jie Du 清熱解毒] — indicated for heat-toxin accumulations, sores, and carbuncles [Re Du Chuang Yang 熱毒瘡瘍]. 
2. Disperses welling-abscesses [Yong 癰] and expels pus [Pai Nong 排膿] — the principal classical indication is Lung abscess [Fei Yong 肺癰], marked by coughing of foul-smelling purulent sputum [Tu Nong 吐膿]. 
3. Clears phlegm-heat and arrests wheezing-cough [Tan Re Chuan Ke 痰熱喘咳]. 
4. Disinhibits urination and frees strangury [Li Niao Tong Lin 利尿通淋] — used for damp-heat strangury patterns [Shi Re Lin Zheng 濕熱淋證] causing painful, inhibited urination. 

Classical Category: Not formally ranked in the original tripartite Shennong Bencaojing classification (Superior / Middle / Inferior [Shang / Zhong / Xia Pin 上中下品]), as the herb entered prominence in post-Han materia medica (notably the Mingyi Bielu [名醫別錄, c. 3rd–5th cent. CE] and later Ben Cao Gang Mu [本草綱目, 1596]). It is conventionally grouped among heat-clearing, toxin-resolving drugs [Qing Re Jie Du Yao 清熱解毒藥]. 

Japanese Kampo Name: Dokudami [ドクダミ / 蕺草]; the dried aerial parts (Houttuynia Herba) are used. In Kampo practice, Dokudami is chiefly employed as a single-herb decoction or as a component in proprietary formulas for respiratory infections, bronchitis, purulent sinusitis, urinary tract infections, and dermatological heat-toxin conditions. 

Preparation: 
— Decoction [Tang 湯]: Fresh or dried herb, 15–30 g (fresh herb up to 60 g); added near the end of decoction or brewed briefly, as prolonged heat diminishes the volatile acrid constituents [the characteristic odor derives from decyl acetaldehyde / houttuynin]. 
— External application [Wai Yong 外用]: Fresh herb crushed and applied as a poultice to sores, swellings, and snake bites. 
— Dokudami tea [ドクダミ茶]: A widespread Japanese folk preparation using dried leaves, taken for detoxification and urinary support. 

Key Classical Formulas: 
1. Yu Xing Cao Tang [魚腥草湯] — simple decoction for Lung abscess and purulent phlegm-cough; often combined with Jie Geng [桔梗, Platycodon root], Lu Gen [蘆根, Phragmites rhizome], Yi Yi Ren [薏苡仁, Job's tears], and Dong Gua Ren [冬瓜仁, winter melon seed]. 
2. Combined with Huang Qin [黃芩, Scutellaria root] and Jin Yin Hua [金銀花, Lonicera flower] in heat-toxin respiratory formulas. 
3. In Kampo, sometimes paired with Ougi [黃耆, Astragalus] in post-suppurative recovery formulas to support Lung qi while clearing residual toxin.

entities_mentioned.name: Yu Xing Cao 魚腥草
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Houttuynia cordata Thunb. (family Saururaceae); aerial parts used medicinally
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most commercially significant medicinal herbs in contemporary East Asian medicine. The strongly fishy odor is due to the volatile compound decyl acetaldehyde (houttuynin), which has demonstrated antibacterial activity in modern pharmacological research. Native to moist shaded environments across southern and central China, Japan, and Southeast Asia.

entities_mentioned.name: Jú cài 蕺菜
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Houttuynia cordata Thunb. (same plant, alternate classical name)
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 蕺菜 (Jú cài) appears in the Mingyi Bielu and other early post-Han sources. The character 蕺 (jú) is rare outside this botanical context. Also edible; used as a vegetable (particularly in Yunnan and Guizhou cuisines, and in Vietnamese cuisine as rau diếp cá).

entities_mentioned.name: Dokudami ドクダミ / 蕺草
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Houttuynia cordata Thunb.; Japanese Kampo designation
entities_mentioned.notes: The Japanese name Dokudami (毒痛み or 毒矯め, folk etymologies vary) reflects the plant's traditional use against toxins and pain. Dokudami is one of the most widely consumed herbal teas in Japan for general wellness, particularly for skin and urinary health.

entities_mentioned.name: Jie Geng 桔梗
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Platycodon grandiflorus (Jacq.) A.DC. (family Campanulaceae); root used
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical 'guide drug' [Yin Jing Yao 引經藥] for the Lung channel, frequently paired with Yu Xing Cao in Lung abscess formulas to open and diffuse Lung qi and facilitate pus expulsion.

entities_mentioned.name: Yi Yi Ren 薏苡仁
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coix lacryma-jobi L. var. ma-yuen (Rom.Caill.) Stapf (family Poaceae); seed used
entities_mentioned.notes: Job's tears seed; used in Lung abscess formulas (e.g., Wei Jing Tang 葦莖湯 tradition) for its ability to drain pus, clear damp-heat, and support recovery from welling-abscess conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Wei Jing Tang 葦莖湯
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical formula from the Jingui Yaolue [金匱要略] tradition for Lung abscess; composed of Lu Gen (Phragmites rhizome), Yi Yi Ren, Dong Gua Ren, and Tao Ren (peach kernel). Yu Xing Cao is commonly substituted for or added to Lu Gen in modern clinical adaptations.

results.translator_notes: The source segment is a modern scholarly prompt rather than a verbatim classical Chinese passage; accordingly, the 'original_text' field has been reconstructed in classical Chinese register consistent with Bencao literature conventions, synthesizing attested terminology from the Mingyi Bielu, Ben Cao Gang Mu (Li Shizhen, 1596), and Kampo sources. Yu Xing Cao does not appear by that name in the received Shennong Bencaojing text (which contains 365 entries); its canonical materia medica documentation begins with the Mingyi Bielu. Any citation specifically attributing it to the Shennong Bencaojing proper should be treated with caution. The Five Flavors classification as 'acrid/slightly cold' is consistent across major sources; some modern Chinese Pharmacopoeia editions also note slight bitterness. The Kampo (Japanese) elaboration is drawn from the Japanese Pharmacopoeia and standard Kampo references (e.g., Tsumura formulary traditions). Volatile constituents are destroyed by prolonged boiling — a preparation note of genuine clinical and pharmacological significance that is implicitly encoded in classical instructions to add the herb late in decoction.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Yu Xing Cao 魚腥草

results.title_inferred: White-Flowered Snake-Tongue Herb (Bai Hua She She Cao): Classical and Kampo Entry
results.original_text: 白花蛇舌草 (Bái Huā Shé Shé Cǎo)
results.translation:
**Chinese Name:** Bái Huā Shé Shé Cǎo (白花蛇舌草) — literally 'White-Flower Snake-Tongue Herb'

**Japanese Kampo Name:** Byakka Jashakuso (白花蛇舌草)

**Modern Botanical Identification:** Hedyotis diffusa Willd. (syn. Oldenlandia diffusa (Roxb.) Wall. ex G.Don), family Rubiaceae. A small, sprawling annual herb bearing tiny white four-petaled flowers, native to southern China, India, and Southeast Asia.

**Classical Category:** Middle Herb [中品, zhōng pǐn] — herbs of moderate potency used therapeutically for specific ailments, taken with caution and not indefinitely.

**Five Flavors [五味, wǔ wèi]:** Bitter (苦, kǔ); slightly Sweet (甘, gān)

**Four Natures [四氣, sì qì]:** Cold (寒, hán)

**Channel Tropism [歸經, guī jīng]:** Stomach (胃, Wèi), Large Intestine (大腸, Dàcháng), Small Intestine (小腸, Xiǎocháng); some Kampo elaborations additionally cite the Lung (肺, Fèi) and Liver (肝, Gān) channels, reflecting its broad detoxifying scope.

**Therapeutic Actions (Classical and Kampo):**
1. Clears Heat and Resolves Toxins [清熱解毒, qīng rè jiě dú]: Principal action; addresses hot toxic conditions including abscesses, carbuncles, snake-bite poisoning, and pyogenic infections.
2. Promotes Diuresis and Resolves Dampness [利尿滲濕, lì niào shèn shī]: Used for painful urinary conditions (lín zhèng, 淋證), edema, and damp-heat accumulation in the lower burner.
3. Clears Heat and Stops Dysentery [清熱止痢]: Addresses intestinal damp-heat manifesting as dysentery with burning, bloody stools.
4. Disperses Swellings and Reduces Abscesses [消癰散結, xiāo yōng sàn jié]: Applied topically and internally for suppurative infections, scrofula, and hard nodular masses.
5. Anti-Tumor Action [抗腫瘤, kàng zhǒngliú] (Post-classical / Modern Kampo elaboration): Extensively documented in 20th-century Chinese clinical literature and Kampo oncology as an adjunct in patterns of toxic heat accumulation with blood stasis, used in cancers of the stomach, liver, esophagus, and rectum.

**Key Classical and Kampo Formulas:**
- **She She Cao Tang (蛇舌草湯):** Simple decoction of Bai Hua She She Cao alone or with Ban Zhi Lian (半枝蓮, Scutellaria barbata) — one of the most widely cited pairings in modern Kampo oncology for heat-toxic tumors.
- **Combined with Bан Zhi Lian (半枝蓮):** This dyad forms the classical kernel of many anti-cancer detoxification formulas in both Chinese and Japanese Kampo practice; together they are said to strongly clear heat, resolve toxins, and inhibit pathological growth.
- **Combined with Yi Yi Ren (薏苡仁), Bai Zhu (白術), and Fu Ling (茯苓):** Used in damp-heat abdominal patterns with possible mass formation.

**Preparation Methods:**
- **Decoction [湯劑, tāng jì]:** 15–60 g dried herb (or up to 120 g fresh herb) simmered in water; the high dose range reflects its relatively mild toxicity profile.
- **Fresh juice [鮮汁]:** Crushed fresh plant juice applied topically to snake bites, carbuncles, and infected wounds.
- **Topical poultice:** Fresh herb pounded and applied directly to abscesses or venomous insect stings.

**Cautions:** Cold in nature; prolonged or high-dose use may injure Spleen-Stomach yang. Use cautiously in patients with Spleen deficiency, cold constitution, or during pregnancy. Not prominently featured in the original Shennong Bencaojing corpus (c. 200 CE) — its formal medicinal use is more thoroughly documented in later materia medica texts and reaches its fullest elaboration in 20th-century Chinese and Kampo oncological practice.

entities_mentioned.name: Bai Hua She She Cao (白花蛇舌草)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hedyotis diffusa Willd. (syn. Oldenlandia diffusa (Roxb.) Wall. ex G.Don); family Rubiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The synonymy between Hedyotis diffusa and Oldenlandia diffusa is well established in botanical literature. The plant is a small creeping annual with tiny white flowers, widely distributed across southern China and tropical Asia. Its anti-inflammatory and potential anti-tumor properties have been extensively studied pharmacologically, with iridoids, flavonoids, and polysaccharides identified as key constituents.

entities_mentioned.name: Ban Zhi Lian (半枝蓮)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scutellaria barbata D.Don; family Lamiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Classically paired with Bai Hua She She Cao in heat-toxic and tumor-pattern formulas. Shares Cold nature and heat-clearing, toxin-resolving actions. This pair is central to modern Kampo oncology protocols.

entities_mentioned.name: Yi Yi Ren (薏苡仁)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coix lacryma-jobi L. var. ma-yuen (Rom.Caill.) Stapf; family Poaceae — Job's Tears seed
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in combination formulas for damp-heat patterns and as an adjunct in abdominal mass presentations.

results.translator_notes: IMPORTANT SOURCE CAVEAT: Bai Hua She She Cao (白花蛇舌草) does not appear as a named, discrete entry in the received text of the Shennong Bencaojing (c. 200 CE). Its documented medicinal use emerges more clearly in later Chinese materia medica traditions and reaches its clinical apex in Republican-era and 20th-century Kampo and Traditional Chinese Medicine practice, particularly in oncological contexts. This entry therefore synthesizes the classical theoretical framework (Five Flavors, Four Natures, channel tropism) as applied retrospectively and in post-classical elaboration, rather than reproducing a verbatim Shennong Bencaojing passage. The source segment provided was a modern indexing label rather than a classical textual excerpt; this has been noted and the entry constructed from established Kampo and TCM canonical sources accordingly. All pharmacological and anti-tumor references reflect post-classical (20th-century) clinical and research literature, not the Shennong Bencaojing tradition proper.
results._label: Shennong Bencaojing / Kampo — Bai Hua She She Cao 白花蛇舌草

results.title_inferred: Ban Zhi Lian (Half-Branch Lotus): Scutellaria barbata in Classical and Kampo Tradition
results.original_text: 半枝蓮 — Scutellaria barbata — 癌、肝
results.translation: Ban Zhi Lian (半枝蓮, 'half-branch lotus' or 'barbed skullcap'): a bitter, acrid, and slightly cold herb [ku, xin; wei han] with action directed primarily upon the Lung [Fei], Liver [Gan], and Kidney [Shen] channels [gui jing]. In the classical Shennong Bencaojing tradition it belongs to no fixed canonical stratum — the herb rose to prominence largely in post-Song compilations and attained its fullest elaboration in the Ming and Qing materia medica, including the Bencao Gangmu Shiyi (本草綱目拾遺) of Zhao Xuemin (c. 1765 CE) and subsequent Qing clinical texts, rather than appearing in the original Shennong canon directly. It is therefore conventionally classed among supplementary or 'acquired' [fulu] entries rather than the Three Grades (superior / middle / inferior) proper. In Kampo (Japanese) tradition it is known as Hangeshiren (ハンシレン / 半枝蓮), and its inclusion in Japanese practice follows the Kampo reception of late-Ming Chinese pharmacopoeia. Classical and subsequent therapeutic actions: (1) Clears Heat and resolves Toxicity [qing re jie du 清熱解毒] — indicated for carbuncles, sores, swellings, and snake-bite [yong zhong chuang du, she chong yao shang]; (2) Activates Blood and disperses Stasis [huo xue hua yu 活血化瘀] — indicated for fixed abdominal masses [zheng jia ji ju], traumatic injury, and menstrual irregularity with Blood stasis; (3) Promotes urination and reduces oedema [li shui xiao zhong 利水消腫] — indicated for lin syndrome [lin zheng], oedema of the face and limbs, and ascites; (4) Modern elaboration (post-1950 Chinese oncology and contemporary Kampo): strongly associated with anti-tumour [kang ai 抗癌] application, particularly for hepatocellular carcinoma [gan ai 肝癌], lung carcinoma, and colorectal carcinoma; used both as a single-herb decoction and in compound formulae. Key classical and modern formulae featuring Ban Zhi Lian: (a) Ban Zhi Lian Dan (半枝蓮丹) — simple toxin-resolving preparation recorded in Qing folk texts; (b) Compound hepato-protective formulae in modern TCM: Ban Zhi Lian combined with Bai Hua She She Cao (白花蛇舌草, Hedyotis diffusa) as the canonical paired anti-tumour dyad — this combination [ban she he ji] forms the backbone of numerous modern oncology decoctions; (c) Kampo formulations (ganzai kanpoyaku) incorporating Hangeshiren alongside Orengedokuto (黄連解毒湯) components for hepatic inflammation and early-stage hepatic neoplasm. Preparation: standard water decoction [shui jian 水煎], 15–30 g dried herb (up to 60 g fresh); topical poultice [xian pin dao lan wai fu 鮮品搗爛外敷] of fresh crushed herb applied to carbuncles and snakebite. Contraindications: blood-deficiency conditions without stasis; pregnancy (activates Blood).

entities_mentioned.name: Ban Zhi Lian 半枝蓮
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scutellaria barbata D. Don (Lamiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely accepted botanical identity across TCM pharmacopoeia, Chinese Pharmacopoeia (2020 edition), and Kampo reference texts. Not to be confused with Scutellaria baicalensis (Huang Qin 黃芩), a distinct species of the same genus with different classical indications.

entities_mentioned.name: Bai Hua She She Cao 白花蛇舌草
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hedyotis diffusa Willd. (Rubiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Canonical clinical partner herb to Ban Zhi Lian in modern anti-tumour TCM practice; the paired combination is well-documented in the oncology literature from the 1970s onward.

entities_mentioned.name: Hangeshiren ハンシレン / 半枝蓮
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scutellaria barbata D. Don
entities_mentioned.notes: Japanese Kampo name; phonetically and graphically derived from the Chinese. Appears in Kampo hepatology formulae and is listed in the Japanese standard Kampo herb reference (Nihon Yakkyokuhō / Japanese Pharmacopoeia supplement).

entities_mentioned.name: Bencao Gangmu Shiyi 本草綱目拾遺
entities_mentioned.notes: Supplementary materia medica compiled by Zhao Xuemin (趙學敏), completed c. 1765 CE, Qing dynasty; the primary classical locus for Ban Zhi Lian's formal pharmacological description.

results.translator_notes: The source segment is extremely terse ('Plant: Ban Zhi Lian 半枝蓮 — Scutellaria barbata — cancer, liver'), providing only a name, botanical identification, and two clinical keywords. The full entry above has been constructed from the Shennong Bencaojing tradition and its Kampo elaboration as requested, drawing on the post-Song materia medica record (principally the Bencao Gangmu Shiyi and Qing clinical texts) because Ban Zhi Lian does not appear in the original c. 200 CE Shennong canon. This absence is itself scholarly significant and has been flagged in the translation.
